topics.title: Basic concepts of plant layout and design including a basic understanding of equipment layout and ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic concepts of plant layout and design including a basic understanding of equipment layout and ventilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Plant Layout" in Food Engineering?

A **Plant Layout** is the physical arrangement of industrial machinery, equipment, workstations, storage facilities, material handling aisles, service utilities (steam, water, refrigeration, electricity), and personnel circulation paths within the four walls of a manufacturing building. In a **food processing plant**, this layout is not merely an architectural exercise — it is a **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) and HACCP-driven** engineering decision that directly governs product safety, hygiene zoning, throughput, worker safety, and regulatory compliance under **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and **Codex Alimentarius** frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Plant layout is the integrated, two-dimensional or three-dimensional pre-planning of men, machines, materials, and supporting services within a production building so as to achieve the optimum sequence of operations at the lowest unit cost while satisfying safety, hygiene, ergonomic, and statutory constraints.*

### 1.2 What is "Plant Design"?

While *layout* answers the question **"Where will things physically sit?"**, *plant design* answers the deeper question **"How will the entire facility be conceived, sized, and engineered — from site selection to commissioning?"**. Plant design is the **macro-level engineering discipline** that precedes layout. It includes capacity estimation, technology selection, building sizing, utility sizing, cost estimation, and project scheduling.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Model

Imagine designing the kitchen of a high-end restaurant before it is built.

| Restaurant Kitchen Analogy | Food Plant Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Choosing the building's neighborhood | **Plant location analysis** |
| Deciding the kitchen's overall size and rooms | **Plant design** |
| Placing the oven, counters, sink, fridge | **Equipment layout** |
| Designing the exhaust chimney above the stove | **Ventilation design** |
| Designing the path a waiter takes to the dining hall | **Material flow & personnel flow** |

In a domestic kitchen, a poor layout means wasted steps and cold food. In a food plant, a poor layout means **microbiological cross-contamination, product recalls, FSSAI license cancellation, and economic loss**. The stakes are scaled up by a factor of thousands.

### 1.4 The Four Universal Layout Types

> [!NOTE]
> Every food plant layout in the world belongs to one of these four families (or a hybrid thereof).

1. **Product Layout (Line Layout)** — Equipment is arranged along the **sequential flow of the product**. Example: a milk processing plant (receiving → clarification → pasteurization → homogenization → packaging).
2. **Process Layout (Functional Layout)** — Equipment of the **same function is grouped together**. Example: a multi-product bakery where all mixers are in one room, all ovens in another.
3. **Fixed-Position Layout** — The product **stays stationary**, and men, machines, and materials come to it. Example: shipbuilding, very large cheese wheels, certain bakery lines.
4. **Cellular / Combined Layout** — A hybrid where a *self-contained manufacturing cell* completes a sub-product. Most modern food plants use this.

### 1.5 Ventilation — A Hygiene Imperative, Not Just Comfort

In a food plant, **ventilation serves three engineering purposes simultaneously**:

- **Removal of contaminants** (heat, moisture, steam, dust, flour, vapors, CO₂).
- **Maintenance of air pressure differentials** between high-risk and low-risk zones (preventing airborne microbial migration).
- **Worker thermal comfort and safety** under the Factories Act, 1948 (India).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Industrial kitchens and food plants typically maintain **15–60 Air Changes per Hour (ACH)** depending on the room's hygiene classification and heat load. The **FSSAI Schedule 4** mandates positive air pressure in high-care zones (≥ 10 Pa).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives (Engineering Goals) of a Good Plant Layout

A well-engineered layout must satisfy **eight classical objectives**, each traceable to a measurable engineering metric:

1. **Minimum material handling cost** — quantified by `tonne-metres travelled`.
2. **Optimum utilization of floor area** — measured by *Plot Coverage Ratio (PCR)* and *Floor Space Index (FSI)*.
3. **Smooth flow of work / minimum backtracking** — measured by *flow efficiency = (forward distance) ÷ (total distance travelled)*.
4. **Maximum worker comfort and safety** — ergonomic reach, OSHA / Factory Act norms.
5. **Flexibility for future expansion** — provision for *n+1* lines.
6. **Effective supervision** — sight-lines from supervisor station.
7. **Adequate lighting and ventilation** — **150–300 lux** in process areas; **≥ 500 lux** in inspection zones.
8. **Minimum capital investment in building and equipment.**

### 2.2 Principles of Plant Layout

The classical **Muther's Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** framework condenses layout principles into a 5-P structure:

| P-Phase | Engineering Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **P**roducts | What is made? | Product-quantity chart (P-Q chart) |
| **P**rocesses | How is it made? | Operation process chart, flow process chart |
| **P**quipment | What machinery? | Equipment list with dimensions |
| **P**ersonnel | Who operates? | Workforce plan, ergonomics |
| **P** (Premises) | What building? | Building footprint, zoning |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **"6M" mnemonic** — *Men, Machines, Materials, Methods, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)* — as the inputs to any layout decision.

### 2.3 Equipment Layout — Engineering Logic

Equipment layout is the **micro-arrangement** within the building envelope. The governing rules are:

- **Spacing for maintenance:** at least **0.6–1.0 m** clearance on all operator sides; **1.2 m** for major overhauls.
- **Hygiene zoning:** **High-risk** (RTE — Ready To Eat) zones must be **physically separated and positively pressurized** vs. **low-risk** (raw material) zones.
- **Linear flow:** product moves in **one direction only**, with **no crossovers** between raw and finished goods.
- **Equipment grouping rule:** *noisy* (compressors, hammer mills) isolated from *vibration-sensitive* (balances, lab instruments).

### 2.4 Ventilation — Engineering Classification

| Type | Driver | Typical ACH | Use Case in Food Plant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Ventilation** | Wind + buoyancy | 2–6 ACH | Warehouses, godowns, dry stores |
| **General Mechanical Ventilation** | Supply/Exhaust fans | 6–15 ACH | Packaging halls, dry processing |
| **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** | Hood + duct + fan | Face velocity **0.5–1.0 m/s** (canopy), **10–15 m/s** (capture) | Frying, frying vapors, dust collection |
| **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning)** | Filtered, T/RH-controlled air | 15–60 ACH, **HEPA H13/H14** in sterile rooms | Aseptic filling, dairy, RTE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Plant Standard Benchmarks:**
> - Ambient temperature in process halls: **≤ 25 °C**
> - Relative humidity: **40–60 %** (for dry products < 60 % to prevent mould)
> - Differential pressure: **+10 Pa to +15 Pa** between zones of increasing hygiene

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$Q = \text{ACH} \times V$$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/h); ACH = air changes per hour (h⁻¹); $V$ = room volume (m³) | Sizing supply/exhaust fans |
| 2 | $$V = L \times W \times H$$ | $L, W, H$ = room length, width, height (m) | Computing room volume for Q |
| 3 | $$q_s = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot Q \cdot \Delta T$$ | $q_s$ = sensible heat (W); $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³; $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K); $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) | HVAC cooling load |
| 4 | $$q_L = \rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot Q \cdot \Delta W$$ | $q_L$ = latent heat (W); $h_{fg}$ = 2501 kJ/kg; $\Delta W$ = humidity ratio difference (kg/kg) | HVAC humidity load |
| 5 | $$v_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{hood}}}$$ | $v_f$ = face velocity (m/s); $A_{\text{hood}}$ = hood opening area (m²) | LEV hood design — KTU favourite |
| 6 | $$D_{\text{duct}} = \left(\dfrac{8 Q^2}{\pi^2 v_d}\right)^{1/4}$$ | $D_{\text{duct}}$ = duct diameter (m); $v_d$ = duct velocity (m/s) | Duct sizing via equal-friction method |
| 7 | $$\text{PCR} = \dfrac{\text{Built-up area}}{\text{Plot area}}$$ | dimensionless | Land-use efficiency |
| 8 | $$\eta_{\text{flow}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{forward}}}{d_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$ | $d$ = distances (m) | Layout flow efficiency check |
| 9 | $$\Delta P_{\text{fan}} = \Delta P_{\text{duct}} + \Delta P_{\text{filters}} + \Delta P_{\text{hood}}$$ | all in Pa | Fan static pressure sizing |

> [!TIP]
> For duct design, **recommended transport velocity** $v_d$:
> - Flour / sugar dust: **18–20 m/s**
> - Steam / vapor: **10–15 m/s**
> - Welding fume: **12–15 m/s**
> - Clean air in HVAC: **6–10 m/s**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Air Change Rate (ACH) → Volumetric Flow Rate

The **ACH formula** is the most fundamental equation a food engineer uses in ventilation design. Below is its step-by-step derivation with clear text annotation.

**Step 1 — Start with the basic definition of volumetric flow rate.**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume of air supplied per cycle}}{\text{Time per cycle}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Air Changes per Hour (ACH).**

By convention, ACH is the number of times the entire room air volume is theoretically displaced in one hour.

$$
\text{ACH} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Volume of air moved in 1 hour}}{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$
\text{Volume moved in 1 hour} \;=\; Q \;=\; \text{ACH} \times V
$$

**Step 4 — Insert room volume in terms of physical dimensions.**

$$
V = L \times W \times H
$$

**Step 5 — Final combined form used in KTU exam problems.**

$$
Q = \text{ACH} \times L \times W \times H
$$

**Textual sanity check:** If a room measures $L = 10\,\text{m}$, $W = 8\,\text{m}$, $H = 4\,\text{m}$, its volume is $V = 320\,\text{m}^3$. At an ACH of 20, the required supply air is $Q = 20 \times 320 = 6{,}400\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$. This single number drives fan selection, duct sizing, and electrical load.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Fan Sizing for a Food Processing Hall

**Problem statement (modelled on a KTU Part B question):**

> A dry-blending food hall has dimensions $L = 12\,\text{m}$, $W = 10\,\text{m}$, $H = 5\,\text{m}$. The process generates sensible heat at a rate of **12 kW**. The plant requires an inside temperature of $T_i = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the outside design temperature is $T_o = 38\,^\circ\text{C}$. Design (a) the supply air volume flow rate assuming **25 ACH**, (b) the actual cooling capacity required, and (c) verify whether the supply-air approach alone can handle the heat load.

**Step 1 — Compute the room volume.**

$$
V = L \times W \times H = 12 \times 10 \times 5 = 600 \,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the volumetric flow from ACH-based formula to SI units.**

$$
Q = \text{ACH} \times V = 25 \times 600 = 15{,}000 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m³/s for the energy balance.**

$$
Q = \dfrac{15{,}000}{3600} = 4.167 \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the sensible heat equation.**

$$
q_s = \rho \, c_p \, Q \, \Delta T
$$

with $\rho = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1005\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$, and $\Delta T = 38 - 25 = 13\,\text{K}$.

$$
q_s = 1.2 \times 1005 \times 4.167 \times 13
$$

**Step 5 — Carry out the multiplication stepwise.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_s &= 1.2 \times 1005 \times 4.167 \times 13 \\
    &= 1206 \times 4.167 \times 13 \\
    &= 5025.6 \times 13 \\
    &= 65{,}332.8 \,\text{W} \\
    &\approx 65.33 \,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**

The supply air can remove **65.33 kW** versus the **12 kW** heat load generated. The system is more than adequate — in fact, oversized by ~5.4×, suggesting ACH can be **lowered to ~5–6** to save fan power, OR the room can host **5× more equipment** before saturation.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU valuation tip: state the units explicitly *at every numerical step*; missing units is a guaranteed **½ to 1 mark** deduction.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting a Layout Type

| Decision Criterion | Product Layout | Process Layout | Fixed Position | Cellular Layout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Production volume | High | Low | One-off | Medium–High |
| Product variety | Low | High | Very low | Medium |
| Equipment utilization | High | Low | Variable | High |
| Worker skill needed | Low | High | High | Medium |
| Capital investment | High | Low | Very high | Medium |
| Suitability for food plants | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★★ |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ventilation Selection

| Process / Room | Recommended Ventilation | Typical ACH | Filter Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw material receiving | Natural + General Mech. | 6–10 | G4 (Coarse) |
| Dry-blending | General Mech. + LEV | 15–20 | M5 (Medium) |
| Wet processing / cooking | LEV + General Mech. | 20–30 | M5 + scrubber |
| Aseptic / RTE filling | HVAC with HEPA | 40–60 | H13 / H14 HEPA |
| Cold storage | Limited mech. + destratification | 3–6 | — |
| Laboratory | Fume hood + HVAC | 15–20 | F7 + carbon |

### 3.5 Layout Efficiency — Flow Index Calculation

For a given production line with stations A → B → C → D → E (forward) and one backtrack D → B, the flow efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{\text{flow}} = \dfrac{d_{A \to E}}{d_{A \to E} + d_{E \to B \to D}} \times 100\%
$$

If the forward span $d_{A \to E} = 40\,\text{m}$ and the backtrack path = $12\,\text{m}$:

$$
\eta_{\text{flow}} = \dfrac{40}{40 + 12} \times 100\% = 76.9\%
$$

> A **layout efficiency ≥ 90 %** is the KTU benchmark for an "excellent" design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Plant Design Workflow

This mermaid block diagram captures the **macro-level engineering sequence** from concept to commissioning. It is the single most important "process map" a KTU student must memorize for the 14-mark design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Market Survey and Demand Forecast] --> A2[Product and Quantity Selection P-Q Chart]
    A2 --> A3[Process Selection and Route Sheet]
    A3 --> A4[Equipment List with Capacity and Footprint]
    A4 --> A5[Plant Location Analysis]
    A5 --> A6[Plant Layout Product Process or Cellular]
    A6 --> A7[Building Design and Civil Works]
    A7 --> A8[Utility Sizing HVAC Steam Water Compressed Air]
    A8 --> A9[Equipment Installation and Piping]
    A9 --> A10[Commissioning Validation and HACCP Audit]
    A10 --> A11[Production and Continuous Improvement]

    classDef step fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000;
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9,A10,A11 step;
```

> Reading guide: each block is a **design phase** whose output becomes the input of the next. Skipping a phase (e.g., going straight from equipment list to installation without layout planning) is the single most common cause of plant redesign costs.

### 4.2 Equipment Layout — Hygienic Zoning Diagram

The diagram below shows the **canonical zoning logic** used in any food plant. The arrows represent **air pressure differentials** (positive pressure pushes outward, protecting the inner zone).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Zone 1 Low Risk Raw Material Store and Washing] -->|unidirectional flow| Z2[Zone 2 Medium Risk Processing and Cooking]
    Z2 -->|unidirectional flow| Z3[Zone 3 High Care RTE and Aseptic Filling]
    Z3 -->|unidirectional flow| Z4[Zone 4 Critical Aseptic Sterile Suite]

    Z4 -. +15 Pa positive pressure .-> Z3
    Z3 -. +10 Pa positive pressure .-> Z2
    Z2 -. neutral .-> Z1
    Z1 -. slightly negative exhaust .-> EXT[External Atmosphere]

    classDef low fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#990000;
    classDef med fill:#FFF5CC,stroke:#996600;
    classDef high fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600;
    classDef crit fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003366;

    class Z1 low;
    class Z2 med;
    class Z3 high;
    class Z4 crit;
    class EXT low;
```

### 4.3 Ventilation System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This is the **engineering schematic** of a typical Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system, the workhorse of food-plant ventilation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Contaminant Source e.g. Frying Vat] --> HOOD[Capture Hood Canopy or Slot]
    HOOD --> DUCT[Galvanized Duct with Transport Velocity v d]
    DUCT --> SEP[Air Cleaning Device Cyclone or Bag Filter]
    SEP --> FAN[Exhaust Fan with Damper]
    FAN --> STACK[Discharge Stack Above Roof Line]
    STACK --> ATM[Atmosphere with Re-entry Clearance]

    MAKEUP[Filtered Make-up Air Unit] -. makeup air replaces exhausted air .-> ROOM[Process Room Maintains Negative Pressure]

    classDef equip fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366;
    class SRC,HOOD,DUCT,SEP,FAN,STACK,ATM,MAKEUP,ROOM equip;
```

> Critical reading: the **make-up air unit** is not optional. Without it, the exhaust fan creates a strong negative pressure that *back-feeds* contaminants from adjacent (less-clean) zones into the process room — defeating the entire purpose of LEV.

### 4.4 Layout Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Annual Production Volume} -->|High| Q2{Product Variety}
    Q1 -->|Low| Q3[Fixed Position or Project Layout]

    Q2 -->|Low - single product| Q4[Product Layout Line]
    Q2 -->|High - many SKUs| Q5[Process Layout Functional]
    Q2 -->|Medium - product family| Q6[Cellular or Group Layout]

    classDef opt fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#CC6600;
    class Q4,Q5,Q6,Q3 opt;
```

### 4.5 Personnel and Material Flow Logic (No-Cross Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Personnel
        P1[Staff Entry - Locker Room] --> P2[Hand Wash and Sanitize] --> P3[PPE Gowning] --> P4[Process Floor]
    end

    subgraph Material
        M1[Raw Material Dock] --> M2[Quarantine Store] --> M3[Approved Store] --> M4[Process Floor] --> M5[Finished Goods Store] --> M6[Dispatch Dock]
    end

    P4 -. never crosses .-> M1
    M6 -. exits away from .-> P1
```

> The **"no-cross" rule** (personnel and material paths must not intersect) is the most frequently violated principle in real food plants and the most frequently cited cause of microbial cross-contamination in **FSSAI audit reports**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define plant layout. List any four objectives of a good plant layout.*

**Model Answer:**

> Plant layout is the pre-planned physical arrangement of machines, equipment, workstations, material handling systems, and service utilities within a manufacturing building so as to optimize the sequence of operations.
>
> Four objectives:
> 1. Minimum material handling cost.
> 2. Optimum utilization of floor space.
> 3. Smooth and unidirectional flow of materials with minimum backtracking.
> 4. Maximum worker comfort, safety, and ease of supervision.

*(Expected length: 8–12 lines; 3 marks split as 1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the four objectives.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "Air Changes per Hour (ACH)"? State the typical ACH values recommended for (a) a general food packaging hall and (b) an aseptic filling room.*

**Model Answer:**

> ACH is the number of times the entire air volume of a room is theoretically replaced by fresh (or conditioned) air in one hour. Mathematically, $\text{ACH} = Q / V$ where $Q$ is the supply air flow in m³/h and $V$ is the room volume in m³.
>
> - **(a) General packaging hall:** ~**15–20 ACH** (general mechanical ventilation, M5 grade filtration).
> - **(b) Aseptic / sterile filling room:** ~**40–60 ACH** (HVAC with HEPA H13/H14 filtration, positive pressure ≥ 15 Pa).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — ANSWER EITHER QUESTION A OR QUESTION B]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **step-by-step procedure for designing a food processing plant** from concept to commissioning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **product layout and process layout** for a multi-product food industry in terms of any seven engineering criteria. State which is most suitable for a **high-volume dairy plant**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The sequential phases of plant design are:

1. **Market Survey and Demand Forecast** — Identify the target product mix, market size, and projected annual turnover (5-year horizon).
2. **Product-Quantity (P-Q) Analysis** — Prepare a P-Q chart to decide whether to make-to-stock or make-to-order.
3. **Process Selection** — Choose the manufacturing technology (e.g., UHT vs. pasteurization for milk).
4. **Equipment List & Capacity Sizing** — Match equipment capacity to peak-hour demand with a 15–20 % safety margin.
5. **Plant Location Analysis** — Apply the Weber / factor-rating method considering raw material, market, transport, power, water, labour, climate.
6. **Plant Layout** — Adopt product, process, or cellular layout based on the P-Q chart and process route sheet.
7. **Building & Civil Design** — Plan column grid, floor loading, drainage gradients (≥ 1:60 toward floor drains).
8. **Utility Sizing** — Compute HVAC, steam, refrigeration, compressed air, water, and effluent treatment loads.
9. **Installation & Piping** — Lay out process piping using 3D modeling; respect GMP slope (≥ 1:100) for drain lines.
10. **Commissioning, Validation & HACCP Audit** — IQ, OQ, PQ protocols; CCP verification before commercial production.

*(7 marks — award ½ mark per correctly explained phase + 1 mark for logical sequencing.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Criterion | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | High | Low |
| Variety | Low | High |
| Equipment utilization | Excellent (90 %+) | Poor (40–60 %) |
| WIP inventory | Low | High |
| Worker skill needed | Low | High |
| Flexibility | Poor | Excellent |
| Capital investment | High | Low |

**Conclusion:** For a **high-volume dairy plant** (single SKU family — fluid milk), the **Product Layout** is most suitable because it offers minimum backtracking, low WIP, and high throughput.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per criterion comparison + 1 mark for justified selection.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write "process layout is best for dairy" because they confuse "process" with "manufacturing process." Always tie your answer to **volume vs variety**, not to the product category.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** Define **ventilation**. Explain the difference between **natural and mechanical ventilation** with two examples of each in a food plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dry-blending room has dimensions $L = 8\,\text{m}$, $W = 6\,\text{m}$, $H = 4\,\text{m}$. The room temperature must be maintained at **28 °C** while the ambient is **40 °C**. The dust load from the blender is **1.2 kW** of sensible heat. Assuming an air density $\rho = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and $c_p = 1005\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$, calculate **(i)** the volumetric flow rate at **18 ACH** and **(ii)** the temperature drop achievable using the supply air alone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Ventilation** is the deliberate supply and/or removal of air from a space to control temperature, humidity, contaminants, and air pressure differentials.

| Aspect | Natural Ventilation | Mechanical Ventilation |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver** | Wind pressure + thermal buoyancy | Electric fans / blowers |
| **Examples in food plant** | (1) Open windows in dry godowns; (2) roof ventilators in warehouses | (1) Exhaust fans over frying line; (2) HEPA-filtered AHU in aseptic suite |
| **Control** | Poor / weather-dependent | Precise, T/RH/dust controlled |
| **ACH achieved** | 2–6 | 15–60 |

*(7 marks — 2 marks definition + 2 marks natural + 2 marks mechanical + 1 mark comparative example.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Volumetric flow rate**

$$
V = L \times W \times H = 8 \times 6 \times 4 = 192\,\text{m}^3
$$

$$
Q = \text{ACH} \times V = 18 \times 192 = 3{,}456\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 0.96\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Temperature drop $\Delta T$ across the room (heat balance)**

$$
q = \rho \, c_p \, Q \, \Delta T
$$

Solve for $\Delta T$:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{q}{\rho \, c_p \, Q}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1{,}200}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 0.96}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1{,}200}{1{,}157.76} = 1.036\,\text{K}
$$

**Interpretation:** The supply air alone can absorb the 1.2 kW heat load with only a **1 °C** temperature rise inside the room, confirming that **18 ACH is more than sufficient** for this dry-blending operation.

*(7 marks — 1 mark volume + 1 mark Q + 1 mark Q conversion + 2 marks heat-balance equation + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark interpretation.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert Q from m³/h to m³/s** when using the SI version of the sensible-heat equation. This single error loses **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Plant layout** = where equipment sits; **plant design** = the bigger engineering from concept to commissioning.
- ✅ The **four layout families**: Product, Process, Fixed-Position, Cellular. Food plants usually combine product + process (cell layout).
- ✅ The **6 M's** of layout: Men, Machines, Materials, Methods, Measurement, Mother Nature.
- ✅ The **two master equations** for ventilation:
$$Q = \text{ACH} \times V \quad \text{and} \quad q_s = \rho \, c_p \, Q \, \Delta T$$
- ✅ **Hygiene zoning** is unidirectional: low-risk → high-risk, with **positive air pressure** pushing outward from clean zones.
- ✅ Typical ACH benchmarks:
  - General: 6–10 | Dry-blending: 15–20 | Cooking/wet: 20–30 | Aseptic RTE: **40–60** with HEPA.
- ✅ **Face velocity** of a canopy hood: **0.5–1.0 m/s**; capture slot: **10–15 m/s**.
- ✅ **Recommended duct velocities**: dust 18–20 m/s; vapor 10–15 m/s; clean HVAC 6–10 m/s.
- ✅ **No-cross principle**: personnel and material paths must never intersect.
- ✅ **Pressure differential** between zones: **+10 Pa to +15 Pa** in favour of the cleaner zone.
- ✅ **Flow efficiency** benchmark: **≥ 90 %** for an excellent layout.
- ✅ Standard lighting: **150–300 lux** process; **≥ 500 lux** inspection.
- ✅ Always carry **units** in every line of a numerical solution — silent units = silent marks.
- ✅ Floor slope for drainage: **minimum 1:100** toward trapped floor drains.
- ✅ Air changes per hour is a *theoretical* concept — real ventilation also involves **fresh-air fraction**, **recirculation ratio**, and **filter loading drop**.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Plant layout = GMP + flow + ergonomics + economics. Plant design = layout + utilities + validation. Ventilation = cleanliness = safety = law.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design considerations for the location of food plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design considerations for the location of food plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plant location** is the strategic, long-term, and irreversible decision of selecting an optimal geographic site for establishing a food processing/manufacturing facility, where the chosen site maximizes operational efficiency, minimizes total logistics cost, ensures uninterrupted raw material supply, and complies with statutory food safety and environmental regulations throughout the plant's economic life.

In the context of KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework (Course Outcome **CO1**: *Plan and design the layout of a food processing plant*), the **design considerations for plant location** represent a multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) problem that integrates engineering, economic, regulatory, and socio-environmental parameters to deliver a location that supports **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)**, **Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP)**, and **ISO 22000 Food Safety Management System** requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to *identify, classify, and justify* the factors governing food plant location, and apply *quantitative methods* (Rating Factor Method, Factor Rating, Break-Even Analysis, Centre of Gravity Method) to arrive at the optimal site among competing alternatives.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Plant Location Strategy

| Strategic Orientation | Definition | Typical Industries | Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw-Material Oriented** | Plant is sited **closest to the source** of perishable/raw agricultural produce | Fruit/vegetable processing, dairy chilling, sugar mills, rice/flour mills | High perishability risk; benefits from short haulage |
| **Market Oriented** | Plant is sited **closest to the consumer/market** to reduce distribution cost and lead time | Bread, beverages, ice cream, frozen foods, ready-to-eat | High distribution cost; benefits from freshness at point-of-sale |

> [!NOTE]
> A **food plant is generally RAW-MATERIAL ORIENTED** because most agricultural raw materials (fruits, vegetables, milk, fish, meat) are **highly perishable**, bulky, and lose weight/volume during processing. The finished product is typically smaller, lighter, and shelf-stable.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding Catering Kitchen" Analogy

Imagine you are opening a large catering kitchen that supplies biryani to wedding halls.

- If you place the kitchen **far from the rice and meat vendors**, vegetables wilt, milk sours, and fuel cost of refrigerated trucks skyrockets — just like a **raw-material-oriented** food plant.
- If you place the kitchen **far from the wedding venues**, the biryani arrives cold and late, leading to customer complaints — just like a **market-oriented** food plant.
- If you build it in a **flood-prone area near a polluted river**, every batch risks contamination — exactly the **environmental, water, and effluent considerations** of food plant siting.

The **optimal kitchen** sits in a clean, well-drained, road-connected industrial pocket — *equidistant* from reliable vendors and main customers, with abundant potable water, cheap electricity, and willing workers. **That intersection of ALL factors is "plant location."**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "SHE-LAW-RIP-MET-CGA"** (8 Master Factors):
> **S**upply of raw material · **H**uman resources · **E**nvironment · **L**and · **A**ccessibility · **W**ater & Utilities · **R**egulation · **I**nfrastructure · **P**roximity to market · **M**arketing · **E**ffluent · **T**axation · **C**limate · **G**overnment policy · **A**vailability of supporting industries

## 1.4 Classification of Location Factors

Location factors are broadly classified into two groups:

1. **Primary / General Factors** — common to *all* manufacturing industries (cost, labor, transport).
2. **Secondary / Specific Factors** — *peculiar* to food processing industries (perishability, sanitation, water quality, effluent disposal, cold chain).

> [!NOTE]
> Food plant location decisions are **dominantly governed by Secondary Factors** — this is the single most important KTU distinguishing feature compared to a mechanical or chemical plant location question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary (General) Location Factors

These apply to any manufacturing plant but must be evaluated for food plants as well.

1. **Proximity to Raw Material Source**
   - Critical when **Raw Material Index (RMI) > 1**.
   - RMI is the ratio of weight/volume of raw material to finished product.
   - *Example:* Sugar refining — 10 tonnes of sugarcane produce 1 tonne of sugar; hence, the plant is **co-located with the sugarcane belt**.

2. **Proximity to Market**
   - Critical when **RMI < 1** (i.e., finished product is bulkier/heavier than raw material).
   - *Example:* Bottled water, bread, ice cream — these are produced near consumption zones.

3. **Transportation Facilities**
   - Availability of road, rail, air, and sea connectivity.
   - Perishable foods require **refrigerated trucks (reefer vans)** — absence raises logistics cost.
   - Inbound cost of raw material + outbound cost of finished product should be **minimised**.

4. **Availability of Labor**
   - Skilled, semi-skilled, and unskilled labor at *reasonable wage rates*.
   - For food plants, **seasonal labor** (during harvest) is also a key determinant.

5. **Availability of Utilities**
   - Continuous supply of **water (potable)**, **electricity (3-phase)**, **steam**, **fuel (LPG/CNG)**, and **compressed air**.
   - Water requirement in food plants is typically **2–8 m³ per tonne of product**.

6. **Land Cost and Topography**
   - Flat, well-drained land with high soil-bearing capacity.
   - Avoid flood plains, marshy zones, and seismic zones.
   - Future expansion possibility must be considered.

7. **Government Policies and Fiscal Incentives**
   - Subsidies, tax holidays, SEZ benefits, single-window clearance.
   - State-level food processing missions (e.g., PMKSY, MIDH).

8. **Capital and Operating Cost**
   - Construction cost, financing cost, local taxes, and recurring OPEX.

## 2.2 Secondary (Food-Specific) Location Factors

These distinguish food plants from other process industries and are heavily tested in KTU exams.

1. **Perishability of Raw Material**
   - The **single most decisive factor**.
   - Time-temperature tolerance of raw material dictates the maximum permissible transit time.
   - *Examples:* Milk — 4 hours; fish — 6–8 hours on ice; leafy vegetables — 12–24 hours.

2. **Water Quality and Quantity**
   - Food plants are **water-intensive**.
   - Required water: **potable**, low hardness, free from pathogens and chemical contaminants.
   - IS 10500:2012 standards must be met.

3. **Climatic Conditions**
   - Cool, dry climate is preferred (low microbial load, low refrigeration cost).
   - Humid coastal areas are **discouraged** for plants producing hygroscopic products (e.g., milk powder, instant coffee).
   - Salt-laden air near coasts causes **corrosion of equipment**.

4. **Sanitation and Hygiene Buffer Zone**
   - Plant should be away from **pollution sources** — chemical factories, sewage treatment plants, tanneries, garbage dumps, animal farms.
   - Minimum **buffer zone** as per FSSAI/FDA: typically **300–500 m** from any contaminant source.

5. **Effluent / Waste Disposal**
   - Liquid effluent (high BOD/COD) must be disposed as per **CPCB / KSPCB norms**.
   - Solid waste (peels, pulp, bones) must have buyers (rendering plants, composting units, biogas plants) within economical haulage distance.
   - Proximity to a **Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP)** is a strong plus.

6. **Cold Chain Infrastructure**
   - Availability of **cold storage, ripening chambers, reefer vehicles, and quick-freezing facilities** in the vicinity.

7. **Availability of Packaging Material**
   - Glass bottles, tin cans, multilayer pouches, corrugated boxes — sourced locally to reduce cost.

8. **Community and Social Factors**
   - Housing, schools, hospitals for workers; community acceptance of the plant.
   - Religious/cultural sensitivity — *e.g., a meat plant must not be located in a predominantly vegetarian area*.

9. **Pest and Vermin Control Environment**
   - Site must have low rodent, insect, and bird activity.
   - Surrounding vegetation should be **managed grassland**, not dense forest.

10. **Fire Safety and Security**
    - Adequate fire-fighting water reservoir, hydrants, and access roads for fire tenders.
    - Security fencing and isolated hazardous zones (boilers, ammonia refrigeration).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Method / Concept | Governing Equation | Key Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Raw Material Index (RMI)** | $$RMI = \frac{W_{RM}}{W_{FP}}$$ | $W_{RM}$ = weight of raw material input (kg/day); $W_{FP}$ = weight of finished product output (kg/day) | If $RMI > 1$ → **raw-material oriented**; if $RMI < 1$ → **market oriented**; if $RMI = 1$ → either/or |
| 2 | **Rating Factor Method (Weighted Score Model)** | $$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$$ | $S_j$ = total weighted score of site *j*; $w_i$ = weight of factor *i* (sum = 1); $r_{ij}$ = rating of factor *i* at site *j* (0–10 scale) | Best site: $\max(S_j)$; weights determined by **paired comparison or Delphi** |
| 3 | **Weight Determination (Rank-Sum Method)** | $$w_i = \frac{(n - r_i + 1)}{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(n - r_k + 1)}$$ | $r_i$ = rank of factor *i* (1 = most important); $n$ = number of factors | $\sum w_i = 1$; integer ranks only |
| 4 | **Break-Even Quantity (Plant Location)** | $$Q^* = \frac{F_A - F_B}{V_B - V_A}$$ | $F_A, F_B$ = fixed cost at sites A and B (₹/yr); $V_A, V_B$ = variable cost per unit at A and B (₹/unit) | If actual $Q > Q^*$ → choose **lower fixed-cost** site; if $Q < Q^*$ → choose **lower variable-cost** site |
| 5 | **Total Cost of Operation (TCO)** | $$TCO = F + V \cdot Q + T \cdot Q + L \cdot Q$$ | $F$ = annual fixed cost; $V$ = variable production cost/unit; $T$ = transport cost/unit; $L$ = labor cost/unit; $Q$ = annual output (units) | Minimise TCO; used in cost-based site ranking |
| 6 | **Centre of Gravity (CoG) Method** | $$x^* = \frac{\sum_{i} x_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_i V_i}, \quad y^* = \frac{\sum_{i} y_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_i V_i}$$ | $x_i, y_i$ = coordinates of source/market *i* (km); $V_i$ = volume/load from *i* (tonnes/month) | Single-facility distribution centres; rectangular coordinate plane assumed |
| 7 | **Distance using CoG coordinates** | $$d_i = \sqrt{(x^* - x_i)^2 + (y^* - y_i)^2}$$ | All distances in **km** | Euclidean (straight-line) distance; multiply by **1.25–1.40** for road distance |
| 8 | **Transportation Cost per Unit** | $$C_T = C_d \cdot d$$ | $C_d$ = cost per km per unit (₹/tonne·km); $d$ = distance in km | Linear with distance (true only for own fleet; actual is piecewise) |
| 9 | **Water Requirement Index (Food Plant)** | $$W_{req} = k \cdot P$$ | $P$ = plant capacity (tonnes/day); $k$ = specific water demand (m³/tonne), typically $k = 2$–$8$ | Industry-specific; dairy ≈ 4; brewery ≈ 6; meat ≈ 8 |
| 10 | **Land Area (Heuristic)** | $$A = a \cdot Q$$ | $Q$ = capacity (tonnes/day); $a$ = specific land (m²/tonne), typical $a = 5$–$15$ | Includes **buffer, expansion, parking, landscaping** |

> [!WARNING]
> In all the above formulas, **never write** $Q \vert V$ for absolute value inside markdown tables — always use $\lvert Q/V \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(Q/V)$ to preserve table formatting.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Industry Use:** Multinational FMCG majors (Nestlé, ITC, Britannia, Amul) employ a **hybrid** of the Rating Factor Method (qualitative) and Break-Even Analysis (quantitative) before sanctioning a Greenfield plant.
- **Consulting Practice:** Site selection consultants use a **GIS-based weighted overlay** model — a digital twin of the Rating Factor Method.
- **Government Use:** Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI) uses similar multi-criteria models to identify **food parks** under the **Pradhan Mantri Kisan SAMPADA Yojana (PMKSY)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Raw Material Index (RMI) Decision

**Problem (KTU Pattern):**
A fruit processing plant requires **10,000 kg/day of mangoes** to produce **2,500 kg/day of mango pulp**. Should the plant be located **near the orchard (A)** or **near the city market (B)**? Compute the RMI and justify.

### Step 1 — Identify the Weights
- $W_{RM} = 10{,}000$ kg/day
- $W_{FP} = 2{,}500$ kg/day

### Step 2 — Apply the RMI Formula

$$RMI = \frac{W_{RM}}{W_{FP}} = \frac{10{,}000}{2{,}500}$$

$$RMI = 4$$

### Step 3 — Interpret
Since $RMI = 4 > 1$, the **raw material is bulkier and heavier** than the finished pulp. **Mangoes are highly perishable** (shelf life 4–6 days at ambient, 14 days cold). Therefore, **transporting mangoes to a distant market-located plant is uneconomical and unsafe**.

### Step 4 — Conclude
> **The plant must be located NEAR THE ORCHARD (Site A).**

### Step 5 — Sanity Check
- Pulp is shelf-stable (6–12 months in aseptic bags).
- After pulping, weight reduces by **75%** → finished product is **cheaper to transport**.
- Locating near the orchard also enables a **collection-cum-grading centre** for just-in-time intake.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rating Factor Method (Full Solution)

**Problem:**
A food company is evaluating **three sites** — **X, Y, Z** — for a dairy plant. The decision factors, their weights, and the rating of each factor (on a 0–10 scale) at each site are given. Determine the **best site**.

| Factor (i) | Weight $w_i$ | Rating $r_{i,X}$ | Rating $r_{i,Y}$ | Rating $r_{i,Z}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Raw milk supply | 0.25 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| 2. Market proximity | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| 3. Water availability | 0.15 | 8 | 7 | 8 |
| 4. Power supply | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| 5. Labor cost | 0.10 | 6 | 8 | 9 |
| 6. Transport links | 0.10 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| 7. Effluent disposal | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 7 |

### Step 1 — Validate the Weight Vector
$$\sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Compute Weighted Score for Site X

$$S_X = (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$S_X = 2.25 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.60 + 0.90 + 0.70$$

$$\boxed{S_X = 7.55}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Weighted Score for Site Y

$$S_Y = (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(8)$$

$$S_Y = 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.80 + 0.80 + 0.70 + 0.80$$

$$\boxed{S_Y = 7.70}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Weighted Score for Site Z

$$S_Z = (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$S_Z = 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.90 + 0.60 + 0.70$$

$$\boxed{S_Z = 7.20}$$

### Step 5 — Rank the Sites
- Site **Y** → 7.70 (Rank 1) ✅ **Best**
- Site **X** → 7.55 (Rank 2)
- Site **Z** → 7.20 (Rank 3)

> **Conclusion:** Site **Y** is selected. Although Site X has the best raw milk supply, Site Y wins on **market proximity** (high weight 0.20) and **labor cost**, which is consistent with a *dairy plant* that is mildly market-oriented (milk is daily-delivered to consumers).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Break-Even Quantity for Plant Location

**Problem:**
Two candidate sites **A** and **B** for a ready-to-eat food plant have the following data:

| Parameter | Site A | Site B |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Fixed Cost $F$ | ₹ 80,00,000 | ₹ 1,20,00,000 |
| Variable Cost/unit $V$ | ₹ 120 | ₹ 80 |
| Annual Output (expected) | 50,000 units | 50,000 units |

Determine the break-even quantity and the preferred site at the expected output.

### Step 1 — Write the Total Cost Equations
$$TC_A = F_A + V_A \cdot Q = 80{,}00{,}000 + 120 \cdot Q$$

$$TC_B = F_B + V_B \cdot Q = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 80 \cdot Q$$

### Step 2 — Equate and Solve for $Q^*$
At break-even, $TC_A = TC_B$:

$$80{,}00{,}000 + 120 Q^* = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 80 Q^*$$

$$120 Q^* - 80 Q^* = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000$$

$$40 Q^* = 40{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ units}}$$

### Step 3 — Compare with Expected Output
- Expected $Q = 50{,}000$ units $< Q^* = 1{,}00{,}000$ units.
- Since actual output is **below the break-even point**, the site with **lower variable cost (B)** may still be more expensive *per unit* at low volume.

### Step 4 — Verify by Direct Cost Calculation
- $TC_A = 80{,}00{,}000 + (120)(50{,}000) = 80L + 60L = $ **₹ 1,40,00,000**
- $TC_B = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + (80)(50{,}000) = 120L + 40L = $ **₹ 1,60,00,000**

### Step 5 — Decision
> **Choose Site A** because $TC_A < TC_B$ at $Q = 50{,}000$ units. If the firm scales to $Q > 1{,}00{,}000$ units, switch to **Site B** for variable-cost savings.

> [!NOTE]
> The break-even method is **deterministic and ignores qualitative factors**. In KTU exams, it is *usually combined* with the Rating Factor Method in a two-stage analysis.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Centre of Gravity Method

**Problem:**
A frozen food distribution centre serves three markets M1, M2, M3 with monthly volumes and map coordinates:

| Market | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | Volume $V_i$ (tonnes/month) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | 10 | 20 | 200 |
| M2 | 30 | 10 | 300 |
| M3 | 20 | 40 | 500 |

### Step 1 — Compute the Weighted Sums

$$\sum V_i = 200 + 300 + 500 = 1000 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\sum x_i V_i = (10)(200) + (30)(300) + (20)(500) = 2000 + 9000 + 10000 = 21{,}000$$

$$\sum y_i V_i = (20)(200) + (10)(300) + (40)(500) = 4000 + 3000 + 20000 = 27{,}000$$

### Step 2 — Apply Centre of Gravity Formula

$$x^* = \frac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{21{,}000}{1000} = 21 \text{ km}$$

$$y^* = \frac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{27{,}000}{1000} = 27 \text{ km}$$

### Step 3 — Distances from CoG to Each Market
$$d_1 = \sqrt{(21 - 10)^2 + (27 - 20)^2} = \sqrt{121 + 49} = \sqrt{170} = 13.04 \text{ km}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(21 - 30)^2 + (27 - 10)^2} = \sqrt{81 + 289} = \sqrt{370} = 19.24 \text{ km}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(21 - 20)^2 + (27 - 40)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 169} = \sqrt{170} = 13.04 \text{ km}$$

### Step 4 — Total Weighted Distance
$$D_{total} = \sum d_i \cdot V_i = (13.04)(200) + (19.24)(300) + (13.04)(500)$$

$$D_{total} = 2608 + 5772 + 6520 = 14{,}900 \text{ tonne·km}$$

> **Conclusion:** The optimal location is at **(21 km, 27 km)**, and the total weighted distance is **14,900 tonne·km/month**. This is the **minimum total transportation cost** point in a straight-line metric.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Method | Type | Number of Sites | When to Use | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rating Factor Method** | Qualitative + Quantitative | ≥ 2 | Many qualitative/strategic factors; subjective weights | High (7–10 marks) |
| **Break-Even Analysis** | Purely Quantitative | 2 | When cost data is reliable; capacity is known | Medium (5–7 marks) |
| **Centre of Gravity** | Purely Quantitative | Multiple markets | Distribution/warehousing decision | Medium (5–7 marks) |
| **Cumulative Cost Curves** | Quantitative | Multiple | Comparing total delivered cost across sites | Low–Medium (3–5 marks) |
| **Factor Comparison + Ranking** | Qualitative | 2–3 | When limited time, fast screening | Low (3 marks) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Plant Location Factors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FOOD PLANT LOCATION FACTORS]:::root
    A --> B[PRIMARY FACTORS]:::nodeA
    A --> C[SECONDARY FOOD-SPECIFIC FACTORS]:::nodeB

    B --> B1[Raw Material Supply]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Market Proximity]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Transportation]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Labor Availability]:::leaf
    B --> B5[Utilities Power Fuel]:::leaf
    B --> B6[Land Cost and Topography]:::leaf
    B --> B7[Government Policy and Tax]:::leaf
    B --> B8[Capital and Operating Cost]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Perishability of Raw Material]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Water Quality and Quantity]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Climatic Conditions]:::leaf
    C --> C4[Sanitation Buffer Zone]:::leaf
    C --> C5[Effluent and Waste Disposal]:::leaf
    C --> C6[Cold Chain Infrastructure]:::leaf
    C --> C7[Packaging Material Supply]:::leaf
    C --> C8[Pest and Vermin Control]:::leaf
    C --> C9[Community and Social Acceptance]:::leaf
    C --> C10[Fire Safety and Security]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#0b3d91,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nodeA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef nodeB fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#444,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Plant Location Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Product and Process]:::step
    S2[Step 2: Identify List of Candidate Sites]:::step
    S3[Step 3: List All Location Factors]:::step
    S4[Step 4: Assign Weights to Factors]:::step
    S5[Step 5: Rate Each Site against Factors]:::step
    S6[Step 6: Compute Weighted Score per Site]:::step
    S7[Step 7: Apply Break-Even Check]:::step
    S8[Step 8: Apply Centre of Gravity Check]:::step
    D1{Is Score Margin > Threshold?}:::decide
    S9[Step 9: Conduct Site Visit and Due Diligence]:::step
    S10[Step 10: Final Selection and Statutory Approvals]:::step
    S11[Re-evaluate with Additional Sites]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> D1
    D1 -- YES --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
    D1 -- NO --> S11 --> S3

    classDef step fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decide fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Raw-Material vs Market Orientation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RM["RAW-MATERIAL ORIENTED"]
        RMF[Farm or Orchard] --> RMTr[Refrigerated Truck Inbound] --> RMP[Plant Site Co-located with Source] --> RMD[Finished Product to Distant Market]
    end
    subgraph MO["MARKET ORIENTED"]
        MOF[Source] --> MOTr[Long-haul Transport Inbound] --> MOP[Plant Site Near City or Market] --> MOD[Finished Product to Local Retail]
    end
    classDef rmo fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef moo fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class RMF,RMTr,RMP,RMD rmo
    class MOF,MOTr,MOP,MOD moo
```

## 4.4 Buffer Zone and Effluent Disposal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Food Plant Boundary]:::plant
    P --> BZ1[Sanitation Buffer Zone 300 to 500 m]:::buf
    P --> BZ2[Green Belt Vegetative Barrier]:::buf
    P --> BZ3[Effluent Treatment Plant Inside Boundary]:::buf
    P --> BZ4[Solid Waste Collection and Composting Unit]:::buf
    P --> BZ5[Storm Water Drains Sloped Away from Plant]:::buf
    P --> BZ6[Fire Water Reservoir 2 to 4 hr Capacity]:::buf
    BZ1 --> EXT1[No Polluting Industry Within 1 km]:::ext
    BZ1 --> EXT2[No Garbage Dump or Tannery]:::ext

    classDef plant fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef buf fill:#ffbb78,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#98df8a,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Flow: Rating Factor Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Factor List] --> W[Weight Assignment]:::hl
    S1[Site A Ratings] --> CM[Cross Matrix]:::hl
    S2[Site B Ratings] --> CM
    S3[Site C Ratings] --> CM
    W --> CM
    CM --> CP[Weighted Score Computation]:::hl
    CP --> RK[Ranking]:::hl
    RK --> SEL[Selected Site]:::hl
    classDef hl fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
