<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Raw Material Index" and state its significance in food plant location.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Raw Material Index (RMI)** is the ratio of the weight of raw material consumed to the weight of the finished product produced.

$$RMI = \frac{W_{RM}}{W_{FP}}$$

**Significance:**
- If $RMI > 1$ → raw material is bulkier/heavier → plant should be **raw-material oriented** (located near source).
- If $RMI < 1$ → finished product is bulkier → plant should be **market oriented** (located near consumer).
- It is the **first quantitative screening test** in food plant location and is highly relevant for *perishable* agricultural raw materials (fruits, milk, fish, meat).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six factors specific to food plant location that distinguish it from other process industries.**

**Model Answer:**
Six food-specific location factors:
1. **Perishability** of raw material (cold chain dependence).
2. **Water quality and quantity** (potable, IS 10500 compliant).
3. **Sanitation buffer zone** from pollution sources.
4. **Effluent disposal** (high BOD/COD wastewater; CPCB norms).
5. **Climatic conditions** (low humidity, low microbial load).
6. **Cold chain infrastructure** (reefer vans, cold stores, ripening chambers).

*(Any 6 valid factors fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **primary and secondary factors** governing the location of a food processing plant. Why are secondary factors more critical for food plants than for, say, a chemical or engineering industry?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A breakfast-cereal manufacturer is comparing **two sites — P and Q**. The decision factors, weights, and ratings are tabulated below. Using the **Rating Factor Method**, identify the preferred site.

| Factor | Weight | Rating (P) | Rating (Q) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheat supply | 0.30 | 9 | 7 |
| Market access | 0.20 | 6 | 9 |
| Skilled labor | 0.15 | 7 | 8 |
| Water | 0.15 | 8 | 7 |
| Power | 0.10 | 7 | 8 |
| Effluent disposal | 0.10 | 8 | 7 |

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Model Answer

**Primary (General) Factors:**
1. Proximity to raw material.
2. Proximity to market.
3. Transportation and infrastructure.
4. Labor availability and cost.
5. Land cost and topography.
6. Utilities — water, power, fuel.
7. Government policy, taxes, and incentives.
8. Capital and operating cost.

**Secondary (Food-Specific) Factors:**
1. Perishability of raw material — dictates cold-chain logistics.
2. Water quality — must be potable, pathogen-free.
3. Sanitation buffer — distance from pollution sources.
4. Effluent/waste disposal — high BOD wastewater and organic solid waste.
5. Climatic conditions — humidity, temperature, salt-laden air.
6. Cold chain infrastructure — reefer vans, cold stores.
7. Pest and vermin control — surrounding vegetation.
8. Community and cultural acceptance — religious/social sensitivity.
9. Fire safety and security — flammability of dust and packaging.

**Why secondary factors dominate in food plants:**
- Food safety (FSSAI, HACCP, ISO 22000) is **non-negotiable**; failure leads to product recall, brand damage, and criminal liability.
- Microbial growth in raw materials is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature and humidity.
- Effluent from food plants has **high BOD/COD** and **organic load**; discharge into municipal drains can cause severe pollution.
- A chemical plant may tolerate a more hostile environment; a food plant cannot.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Validate Weights**
$$\sum w_i = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute Score for Site P**
$$S_P = (0.30)(9) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(8)$$

$$S_P = 2.70 + 1.20 + 1.05 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.80 = 7.65$$

**Step 3 — Compute Score for Site Q**
$$S_Q = (0.30)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(8) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(7)$$

$$S_Q = 2.10 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 1.05 + 0.80 + 0.70 = 7.65$$

**Step 4 — Tie-Breaking**
Both sites score **7.65** → tie. Apply a **break-even cost check** (assumed equal in question) → recommend **both sites equally viable**, with Site P preferred for **raw material (wheat) strength** and Site Q for **market access strength**. **Engineering judgement + secondary-factor field visit** must resolve the tie.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):**
> Students often *only list factors* without comparing food vs chemical industry — losing 2 marks on the "why secondary factors dominate" part. Always explicitly contrast food industry characteristics (perishability, sanitation, BOD effluent) with chemical/engineering industry to fetch full marks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two candidate sites **A** and **B** for a fruit juice plant have the following cost data:

| Parameter | Site A | Site B |
|---|---|---|
| Annual fixed cost | ₹ 60 lakh | ₹ 90 lakh |
| Variable cost per unit | ₹ 150 | ₹ 100 |

If the expected annual output is **40,000 units**, determine the **break-even quantity** and recommend the preferred site.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Centre of Gravity Method** for locating a food distribution warehouse. A frozen-food warehouse must serve four markets with the following data. Determine the optimal location.

| Market | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | Volume (tonnes/mo) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | 5 | 10 | 100 |
| M2 | 25 | 5 | 200 |
| M3 | 15 | 30 | 300 |
| M4 | 35 | 20 | 400 |

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Write Total Cost Equations**
$$TC_A = 60{,}00{,}000 + 150 \cdot Q$$
$$TC_B = 90{,}00{,}000 + 100 \cdot Q$$

**Step 2 — Equate to Find Break-Even**
$$60L + 150 Q^* = 90L + 100 Q^*$$

$$50 Q^* = 30L$$

$$Q^* = 60{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Expected $Q = 40{,}000$**
- $Q = 40{,}000 < Q^* = 60{,}000$
- Expected volume is **below the break-even**; hence the site with **lower fixed cost (A)** is preferred at this volume.

**Step 4 — Verify by Direct Computation**
- $TC_A = 60L + 150(40{,}000) = 60L + 60L = $ **₹ 1.20 crore**
- $TC_B = 90L + 100(40{,}000) = 90L + 40L = $ **₹ 1.30 crore**

> **Recommendation: Choose Site A.** The firm will break-even with Site A vs Site B at 60,000 units/yr. If production scales beyond 60,000 units, switch to Site B.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Concept of Centre of Gravity (CoG) Method:**
The CoG method finds the **single optimal point** $(x^*, y^*)$ that minimises the **total weighted transportation distance** between a facility and multiple demand points. It assumes:
1. Locations are on a flat 2-D plane with rectangular coordinates.
2. Transportation cost is **linear with distance and volume**.
3. All demand points are served from a single facility.

**Step 1 — Compute Volume Sum**
$$\sum V_i = 100 + 200 + 300 + 400 = 1000 \text{ tonnes/month}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Weighted Coordinate Sums**
$$\sum x_i V_i = (5)(100) + (25)(200) + (15)(300) + (35)(400)$$

$$\sum x_i V_i = 500 + 5000 + 4500 + 14000 = 24{,}000$$

$$\sum y_i V_i = (10)(100) + (5)(200) + (30)(300) + (20)(400)$$

$$\sum y_i V_i = 1000 + 1000 + 9000 + 8000 = 19{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Apply CoG Formula**

$$x^* = \frac{24{,}000}{1000} = 24 \text{ km}$$

$$y^* = \frac{19{,}000}{1000} = 19 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Total Weighted Distance from CoG**
$$d_1 = \sqrt{(24-5)^2 + (19-10)^2} = \sqrt{361 + 81} = \sqrt{442} = 21.02 \text{ km}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(24-25)^2 + (19-5)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 196} = \sqrt{197} = 14.04 \text{ km}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(24-15)^2 + (19-30)^2} = \sqrt{81 + 121} = \sqrt{202} = 14.21 \text{ km}$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{(24-35)^2 + (19-20)^2} = \sqrt{121 + 1} = \sqrt{122} = 11.05 \text{ km}$$

$$D_{total} = (21.02)(100) + (14.04)(200) + (14.21)(300) + (11.05)(400)$$

$$D_{total} = 2102 + 2808 + 4263 + 4420 = 13{,}593 \text{ tonne·km}$$

> **Conclusion:** The frozen-food warehouse should be located at **(24 km, 19 km)** to minimise total weighted transportation distance to **13,593 tonne·km/month**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):**
> Common errors include (1) forgetting to multiply coordinates by volume, (2) using arithmetic mean of coordinates (which is wrong unless all volumes are equal), (3) computing straight-line distance and quoting it as actual road distance without the **1.25–1.40 road conversion factor**. Always state the **assumption of linear transport cost** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Combined Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Plant Location Questions:**
> 1. **No mention of perishability** — food plants are unique because raw materials decay; this single keyword can fetch 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to validate weight sum = 1** in Rating Factor Method — leads to 1 mark deduction if not done.
> 3. **Not specifying units** in break-even and CoG answers (units/year, tonnes/km, km) — KTU examiners are strict.
> 4. **Ignoring secondary factors** like water, effluent, cold chain — these are *food-specific* and carry high weight.
> 5. **Confusing RMI with Finished Product Index (FPI)** — remember: **RMI > 1 → raw-material oriented**; **RMI < 1 → market oriented**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **RMI > 1 → plant near source; RMI < 1 → plant near market.** Food plants are dominantly raw-material oriented.
- **Two categories of factors:** Primary (general) and Secondary (food-specific). Secondary factors *dominate* in food plant location.
- **Eight food-specific must-know factors:** Perishability, Water, Climate, Sanitation buffer, Effluent, Cold chain, Pest control, Community.
- **Rating Factor Method:** $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$; weights $\sum w_i = 1$; ratings 0–10.
- **Break-Even Quantity:** $Q^* = (F_B - F_A) / (V_A - V_B)$. If actual $Q < Q^*$ → lower fixed cost site; if $Q > Q^*$ → lower variable cost site.
- **Centre of Gravity:** $x^* = \sum x_i V_i / \sum V_i$, $y^* = \sum y_i V_i / \sum V_i$. Distances are *Euclidean*; multiply by 1.25–1.40 for road.
- **Site validation checklist:** Potable water, three-phase power, road connectivity, effluent discharge compliance, fire safety, future expansion, 300–500 m buffer from pollution sources.
- **Decision integration:** Use **Rating Factor** for qualitative ranking + **Break-Even** for cost validation + **CoG** for distribution centre siting.
- **KTU Module 1 outcome:** Be able to *list, classify, justify, and apply quantitative methods* for food plant location.
- **Common KTU exam keywords to embed in answers:** perishability, RMI, FSSAI, GMP, HACCP, BOD/COD, cold chain, buffer zone, common effluent treatment plant, rating factor, break-even, centre of gravity.
- **Units always mandatory:** ₹/year, tonnes/month, km, m³/tonne, kWh, m².

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material of construction for food processing equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material of construction for food processing equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material of Construction (MOC)** for food processing equipment refers to the specific metals, alloys, non-metals, polymers, and composites selected for fabricating machinery, piping, vessels, conveyors, and contact surfaces that handle food raw materials, intermediates, and finished products.

The selection is governed by the dual mandate of **product safety** (no migration of toxic substances into food) and **equipment longevity** (resistance to corrosion, mechanical wear, thermal fatigue, and chemical attack from cleaning agents like CIP — Clean-In-Place chemicals such as $\text{NaOH}$ and $\text{HNO}_3$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Concept:** The material in contact with food is called the **Food Contact Surface (FCS)**. Regulatory bodies such as **USFDA**, **EU 1935/2004**, and **FSSAI (India)** mandate that FCS must be *inert, non-absorbent, smooth, non-toxic, and cleanable*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **sterile hospital kitchen** where every utensil, floor tile, and pipe must be:
- **Non-reactive** (does not leach chemicals into food),
- **Easy to sanitize** (no microscopic cracks where bacteria hide),
- **Durable** (survives hot steam, cold brine, and harsh detergents for 15+ years).

Food processing equipment is exactly that — a **sterile industrial kitchen**. The *Material of Construction* is the DNA that decides whether the equipment is a **stainless steel surgeon** (SS 316L), a **cast-iron workhorse** (carbon steel), or a **plastic helper** (food-grade HDPE). Choose wrong, and you get **corrosion pits harboring *Listeria***, **metal leaching into tomato sauce**, or **catastrophic weld failure under steam pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Golden Rule" of Food Equipment Material Selection:**
> $$\text{Stainless Steel} \; \neq \; \text{Stainless Steel}$$
> The grade matters — **SS 304** is for dry foods, **SS 316L** is for salty/acidic foods (dairy, sauces, marine products).

---

### 1.3 General Requirements of MOC for Food Processing Equipment

| # | Property | Why It Matters in Food Plants |
|---|----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Non-toxicity & Inertness** | No chemical migration into food (regulatory compliance) |
| 2 | **Corrosion Resistance** | Withstands acids (citric, lactic), salts (brines), and CIP chemicals |
| 3 | **Mechanical Strength** | Resists pressure (e.g., **0.5–2.5 MPa** in HTST pasteurizers), impact, fatigue |
| 4 | **Thermal Stability** | Tolerates **−40 °C** to **300 °C** (retort, blast freezer) |
| 5 | **Cleanability & Surface Finish** | Surface roughness **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** (sanitary finish) prevents biofilm |
| 6 | **Weldability & Fabricability** | Allows sanitary welding (TIG/orbital) and forming |
| 7 | **Cost & Availability** | CapEx vs. lifecycle cost (15–25 year plant life) |
| 8 | **Thermal Conductivity** | $k$ value affects heat exchanger design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted:** Surface roughness is measured in **µm (micrometers)** or **µin (microinches)**. The sanitary standard **3-A Sanitary Standards** require **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm (32 µin)** for product contact surfaces in dairy and beverage plants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Materials (Master Taxonomy)

The vast universe of food-plant materials collapses into **four families**:

#### **A. Ferrous Metals (Iron-Based)**

##### **i. Carbon Steel (CS / Mild Steel)**
- Composition: $\text{Fe} + (0.05–2.0)\% \text{ C}$
- **Pros:** Cheap (**₹60–80/kg**), high tensile strength (**400–550 MPa**), easy to fabricate.
- **Cons:** **Corrodes rapidly** in presence of moisture, acids, and salts. Forms **iron oxide ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ — rust)** which is **toxic and discoloring**.
- **Application:** **Non-contact** structural frames, support legs, gearbox housings, external platforms, storage hoppers (with food-grade epoxy lining).

##### **ii. Stainless Steel (SS) — The King of Food Plants**
- Composition: $\text{Fe} + \geq 10.5\% \text{ Cr}$ (forms passive $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ film).
- **Why chromium?** It reacts with oxygen to form an invisible, self-healing **chromium oxide passive layer**:
$$\text{4 Cr + 3 O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2 Cr}_2\text{O}_3$$
- This passive film is the **reason stainless steel "doesn't rust"** — it does, but the layer re-forms instantly when scratched (in oxidizing environments).

**The Three Kings of Food-Grade Stainless Steel:**

| Grade | Composition (Key Alloys) | Corrosion Resistance | Typical Food Application |
|-------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **SS 304** (AISI 304) | 18% Cr, 8% Ni | Good (general) | Milk tanks, conveyor frames, dry-food hoppers |
| **SS 316** (AISI 316) | 16% Cr, 10% Ni, **2–3% Mo** | Excellent (chloride-rich) | Brine systems, seafood, tomato paste, pharma |
| **SS 316L** | 316 + **low C (≤0.03%)** | Excellent + weld-safe | Welded vessels, fermentation tanks, retorts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "L" in 316L?** The "L" stands for **Low Carbon (≤ 0.03%)**. During welding, regular 316 can form **chromium carbide ($\text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6$)** at grain boundaries — a phenomenon called **sensitization** — which depletes Cr at the weld zone and causes **intergranular corrosion**. The low carbon prevents this.

##### **iii. Other Stainless Grades**
- **SS 430** (ferritic, no Ni): Cheap, magnetic; used in decorative panels and low-corrosion zones.
- **SS 2205 (Duplex)**: Combines austenitic + ferritic; used in **high-pressure** and **chloride-rich** processing.

---

#### **B. Non-Ferrous Metals & Alloys**

##### **i. Aluminum & Alloys**
- Composition: Pure Al or Al-Mg, Al-Mn, Al-Si alloys.
- **Pros:** Lightweight (**density 2.7 g/cm³**), excellent thermal conductivity ($k = 237 \text{ W/m·K}$), good for heat exchangers.
- **Cons:** Reacts with **acidic** (pH < 4) and **alkaline** (pH > 9) foods; forms **aluminum oxide film** but can pit.
- **Application:** Brewing kettles, milk cans (older designs), chocolate moulds, foil packaging.

##### **ii. Copper & Alloys (Brass, Bronze)**
- Composition: Cu + Zn (brass) or Cu + Sn (bronze).
- **Pros:** Superior thermal conductivity ($k_{\text{Cu}} = 401 \text{ W/m·K}$), antimicrobial.
- **Cons:** Reacts with **protein-rich** and **acidic** foods → **catalyzes oxidation** (turns milk rancid, beer cloudy).
- **Application:** **Restricted to non-contact** parts: steam coils (tinned), bearings, valves.

##### **iii. Nickel & Nickel Alloys (Monel, Inconel)**
- Used in highly corrosive food processes (e.g., **caustic peeling of potatoes** with **10% NaOH at 90 °C**).
- Expensive; used as cladding on CS vessels.

---

#### **C. Non-Metallic Materials**

##### **i. Plastics & Polymers (Food-Grade)**

| Polymer | Food Application | Max Temp |
|---------|------------------|----------|
| **HDPE** (High-Density Polyethylene) | Milk crates, bottle caps, chemical tanks | 120 °C |
| **PP** (Polypropylene) | Yogurt cups, CIP chemical tanks | 135 °C |
| **PTFE** (Teflon) | Gaskets, seals, non-stick surfaces (bakery) | 260 °C |
| **PVDF** (Polyvinylidene Fluoride) | Piping for aggressive chemicals | 150 °C |
| **Epoxy Resin** (food-grade) | Internal coating of carbon steel tanks | 90 °C |

##### **ii. Rubber (Food-Grade)**
- **EPDM, Nitrile, Silicone** used in **gaskets, O-rings, pump diaphragms**.
- Must be **FDA / FSSAI approved**.

##### **iii. Glass**
- **Borosilicate glass**: Lab-scale reactors, sight glasses, pipelines for beer/wine.
- **Pros:** Inert, transparent, easy to clean.
- **Cons:** **Brittle**, breaks on impact — not for high-pressure lines.

##### **iv. Ceramics**
- Used in **grinding media** (ball mills for spice grinding), **brick-lined** reactors, and **porcelain enamel** coatings on steel.

##### **v. Wood**
- Used historically for cheese aging racks, barrels (oak for wine/whiskey).
- **Restricted** in modern plants due to **hygiene issues** (porous, harbours microbes).

---

#### **D. Composite & Coated Materials**
- **Glass-Lined Steel (enamel)**: Carbon steel substrate with vitreous glass coating for **acidic reactors** (e.g., sauce kettles).
- **Rubber-Lined Steel**: For abrasive slurries (e.g., tomato concentrate).
- **FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Plastic)**: For outdoor storage tanks, fume ducts.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Corrosion Rate (CR)** | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | mpy (mils/yr) | Material selection |
| 2 | **Corrosion Rate (SI)** | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87600 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | mm/yr | Material selection |
| 3 | **Heat Conduction** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W | Heat exchanger MOC |
| 4 | **Fouling Resistance** | $R_f = \dfrac{1}{h_{\text{clean}}} - \dfrac{1}{h_{\text{fouled}}}$ | m²·K/W | Surface finish design |
| 5 | **Sanitary Surface Finish** | $R_a \leq 0.8 \; \mu\text{m} \; (32 \; \mu\text{in})$ | µm | 3-A / EHEDG standard |
| 6 | **Tensile Strength Selection** | $\sigma \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\text{FoS}}$ | MPa | Pressure vessel MOC |
| 7 | **Thin-Wall Cylinder Stress** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | MPa | Vessel wall design |
| 8 | **Thermal Expansion** | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | m | Welded joint design |

> **Variable Definitions (for formulas above):**
> $W$ = weight loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = exposed area (in²), $T$ = time (hr),
> $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $P$ = internal pressure (MPa), $r$ = radius, $t$ = wall thickness,
> $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion (1/°C), $R_a$ = arithmetic mean roughness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Formulas Matter in Plant Design:** The **corrosion rate** directly dictates the **replacement schedule** and **wall thickness allowance** for vessels. The **thermal conductivity $k$** drives the choice of MOC in heat exchangers (aluminum for high $k$, SS for moderate). The **$R_a$ finish** determines whether bacteria can attach and form biofilm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Corrosion Rate Formula (ASTM G1 Standard)

The **corrosion rate (CR)** is the single most important metric used by KTU examiners to compare material suitability. Let us derive it from **first principles**.

---

#### **STEP 1 — Define the Physical Meaning**

A metal coupon of known **mass $M_0$** and **surface area $A$** is exposed to a corrosive food/chemical medium for time $T$. After exposure, the corroded mass is $M_1$. The mass lost is:

$$W = M_0 - M_1$$

This $W$ (in mg) is the experimentally measured quantity.

---

#### **STEP 2 — Convert Mass Loss to Volume Loss**

The volume of metal lost is:

$$V_{\text{lost}} = \dfrac{W}{D}$$

where $D$ is the **density** of the metal (g/cm³) and $W$ must be in grams. To keep units consistent, we use $W$ in mg and convert:

$$V_{\text{lost}} = \dfrac{W (\text{mg})}{D (\text{g/cm}^3) \cdot 1000} \;\;\;\; \text{[in cm}^3\text{]}$$

---

#### **STEP 3 — Assume Uniform Attack (Worst-Case Engineering Approximation)**

If the corrosion is **uniform**, the volume loss corresponds to a uniform thickness reduction $L$ (in inches or mm) over the exposed area $A$:

$$V_{\text{lost}} = A \cdot L$$

Solving for $L$:

$$L = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A}$$

---

#### **STEP 4 — Convert "Length Lost" to a Time-Normalized Rate**

The **corrosion rate** is the depth lost per year. There are **8760 hours in a non-leap year (365 × 24)**. To express $L$ as **mils per year (mpy)**, we must:

- Convert cm to **mils** (1 cm = 393.7 mils, since 1 inch = 2.54 cm and 1 inch = 1000 mils → 1 cm = 1000/2.54 = 393.7 mils).
- Multiply by the **hours-per-year factor (8760)** and divide by exposure time $T$ in hours.

Combining all constants:

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{W \times 393.7 \times 8760}{D \cdot A \cdot T \times 1000 \times 1000}$$

The numerical value simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}}$$

---

#### **STEP 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**

**Problem:** A **SS 316L** plate of density **$D = 8.0 \;\text{g/cm}^3$** and area **$A = 10 \;\text{in}^2$** is exposed to **tomato pulp (pH 4.2)** at **80 °C** for **720 hours**. The mass loss observed is **$W = 120 \;\text{mg}$**. Determine the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**, and decide if SS 316L is acceptable.

**Solution:**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot 120}{8.0 \cdot 10 \cdot 720}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$534 \cdot 120 = 64080$$

Computing the denominator:

$$8.0 \cdot 10 \cdot 720 = 57600$$

Dividing:

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{64080}{57600} = 1.1125 \;\;\; \text{mpy}$$

Converting mpy to mm/yr (1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr):

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 1.1125 \times 0.0254 = 0.02826 \;\;\; \text{mm/yr}$$

**Decision Rule (NACE / ISO 17474):**

| Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | Suitability |
|------------------------|-------------|
| < 0.05 | Excellent — long-term service |
| 0.05 – 0.10 | Good — acceptable |
| 0.10 – 0.50 | Marginal — needs monitoring |
| > 0.50 | Not acceptable — replace |

Since **0.028 mm/yr < 0.05 mm/yr**, **SS 316L is excellent** for this tomato pulp service. ✅

---

#### **STEP 6 — Comparative Analytical Matrix**

| Material | CR in 5% NaCl Spray (mm/yr) | CR in 10% Citric Acid (mm/yr) | CR in Milk (mm/yr) | Verdict |
|---------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|---------|
| Carbon Steel (CS) | 0.80 – 1.50 | 5.00 – 8.00 | 0.30 – 0.60 | ❌ Unfit for FCS |
| SS 304 | 0.005 – 0.020 | 0.05 – 0.10 | < 0.005 | ✅ Good (general) |
| SS 316 | 0.001 – 0.010 | 0.01 – 0.05 | < 0.005 | ✅ Excellent |
| SS 316L | 0.001 – 0.008 | 0.01 – 0.04 | < 0.005 | ✅ Best for welds |
| Aluminum 1100 | 0.10 – 0.50 | 1.00 – 3.00 | 0.05 – 0.20 | ⚠️ Marginal |
| Copper C110 | 0.05 – 0.20 | 2.00 – 4.00 | 0.20 – 0.50 | ❌ Toxic ion leaching |
| HDPE | < 0.001 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 | ✅ Chemically inert |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Note how **SS 316L** is the *only* material with < 0.01 mm/yr in **all three** food environments. This is why 90% of modern dairy and beverage plants in Kerala (KMF, Milma plants) use SS 316L for product contact.

---

### 3.2 Sanitary Surface Finish: Why $R_a$ Matters

The **biofilm formation rate** is empirically related to surface roughness. The classic Characklis equation (simplified):

$$R_{\text{biofilm}} \propto R_a^{0.5}$$

This means a surface with $R_a = 0.8 \; \mu\text{m}$ harbours **less than half the biofilm** of one with $R_a = 3.2 \; \mu\text{m}$. Hence, **electropolishing** (which reduces $R_a$ from 0.8 to 0.2 µm) is standard in pharmaceutical and premium food plants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Material Selection Hierarchy Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Product Profile] --> B{pH Condition}
    B -->|pH less than 4| C[Acidic Zone]
    B -->|pH 4 to 7| D[Neutral Zone]
    B -->|pH greater than 7| E[Alkaline Zone]
    A --> F{Temperature}
    F -->|LT minus 20 to 50| G[Low Temp Process]
    F -->|MT 50 to 100| H[Medium Temp Process]
    F -->|HT 100 to 300| I[High Temp Process]
    A --> J{Chloride Contact}
    J -->|Yes| K[High Cl Zone]
    J -->|No| L[Low Cl Zone]
    C --> M{Material Selection Engine}
    E --> M
    D --> M
    G --> M
    H --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[SS 316L Recommended]
    M --> O[SS 304 Acceptable]
    M --> P[HDPE for Non Contact]
    M --> Q[Aluminum for Heat Exchanger]
    M --> R[Glass Lined CS for Reactor]
    N --> S[Final MOC Selection]
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[Cost Optimization]
    T --> U[Plant Layout Integration]
```

**Reading the Flow:**
The diagram shows a **3-axis decision engine**:
1. **pH Condition** (chemistry of food),
2. **Temperature** (thermal load),
3. **Chloride Contact** (salty/sea-food presence).

All three inputs feed into a **Material Selection Engine** that recommends one of five MOC families, which then flow to **Cost Optimization** and **Plant Layout Integration**.

---

### 4.2 Stainless Steel Family Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stainless Steel Family] --> B[Austenitic 300 Series]
    A --> C[Ferritic 400 Series]
    A --> D[Martensitic 400 Series]
    A --> E[Duplex 2000 Series]
    B --> B1[SS 304 General Use]
    B --> B2[SS 316 Chloride Rich]
    B --> B3[SS 316L Welded Vessels]
    C --> C1[SS 430 Magnetic Low Cost]
    D --> D1[SS 410 Cutlery Grade]
    E --> E1[SS 2205 High Pressure]
    B1 --> X[Food Plant Application]
    B2 --> X
    B3 --> X
    C1 --> X
    E1 --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Note:** Austenitic grades (300 series) are **non-magnetic** and dominate food plants because of superior corrosion resistance and formability. Ferritic and martensitic grades are **magnetic** and used in cutting/structural applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. List any six desirable properties of materials used for the construction of food processing equipment.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (6 points, ½ mark each):**

1. **Non-toxicity** — must not leach harmful substances into food.
2. **Corrosion resistance** — withstands food acids, salts, and CIP chemicals.
3. **Mechanical strength** — resists pressure, impact, and fatigue.
4. **Thermal stability** — operates over the required temperature range.
5. **Sanitary surface finish** — low $R_a$ value to prevent biofilm.
6. **Weldability and cleanability** — supports sanitary welding and CIP/SIP.
7. *(Bonus)* Cost-effectiveness and ready availability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark question):** Students often write vague phrases like *"should be good"* or *"should last long"*. The 3-mark scheme demands **6 distinct technical properties**. Write the property **name + one-line justification**.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between SS 304 and SS 316 stainless steel.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (Tabular — 6 distinguishing points):**

| Parameter | SS 304 | SS 316 |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| **Composition** | 18% Cr, 8% Ni | 16% Cr, 10% Ni, **2–3% Mo** |
| **Molybdenum** | Absent | Present (key difference) |
| **Corrosion in chlorides** | Susceptible to pitting | **Resistant** (Mo stabilizes passive film) |
| **Cost** | Lower (₹250/kg) | Higher (₹380/kg) |
| **Typical food use** | Dry foods, milk tanks, conveyors | Brine, seafood, sauces, pharma |
| **Magnetic?** | No | No |
| **Weldability** | Good | Good (316L preferred for welds) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing *"316 is better than 304"* without specifying **why**. The presence of **molybdenum** and its role in resisting **chloride-induced pitting corrosion** is the key answer.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Discuss in detail the various types of stainless steel used in food processing equipment. Compare their properties, applications, and reasons for selection.**

**Sub-part (a) — Classification & Properties (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Introduction to Stainless Steel** (1 mark)
   - Definition: Iron-based alloy with $\geq 10.5\%$ Cr forming passive $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ film.
   - Reaction: $4\text{Cr} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$.

2. **Austenitic SS — 300 Series** (2 marks)
   - **SS 304**: 18 Cr / 8 Ni, non-magnetic, workhorse grade.
   - **SS 316**: + 2–3% Mo, chloride-resistant.
   - **SS 316L**: low C (≤0.03%), resists sensitization at welds.
   - **SS 321**: Ti-stabilized, high-temp service.

3. **Ferritic SS — 400 Series** (1 mark)
   - **SS 430**: 16–18% Cr, no Ni, magnetic, low cost.
   - Used in decorative panels, low-corrosion zones.

4. **Martensitic SS** (1 mark)
   - **SS 410, 420**: Hardenable, used for **cutting blades, mixer shafts, valve seats**.
   - Limited corrosion resistance.

5. **Duplex SS** (1 mark)
   - **SS 2205**: Austenitic + Ferritic, high strength, chloride-rich services.

6. **Properties Summary** (1 mark) — table of $R_m$, $R_{p0.2}$, $R_a$, max temp.

**Sub-part (b) — Applications & Selection Logic (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Dairy Industry** (1.5 marks)
   - **SS 304**: Cold milk storage tanks, conveyor piping.
   - **SS 316L**: Pasteurizer plates, cream separators, cheese vats.

2. **Beverage Industry** (1.5 marks)
   - **SS 316L**: Brewing fermenters, wine storage, juice heat exchangers.
   - Reason: resistance to citric acid and tannins.

3. **Seafood & Marine** (1 mark)
   - **SS 316 / 316L** mandatory due to high chloride.

4. **Meat & Poultry** (1 mark)
   - **SS 304** for contact surfaces; **SS 316L** for brine injectors.

5. **Bakery & Confectionery** (1 mark)
   - **SS 304** for oven interiors, dough mixers.
   - **SS 316L** for chocolate conches (cocoa is acidic).

6. **Selection Logic Flowchart** (1 mark)
   - pH < 4 → 316L; pH 4–7 + low Cl → 304; Cl present → 316L.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (14-mark question):**
> - **Do not** write only definitions. KTU expects **classification + properties + applications + selection logic**.
> - **Do not** confuse **austenitic** (FCC crystal, non-magnetic) with **ferritic** (BCC, magnetic).
> - **Always mention** the passive film chemistry — it is a favourite 2-mark follow-up.
> - For **14 marks**, the model answer should run **4–5 pages** of legible handwriting.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Discuss the requirements and selection criteria for materials in food processing equipment. Explain with examples how pH, temperature, and chloride content influence material choice.**

**Sub-part (a) — Requirements & Selection Criteria (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Functional Requirements** (2 marks)
   - Mechanical: tensile, fatigue, impact, hardness.
   - Thermal: conductivity, expansion, max service temperature.
   - Chemical: corrosion resistance, inertness, CIP compatibility.

2. **Hygienic Requirements** (2 marks)
   - Surface finish $R_a \leq 0.8 \; \mu\text{m}$ (3-A standard).
   - No crevices, dead legs, or threaded joints in product zones.
   - Weld smoothness: orbital TIG, electropolished.

3. **Regulatory Requirements** (1.5 marks)
   - USFDA 21 CFR, EU 1935/2004, FSSAI, BIS, 3-A Sanitary Standards.

4. **Economic Requirements** (1.5 marks)
   - CapEx vs. lifecycle cost. SS 316L costs 1.5× SS 304 but lasts 2–3× longer in chloride service.

**Sub-part (b) — Influence of pH, T, and Cl⁻ (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Effect of pH** (2.5 marks)
   - **Acidic (pH < 4)**: citrus, tomato, vinegar → require **SS 316L** or **glass-lined CS**. Carbon steel corrodes at > 1 mm/yr.
   - **Neutral (pH 4–7)**: milk, beer, juice → **SS 304** adequate.
   - **Alkaline (pH > 9)**: lye peeling (NaOH 10%) → **SS 316L** or **nickel alloys**.

2. **Effect of Temperature** (2.5 marks)
   - **Low T (< 50 °C)**: cold storage, chilling — HDPE, SS 304 acceptable.
   - **Medium T (50–100 °C)**: pasteurization — **SS 316L** (chloride + heat accelerates pitting).
   - **High T (100–300 °C)**: retort, sterilization — **SS 316L** with cladding or enamel-lined CS.

3. **Effect of Chloride Content** (2 marks)
   - **Low Cl⁻ (< 50 ppm)**: SS 304 acceptable.
   - **High Cl⁻ (> 500 ppm)**: seawater, brine, cheese whey → **SS 316L mandatory** (Mo prevents pitting).
   - **Case Study**: KMF dairy plant — switched from SS 304 to SS 316L after 18 months of pitting in cheese brine tanks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (14-mark question):**
> - **Do not** give a generic list. KTU demands **specific examples** (tomato = SS 316L, milk = SS 304, lye peeling = SS 316L).
> - **Always link** the property to the **food chemistry**: *"Tomato contains citric + malic acid, pH 4.0, hence SS 316L."*
> - **Do not forget** the **3-A / EHEDG hygienic finish requirement** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SS 304 = 18 Cr / 8 Ni**; **SS 316 = + 2–3% Mo**; **SS 316L = low carbon (≤0.03%)**.
- [x] **Molybdenum (Mo)** is the magic element — it prevents **chloride pitting corrosion**.
- [x] **"L" grade (316L)** is mandatory for **welded** food contact vessels — prevents **sensitization**.
- [x] **Passive film:** $4\text{Cr} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ — self-healing oxide layer.
- [x] **Carbon steel** is **NEVER** used for food contact — only for structural/non-contact parts.
- [x] **Copper** is **forbidden** for direct food contact with acidic/protein-rich foods.
- [x] **Aluminum** is great for heat exchangers (high $k$) but **not for pH < 4 or pH > 9**.
- [x] **Sanitary surface finish:** $R_a \leq 0.8 \; \mu\text{m}$ (32 µin) — 3-A Sanitary Standard.
- [x] **HDPE/PP** for chemical tanks & CIP piping; **PTFE** for high-temp gaskets.
- [x] **Corrosion rate formula:** $\text{CR (mpy)} = 534 \cdot W \div (D \cdot A \cdot T)$.
- [x] **Selection Rule of Thumb:** pH < 4 → SS 316L | pH 4–7 + low Cl → SS 304 | High Cl → SS 316L.
- [x] **Pitting corrosion** is the #1 failure mode in food plant SS — controlled by Mo and low $R_a$.
- [x] **Austenitic SS** = non-magnetic; **Ferritic/Martensitic** = magnetic.
- [x] **Regulatory codes to cite:** USFDA 21 CFR, EU 1935/2004, FSSAI, 3-A, EHEDG, BIS 6911.
- [x] **Hot caustic (NaOH)** peeling of fruits/vegetables → use **SS 316L** or **nickel-clad** vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra for this Module:**
> *"In food plants, the material is not chosen — it is **engineered** based on the food's pH, temperature, and chloride exposure, with stainless steel 316L being the gold standard."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications of processing equipment and accessories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specifications of processing equipment and accessories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Food Plant Layout and Design**, the **Specifications of Processing Equipment and Accessories** refer to the comprehensive set of engineered technical documents and data sheets that quantitatively and qualitatively define every functional, dimensional, operational, and sanitary attribute of a piece of food processing machinery and its ancillaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A *specification* is a structured engineering datasheet that conveys the **design basis**, **operational envelope**, **material of construction (MOC)**, **utility requirements**, and **sanitary compliance codes** of a unit operation, thereby dictating the spatial, structural, and service-routing decisions in the plant layout.

The four canonical pillars of any food processing equipment specification are:

1. **Process Specification** – capacity, throughput, residence time, yield, and product quality parameters.
2. **Mechanical Specification** – dimensions, weight, footprint, MOC, surface finish (e.g., Ra ≤ 0.8 μm for sanitary contact surfaces).
3. **Utility Specification** – steam (kg/hr), cooling water (m³/hr), compressed air (Nm³/hr), electrical (kW), refrigerant duty (TR).
4. **Regulatory Specification** – compliance with **3-A Sanitary Standards**, **FDA 21 CFR**, **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **ASME BPVC**, **CE / ISO 22000**, and **FSSAI** requirements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Buyer's Brochure"

Imagine you walk into a Maruti Suzuki showroom to buy a Swift. The salesman hands you a brochure.

- The **Mileage (22 km/l)** and **Top Speed (165 km/h)** are the *process specs*.
- The **Dimensions (L×W×H = 3845×1735×1530 mm)** and **Kerb Weight (875–905 kg)** are the *mechanical specs* — they decide whether it fits in your garage.
- The **Fuel Type (Petrol/CNG)**, **Battery (12V, 35Ah)**, and **Service Interval (10,000 km)** are the *utility specs*.
- The **BS6 Phase 2 emission compliance** and **Global NCAP 1-star rating** are the *regulatory specs*.

**You do not buy a car without a brochure. Likewise, no Food Plant Layout can be drawn without equipment specifications.** Just as the brochure dictates your garage door width, your electricity connection, and the parking layout — the equipment datasheet dictates the column spacing, the pipe rack, and the service corridor in a food factory.

## 1.3 Key Standard Reference Metrics

The following **bold** metrics are universally used in food plant equipment datasheets:

- **Surface Roughness (Ra): ≤ 0.8 μm** for product-contact surfaces (mirror finish).
- **Steam Pressure:** **3.5 bar (g)** for cooking kettles, **7 bar (g)** for high-pressure boilers.
- **CIP Flow Velocity:** **1.5 m/s** (minimum turbulence for Clean-In-Place).
- **Hygienic Piping Slope:** **1:100** (1% gradient) for self-draining lines.
- **Steam Specific Volume (saturated at 5 bar abs):** **0.3749 m³/kg** (used in kettle sizing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to correlate equipment specifications with floor-space allocation, headroom clearance (typically **+1.0 m above tallest vessel**), and service-loop routing — the spec sheet is the *bridge* between process engineering and plant layout.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Processing Equipment and Accessories

The food plant universe of equipment is best classified by the unit operation it performs. Below is the structured logic that examiners expect.

### 2.1.1 Primary Processing Equipment
- **Material Handling:** Bucket elevators, screw conveyors, pneumatic conveyors, vibratory feeders.
- **Size Reduction:** Hammer mills, roller mills, attrition mills, ball mills.
- **Heat Transfer:** Plate Heat Exchangers (PHE), Scraped Surface Heat Exchangers (SSHE), jacketed kettles, tubular heaters, ohmic heaters.
- **Mass Transfer:** Evaporators (MVR, falling film, rising film), distillation columns, extractors, absorbers.
- **Separation:** Centrifuges (basket, disc-stack, decanter), membrane filters (MF, UF, NF, RO), cyclones, screens.
- **Mixing & Forming:** Ribbon blenders, sigma mixers, planetary mixers, extruders (single/twin-screw).
- **Thermal Processing:** Retorts (batch, continuous hydrostatic), autoclaves, pasteurizers (HTST, UHT), spray dryers, freeze dryers.
- **Packaging:** Form-Fill-Seal (FFS), aseptic fillers, vacuum packers, retort pouches.

### 2.1.2 Plant Accessories
- **Fluid Handling:** Centrifugal pumps (sanitary, EHEDG-compliant), positive displacement pumps, lobe pumps, peristaltic pumps.
- **Piping & Fittings:** Tri-Clover / DIN 11851 fittings, sanitary valves (butterfly, ball, diaphragm, mixproof), sight glasses, filters.
- **Storage:** Silos (carbon/stainless), insulated tanks, aseptic bag-in-box, IBC totes.
- **Utilities Generation:** Boilers, chillers, cooling towers, air compressors, RO plants, CIP skid.
- **Instrumentation:** PT-100 RTDs, hygienic pressure transmitters, conductivity sensors, pH probes, flow meters (Coriolis, magmeter).
- **Safety & Environmental:** Pressure Relief Valves (PRV), rupture discs, scrubbers, ETP/STP units, fire suppression.

## 2.2 Anatomy of an Equipment Datasheet (The "Spec Sheet DNA")

Every food plant equipment specification must contain the following **eight logical blocks**:

1. **Header** — Tag number (e.g., `V-101`), equipment name, P&ID reference, project code.
2. **Process Data** — Capacity (kg/hr or TPD), inlet/outlet conditions (T, P, composition), duty.
3. **Mechanical Data** — Diameter (m), height (m), shell thickness (mm), MOC (SS304/SS316L), volume (m³), weight (empty + operating, MT).
4. **Internals** — Baffles, agitator type & RPM, motor kW, gearbox ratio, shaft seal.
5. **Connections** — Nozzle schedule (size, flange rating, service), manholes, drains.
6. **Utility Summary** — Steam (kg/hr @ bar), CW (m³/hr), power (kW @ V/Ph/Hz), air (Nm³/hr).
7. **Surface Finish & Cleaning** — Ra value, electropolish requirement, CIP spray ball coverage.
8. **Codes & Approvals** — ASME Sec VIII Div 1, IS 2825, 3-A 01-09, FDA, FSSAI.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1: Core Equations for Equipment Specification & Plant Layout**

| **S.No** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric capacity of cylindrical tank | $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H \times \eta_f$ | m³ | Storage tank sizing |
| 2 | Heat exchanger area (LMTD) | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \times LMTD}$ | m² | PHE, SSHE sizing |
| 3 | LMTD (counter-current) | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | °C / K | Heat exchanger design |
| 4 | Pump power (brake) | $P_{brake} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_t}{\eta_{pump} \times 1000}$ | kW | Pump selection |
| 5 | Pump total head | $H_t = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{fittings} + H_{velocity}$ | m | Pump duty |
| 6 | Conveying power (screw) | $P = \dfrac{C \times L \times \rho \times F}{367}$ | kW | Screw conveyor motor |
| 7 | Steam consumption | $m_s = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}}$ | kg/s | Kettle & heater spec |
| 8 | CIP flow rate | $Q_{CIP} = v \times A_{pipe} = v \times \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | m³/hr | CIP skid pump |
| 9 | Centrifuge g-force | $G = \dfrac{(2\pi N/60)^2 \times r}{g}$ | — | Separator bowl speed |
| 10 | Floor area for equipment | $A_{floor} = L \times W + \sum A_{access}$ | m² | Plant layout footprint |
| 11 | Building column spacing | $S_c = 1.2 \times L_{max,eq}$ | m | Structural grid |
| 12 | Stack height (chimney) | $h = 14 \times \sqrt{Q_s}$ | m (Q in T/hr) | Boiler flue sizing |
| 13 | Retention time | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hr | Holding tubes, retorts |
| 14 | Headroom clearance | $H_{clear} = H_{vessel} + 1.0$ | m | Layout vertical space |
| 15 | Steam specific volume (5 bar abs) | $v_s = 0.3749$ | m³/kg | Vessel relief sizing |

**Boundary Conditions & Practical Limits:**
- $\eta_f$ (fill factor) = **0.80 to 0.90** for liquids, **0.50 to 0.60** for viscous pastes.
- $v$ (CIP velocity) ≥ **1.5 m/s**, ideally **2.0 m/s**.
- $U$ (overall heat transfer coefficient): PHE for milk pasteurization = **2000–3500 W/m²K**; SSHE for viscous food = **1000–1500 W/m²K**.
- $\eta_{pump}$ typically **0.55–0.75**.
- $C$ in screw conveyor formula = load factor (1.0 for starch, 0.6 for flour, 0.4 for whole grain).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Plant Engineering

The specification sheet directly drives the following downstream engineering deliverables:

- **General Arrangement (GA) Drawings** — equipment footprint sets column grid.
- **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)** — nozzle list defines pipe sizes.
- **Utility Distribution** — steam, water, and air headers are sized from the sum of all utility specs.
- **Civil Foundation Drawings** — operating weight dictates pedestal and pile design.
- **Electrical Single Line Diagrams (SLD)** — total connected load defines transformer KVA.
- **HACCP & Validation** — surface finish and CIP data feed the validation master plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *P&ID cannot be drawn without the nozzle schedule*, and a *nozzle schedule cannot exist without the equipment specification*. Hence, equipment specs are the **first deliverable** (longest critical-path item) in any food plant EPC project, typically locked at **30% model review** (FEL-2 stage).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Exchanger Area from LMTD (PHE for Milk Pasteurization)

### Problem Statement
A Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) is to pasteurize **20,000 L/hr of milk** from **4 °C to 72 °C** using hot water entering at **85 °C** and leaving at **75 °C** in a counter-current arrangement. Given $U = 2500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $\rho_{milk} = 1030 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_{p,milk} = 3.93 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Determine the **required heat exchange area (A)** and the **number of plates** if each plate has $A_p = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the mass flow rate of milk.**

$$Q_{milk} = 20{,}000 \text{ L/hr} = 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\dot{m}_{milk} = Q_{milk} \times \rho_{milk} = 20 \times 1030 = 20{,}600 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2: Compute the heat duty (Q).**

$$\dot{m}_{milk} = \frac{20{,}600}{3600} = 5.722 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q = \dot{m}_{milk} \times c_{p,milk} \times \Delta T = 5.722 \times 3930 \times (72 - 4)$$

$$Q = 5.722 \times 3930 \times 68$$

$$Q = 1{,}528{,}948 \text{ W} \approx 1.529 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Determine the two end-temperature differences (counter-current).**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 85 - 72 = 13 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 75 - 4 = 71 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD).**

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{13 - 71}{\ln(13 / 71)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{-58}{\ln(0.1831)} = \frac{-58}{-1.6977} \approx 34.17 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5: Apply the correction factor.**
For a 1-pass-1-pass PHE with both fluids unmixed, the LMTD correction factor $F \approx 0.96$. Using $LMTD_{corrected} \approx 32.8 \text{ °C}$.

**Step 6: Compute the heat exchange area.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \times LMTD} = \frac{1{,}528{,}948}{2500 \times 32.8}$$

$$A \approx 18.65 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7: Compute the number of plates.**

$$N_{plates} = \frac{A}{A_p} = \frac{18.65}{0.25} \approx 75 \text{ plates}$$

With 2 end-plates and considering effective plates ($N_p = 75 + 1 = 76$ total plates, with 1 hot pass and 1 cold pass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Implication:** The PHE footprint is typically 1.5 m × 0.8 m × 1.6 m (L×W×H). The plant layout must allocate **at least 1.0 m front clearance** for plate removal and **0.8 m on the connecting side** for the frame. Total floor area reserved = **2.5 m × 2.3 m = 5.75 m²** per unit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sanitary Pump Specification

### Problem Statement
A centrifugal sanitary pump is to transfer **30 m³/hr of fruit pulp** ($\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$, viscosity = 50 cP) from a receiving tank (ground level) to an evaporator **8 m above** through **40 m of 50 mm ID sanitary piping** with **6 elbows, 2 butterfly valves, and 1 strainer**. The pipe is **SS316L, sanitary finish**. Compute **(a) Total head (Hₜ)** and **(b) Brake power (P_brake)**. Use $\eta_{pump} = 0.65$ and $C = 0.0008$ in $H_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D}$ approximation with equivalent length method (Le = 1.5 m per elbow, 8 m per butterfly valve, 12 m per strainer).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the linear velocity (v).**

$$A_{pipe} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A_{pipe}} = \frac{30/3600}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.00833}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 4.245 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the equivalent pipe length.**

$$L_{eq} = L_{straight} + \sum L_{fittings}$$

$$L_{eq} = 40 + (6 \times 1.5) + (2 \times 8) + (1 \times 12)$$

$$L_{eq} = 40 + 9 + 16 + 12 = 77 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the friction head (Darcy-Weisbach, $f = 0.022$ for turbulent flow of fruit pulp at v = 4.245 m/s).**

$$H_f = \frac{4 f L_{eq} v^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 \times 0.022 \times 77 \times (4.245)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050}$$

$$H_f = \frac{4 \times 0.022 \times 77 \times 18.02}{0.981}$$

$$H_f = \frac{122.1}{0.981} \approx 124.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the static head.**

$$H_{static} = 8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Velocity head (assume both nozzles at same diameter).**

$$H_{velocity} \approx 0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Total head.**

$$H_t = H_{static} + H_f + H_{fittings} + H_{velocity}$$

$$H_t = 8 + 124.5 + 0 + 0 \approx 132.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical 50 mm sanitary centrifugal pump at 30 m³/hr delivers ~30 m head. The high head is dominated by **viscosity-induced friction**. The selection must be revised to a **lobe pump** or **progressive cavity pump** for viscous pulps.

**Step 7: Brake power.**

$$P_{brake} = \frac{\rho g Q H_t}{\eta_{pump} \times 1000} = \frac{1100 \times 9.81 \times (30/3600) \times 132.5}{0.65 \times 1000}$$

$$P_{brake} = \frac{1100 \times 9.81 \times 0.00833 \times 132.5}{650}$$

$$P_{brake} = \frac{11{,}948.6}{650} \approx 18.38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Select motor (with 1.15 service factor).**

$$P_{motor} = 1.15 \times 18.38 \approx 21.1 \text{ kW} \rightarrow \text{Use standard 22 kW motor}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Storage Tank Specification from Batch Size

### Problem Statement
A dairy plant processes **1,20,000 litres of milk per day** in **2 equal shifts of 6 hours each**. The storage tank must hold **2 hours of production** to feed the pasteurizer continuously. The tank is **vertical, cylindrical, with flat bottom and dished top**. Diameter-to-height ratio $D/H = 1.0$. Fill factor $\eta_f = 0.85$. Compute **(a) Tank volume, (b) Diameter & height, (c) Floor area reserved** (assume 1.5 m annular access space).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute hourly throughput.**

$$Q_{hr} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{12} = 10{,}000 \text{ L/hr}$$

**Step 2: Compute required holding volume.**

$$V_{gross} = Q_{hr} \times 2 \text{ hr} = 20{,}000 \text{ L} = 20 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply fill factor to get actual tank volume.**

$$V_{tank} = \frac{V_{gross}}{\eta_f} = \frac{20}{0.85} \approx 23.53 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply D = H geometry.**

$$V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times H = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times D = \frac{\pi D^3}{4}$$

$$D^3 = \frac{4V}{\pi} = \frac{4 \times 23.53}{3.1416} \approx 29.96$$

$$D \approx 3.11 \text{ m}, \quad H \approx 3.11 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compute floor area (with 1.5 m annular access).**

$$D_{plot} = 3.11 + 2(1.5) = 6.11 \text{ m}$$

$$A_{floor} = \frac{\pi (D_{plot})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (6.11)^2}{4} \approx 29.32 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6: Add headroom.**

$$H_{clear} = 3.11 + 1.0 = 4.11 \text{ m} \rightarrow \text{Building clear height} \geq 5.0 \text{ m}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Equipment Selection

| **Criterion** | **Centrifugal Pump** | **Lobe Pump** | **Positive Displacement Pump** | **Best Choice (Viscous Food)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Viscosity handling | Poor (≤200 cP) | Good (≤5000 cP) | Excellent (≤50,000 cP) | **Lobe / PD** |
| Flow rate | High | Medium | Low–Medium | **Centrifugal (low visc)** |
| Shear damage | Low | Medium | High (slip) | **Lobe (gentle)** |
| CIP compatibility | Excellent | Good | Good | **Centrifugal** |
| Capital cost | Low | High | Medium | **Centrifugal** |
| NPSH requirement | High | Low | Low | **Lobe** |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Material of Construction (MOC)" in the context of food processing equipment specifications. List the two most commonly used stainless-steel grades and their typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Material of Construction (MOC)** refers to the engineering specification of the metal, alloy, polymer, or composite used to fabricate each wetted and non-wetted part of a piece of food processing equipment. It directly influences corrosion resistance, cleanability, hygienic compliance, and capital cost.

**Two most common stainless-steel grades:**

1. **SS 304** — Used for non-contact structural parts, conveyor frames, guards, and storage silos for dry goods. Cost-effective. Contains **18% Cr, 8% Ni**.
2. **SS 316L** — Used for **all product-contact surfaces** in dairy, beverage, and aseptic plants. The "L" denotes low carbon (≤ 0.03%), preventing sensitization during welding. Contains **16–18% Cr, 10–14% Ni, 2–3% Mo**, providing superior pitting resistance against chlorides in CIP solutions.

> [3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for grades & applications]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a "3-A Sanitary Standard"? Why is it important in the specification of food processing equipment?**

**Model Answer:**

The **3-A Sanitary Standards** are a set of consensus-based hygienic design criteria developed by three U.S. organizations: the **International Association for Food Protection (IAFP)**, the **American Dairy Products Institute (ADPI)**, and the **International Association of Food Industry Suppliers (IAFIS)**. They define criteria for **material, design, fabrication, and cleanability** of equipment used in dairy, food, and beverage processing.

**Importance in equipment specification:**

- Guarantees **product-contact surfaces** meet hygienic finish requirements (Ra ≤ 0.8 μm).
- Ensures **self-draining geometry** (slope ≥ 1%, no dead legs).
- Validates **CIP effectiveness** through validated spray ball coverage.
- Acts as a **regulatory benchmark** accepted by FSSAI, USFDA, and EU regulators.
- Reduces **HACCP risk** by design.

> [3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for importance]

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **eight essential blocks** that constitute a complete specification datasheet for a food processing equipment. Give at least one example of data under each block for a **falling-film evaporator** in a fruit-juice concentrate plant.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plate heat exchanger is to heat **15,000 kg/hr of milk** (cp = 3.9 kJ/kg·K) from **6 °C to 75 °C** using hot water (cp = 4.18 kJ/kg·K) entering at **90 °C** and leaving at **70 °C**, counter-current flow. Take overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 2500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ and LMTD correction factor $F = 0.95$. Calculate the **(i) heat duty, (ii) LMTD, (iii) effective heat transfer area, and (iv) number of plates** if each plate has an effective area of 0.30 m².

---

### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

The eight essential blocks of a food processing equipment specification datasheet, with reference to a **falling-film evaporator (FFE)** rated at 5 TPH water evaporation from 12°Brix to 72°Brix juice:

1. **Header Block** — Tag No: `FFE-201`; P&ID Ref: `PID-300-001 Rev B`; Project: `ABC Fruit Juices Pvt. Ltd., 50 TPD Citrus Plant`.
2. **Process Data** — Capacity: **5,000 kg/hr water evaporation**; Inlet: 12°Brix @ 85 °C; Outlet: 72°Brix @ 60 °C; Vapour temperature: 55 °C.
3. **Mechanical Data** — Vessel: **SS316L**, shell OD **1.2 m**, total height **8.5 m**, weight empty **2.4 MT** + operating **3.1 MT**, surface finish **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**, electropolished.
4. **Internals** — Tube bundle: **316 tubes × 38 mm OD × 1.2 mm wall × 6 m long**; vapour-liquid separator; CIP spray ball; sight glass.
5. **Nozzle Schedule** — Feed inlet (DN50, DIN 11851), vapour outlet (DN200, ANSI 150#), concentrate outlet (DN50), vapour inlet (DN250), manhole DN500.
6. **Utility Summary** — Steam: **3,200 kg/hr @ 3.5 bar (g)**; Cooling water: **8 m³/hr**; Power: **7.5 kW**; Compressed air: **2 Nm³/hr @ 6 bar**.
7. **Surface Finish & Cleaning** — All wetted surfaces **electropolished to Ra ≤ 0.4 μm**; CIP flow **15 m³/hr** with spray ball; drainable geometry confirmed.
8. **Codes & Approvals** — ASME Sec VIII Div 1, IS 2825, 3-A 09-07 (evaporators), FDA 21 CFR 110, FSSAI compliant.

> [7 marks: 1 mark for each block, 0.5 for block header + 0.5 for FFE example]

---

### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{milk} = 15{,}000 / 3600 = 4.167 \text{ kg/s}$
- $c_{p,milk} = 3900 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- $\Delta T_{milk} = 75 - 6 = 69 \text{ °C}$
- Hot water: $T_{h,in} = 90 \text{ °C}, T_{h,out} = 70 \text{ °C}$

**Step (i): Heat Duty Q**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{milk} \times c_{p,milk} \times \Delta T = 4.167 \times 3900 \times 69$$

$$Q = 1{,}121{,}548 \text{ W} \approx 1.122 \text{ MW}$$

**Step (ii): LMTD**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 90 - 75 = 15 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 70 - 6 = 64 \text{ °C}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{15 - 64}{\ln(15/64)} = \frac{-49}{\ln(0.2344)} = \frac{-49}{-1.4508} \approx 33.77 \text{ °C}$$

Applying correction factor $F = 0.95$:

$$LMTD_{corrected} = 0.95 \times 33.77 = 32.08 \text{ °C}$$

**Step (iii): Heat Transfer Area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \times LMTD_{corrected}} = \frac{1{,}121{,}548}{2500 \times 32.08}$$

$$A \approx 13.98 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (iv): Number of Plates**

$$N_{plates} = \frac{A}{A_p} = \frac{13.98}{0.30} \approx 46.6 \rightarrow 47 \text{ effective plates} + 2 \text{ end plates} = 49 \text{ plates total}$$

> [7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 1.5 marks for (iii), 1.5 marks for (iv)]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **classification of food plant accessories** with suitable examples. Explain the role of **positive displacement pumps** in handling viscous food products. How does the **CIP flow velocity of 1.5 m/s** govern the design of the CIP return piping?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **steam-jacketed kettle** is to process **800 kg/batch of tomato sauce** ($\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 3.8 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$) from **25 °C to 95 °C** in **45 minutes**. Saturated steam is supplied at **3.5 bar (g)**. The kettle has a **diameter of 1.2 m** and **height of 1.5 m**, with a **fill factor of 0.70**. The **overall heat transfer coefficient** is $U = 1200 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, and the **LMTD is approximately the difference between steam temperature and average product temperature**. Calculate the **(i) effective heat transfer area required, (ii) steam consumption rate, and (iii) verify the kettle capacity**.

**Steam properties at 3.5 bar (g) = 4.5 bar abs:** $T_{sat} \approx 147.2 \text{ °C}$, $h_{fg} = 2113 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

---

### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Classification of Food Plant Accessories:**

1. **Fluid Handling Accessories:** Sanitary centrifugal pumps, lobe pumps, peristaltic pumps, mag-drive pumps.
2. **Piping & Valves:** Tri-Clover fittings, DIN 11851 unions, butterfly valves, mixproof valves, check valves.
3. **Storage Accessories:** Silos, IBC totes, aseptic bag-in-box, jacketed tanks.
4. **Utility Generation:** Boilers, chillers, cooling towers, air compressors, RO plants.
5. **Instrumentation:** RTDs, magmeters, conductivity sensors, load cells.
6. **Safety:** Pressure Relief Valves (PRV), rupture discs, flame arrestors.

**Role of Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps in Viscous Foods:**

PD pumps (lobe, gear, progressive cavity) deliver a **fixed volume per revolution**, making them ideal for **viscous, shear-sensitive foods** such as fruit pulp, cream, chocolate, and dough. They:
- Provide **constant flow** independent of pressure variations.
- Handle viscosities up to **50,000 cP** without flow loss.
- Offer **low-shear**, **gentle pumping** preserving product texture.

**CIP Flow Velocity of 1.5 m/s:**

The **1.5 m/s minimum turbulent velocity** ensures **Reynolds number > 4000**, generating the wall shear stress required to remove tenacious food soils. To maintain this velocity, the CIP return pipe diameter is sized from:

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q_{CIP}}{\pi v}} \quad \text{with} \quad v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$$

This diameter must be **uniform throughout the return loop**, with no reductions, to prevent low-velocity dead spots.

> [7 marks: 3 marks for classification, 2 marks for PD pump role, 2 marks for CIP velocity logic]

---

### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step (i): Heat Duty and Heat Transfer Area**

Average product temperature: $T_{avg} = (25 + 95)/2 = 60 \text{ °C}$

Steam saturation temperature: $T_{steam} = 147.2 \text{ °C}$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx T_{steam} - T_{avg} = 147.2 - 60 = 87.2 \text{ °C}$$

**Heat duty:**

$$Q = m c_p \Delta T = 800 \times 3800 \times (95 - 25) = 800 \times 3800 \times 70$$

$$Q = 212{,}800{,}000 \text{ J per batch}$$

Batch time: $t = 45 \text{ min} = 2700 \text{ s}$

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{212{,}800{,}000}{2700} = 78{,}815 \text{ W} \approx 78.82 \text{ kW}$$

**Heat transfer area:**

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \times \Delta T} = \frac{78{,}815}{1200 \times 87.2} = \frac{78{,}815}{104{,}640} \approx 0.753 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (ii): Steam Consumption Rate**

Latent heat of steam: $h_{fg} = 2113 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2{,}113{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{78{,}815}{2{,}113{,}000} \approx 0.0373 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.0373 \times 3600 \approx 134.3 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step (iii): Verification of Kettle Capacity**

Kettle volume: $V = \dfrac{\pi (1.2)^2}{4} \times 1.5 = 1.696 \text{ m}^3 = 1696 \text{ L}$

Product volume per batch: $V_{product} = \dfrac{800}{1050} = 0.762 \text{ m}^3 = 762 \text{ L}$

Fill factor used: $\eta_f = \dfrac{762}{1696} = 0.449$

Since 0.449 < 0.70 (specified fill factor), the kettle is **over-sized** for this batch. The actual maximum batch capacity is:

$$m_{max} = 0.70 \times 1.696 \times 1050 = 1246.6 \text{ kg}$$

Alternatively, if exactly 800 kg is required, a smaller kettle with $V = 0.762/0.70 = 1.088 \text{ m}^3$ is sufficient (e.g., D = 1.0 m, H = 1.4 m).

> [7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with detailed verification]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Equipment Specification Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit on the answer script.
> 2. **Using horizontal flow value of v for CIP** — the **1.5 m/s** is a **turbulent threshold**, not just a recommended value. Always quote the Reynolds-number justification.
> 3. **Confusing SS 304 vs SS 316L** — students often write "SS 304 for all parts." The product-contact surface **must** be 316L; 304 is for structural parts only.
> 4. **Skipping fill factor (η_f)** — examiners specifically look for the η_f application in tank sizing. A direct $V = Q \times t$ answer without η_f loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not adding 1 m headroom** — in layout questions, forgetting $H_{clear} = H_{vessel} + 1.0 \text{ m}$ is a recurring error costing 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting the LMTD correction factor (F)** — for plate heat exchangers, students forget the 0.95 correction and end up with an under-sized area.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Equipment specification is the FIRST deliverable** in a food plant EPC project, locking at 30% model review.
- ⭐ The **eight datasheet blocks**: Header, Process, Mechanical, Internals, Nozzle, Utility, Finish, Codes.
- ⭐ **SS 316L** is mandatory for product-contact surfaces; **SS 304** for non-contact structural parts.
- ⭐ Standard sanitary surface finish: **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**; for aseptic contact: **Ra ≤ 0.4 μm** (electropolished).
- ⭐ Fill factor: **0.80–0.90** for liquids, **0.50–0.60** for viscous pastes.
- ⭐ CIP return piping **must sustain v ≥ 1.5 m/s** to ensure turbulent cleaning (Re > 4000).
- ⭐ Sanitary piping slope: **1:100 (1%) minimum** for self-drainage.
- ⭐ Storage tank layout: always reserve **+1.5 m annular access** and **+1.0 m headroom**.
- ⭐ **3-A, FDA, FSSAI, ASME BPVC, IS 2825** are the five non-negotiable code families.
- ⭐ Pump selection rule of thumb: **Centrifugal for low-viscosity (<200 cP)**; **Lobe/PD for high-viscosity (>1000 cP)**.
- ⭐ Steam kettle design: **LMTD ≈ $T_{steam} - T_{avg,product}$**; **$h_{fg}$ at operating pressure** is the latent heat to use.
- ⭐ Plant layout column grid: typically **$S_c = 1.2 \times L_{max,eq}$** to accommodate the largest equipment footprint with maintenance access.
- ⭐ Always cross-verify **mass balance, energy balance, and capacity** of every equipment spec before issuing for layout freeze.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Provisions for waste disposal and safety arrangements in plant layout and design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Provisions for waste disposal and safety arrangements in plant layout and design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Waste Disposal Provisions** in food plant layout refer to the systematic engineering and architectural arrangements integrated into the plant infrastructure for the collection, segregation, treatment, storage, and final disposal of solid, liquid, and gaseous waste streams generated during food processing operations, in compliance with the **Food Safety and Standards Act (FSSAI)**, **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** norms, and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** guidelines.

**Safety Arrangements** denote the engineered and procedural provisions embedded within the plant layout to safeguard human life, equipment, product integrity, and the environment from occupational hazards, fire, electrical faults, chemical exposure, structural failure, and biological contamination, aligned with **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, **OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)** standards, and **HACCP-based** design protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every food plant layout MUST incorporate waste handling and safety provisions as **non-negotiable design parameters** — not as afterthought additions. The layout review carries 2-mark weightage on waste/safety considerations in Part A questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 – The Kitchen Sink Model:**
Think of your home kitchen. You don't throw vegetable peels into the same sink where you wash dishes, do you? You have a separate dustbin for organic waste, a sink for water, and a chimney for smoke. A food plant is essentially a **gigantic, industrial-scale kitchen** where every type of waste must have its own dedicated "exit route" — just like in your home, but with **regulatory inspectors checking every pipe and bin**.

**Analogy 2 – The Airport Security Analogy:**
Safety arrangements in a food plant work like airport security. There are **multiple layers** — entry checks (PPE for personnel), metal detectors (fire alarms), restricted zones (hazardous storage), emergency exits (evacuation routes), and trained staff (first aid responders). If you forgot even one layer, the whole system collapses.

**Analogy 3 – The Human Body Analogy:**
A plant layout is like the human body. The **waste disposal system** is the excretory system (kidneys, lungs, intestines), while the **safety system** is the immune system (skin, reflexes, antibodies). Just as the body cannot function if waste accumulates or immunity fails, a food plant cannot operate if waste isn't managed or safety is compromised.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Industry-Recognized):**
> - Effluent discharge BOD limit: **< 100 mg/L** (inland surface water)
> - Effluent discharge COD limit: **< 250 mg/L**
> - Solid waste segregation: minimum **3-stream** system (organic, recyclable, hazardous)
> - Emergency exit distance: maximum **45 meters** from any point (NBC India)
> - Fire extinguisher reach: maximum **15 meters** travel distance
> - Minimum corridor width in food plant: **1.5 meters**

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Examinations

This topic is a **favourite Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark Module 1)** question area. Examiners frequently test:
- Classification of food plant waste
- Design provisions for effluent treatment
- Fire safety requirements
- PPE specifications
- Regulatory bodies and applicable acts
- Layout features that prevent cross-contamination

Mastering this topic guarantees **easy 3-mark pickups** in Part A and forms a **foundation for Module 2** (HACCP and food safety integration).
