## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sequence of a Food Plant Feasibility Study

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Idea Identification] --> B[Pre-Feasibility Screening]
    B --> C{In-house quick check viable?}
    C -- No --> D[Drop / Re-scope Project]
    C -- Yes --> E[Detailed Feasibility Study]
    E --> F[Market Feasibility Analysis]
    E --> G[Technical Feasibility Analysis]
    E --> H[Financial Feasibility Analysis]
    E --> I[Legal Feasibility Analysis]
    E --> J[Environmental Feasibility Analysis]
    E --> K[Operational Feasibility Analysis]
    E --> L[Schedule Feasibility Analysis]
    F --> M[Integrate Inputs into TEFR]
    G --> M
    H --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compute NPV, IRR, BCR, Payback]
    N --> O{NPV > 0 AND IRR > r?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Submit Detailed Project Report DPR]
    O -- No --> Q[Re-engineer Inputs and Re-evaluate]
    P --> R[Site Selection and Plant Layout Design]
    Q --> N
    R --> S[Process Flow and Equipment Selection]
    S --> T[Detailed Engineering]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Capital Cost Breakdown for a Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Capital Investment] --> B[Fixed Capital Investment]
    A --> C[Working Capital Investment]
    B --> D[Land and Site Development]
    B --> E[Buildings and Civil Works]
    B --> F[Plant Machinery and Equipment]
    B --> G[Process Piping and Valves]
    B --> H[Electrical and Instrumentation]
    B --> I[Utilities Boiler Refrigeration ETP]
    B --> J[Storage Silos Cold Rooms Warehouses]
    B --> K[Engineering and Contingency 10 percent]
    C --> L[Raw Material Inventory]
    C --> M[Finished Goods Inventory]
    C --> N[Accounts Receivable]
    C --> O[Cash and Bank Balance]
```

## 4.3 Decision-Tree Schematic — Go / No-Go Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Feasibility Inputs] --> P1{Market Demand Positive?}
    P1 -- No --> X1[Reject Project]
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Technology Available?}
    P2 -- No --> X2[Reject Project]
    P2 -- Yes --> P3{NPV Greater than Zero?}
    P3 -- No --> X3[Reject or Re-scope]
    P3 -- Yes --> P4{IRR Greater than Cost of Capital?}
    P4 -- No --> X4[Reject Project]
    P4 -- Yes --> P5{Legal Approvals Obtainable?}
    P5 -- No --> X5[Reject Project]
    P5 -- Yes --> P6{Environmental Clearance Possible?}
    P6 -- No --> X6[Reject Project]
    P6 -- Yes --> P7{Schedule Realistic?}
    P7 -- No --> X7[Reject Project]
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Approve — Proceed to DPR and Layout Design]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Information Flow During Feasibility

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Market Survey Team] -->|Demand Data| F[Feasibility Coordination Cell]
    B[Process Engineering Team] -->|Technology Options| F
    C[Cost Estimation Team] -->|Capex Opex Estimates| F
    D[Legal and Compliance Team] -->|Regulatory Matrix| F
    E[Environmental Team] -->|EIA Effluent Data| F
    F --> G[Techno-Economic Feasibility Report]
    G --> H[Management Review Board]
    H --> I{Approve?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Project Sanction and DPR Initiation]
    I -- No --> K[Revise Scope and Re-Submit]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a feasibility study. List any four types of feasibility analysis relevant to a food processing plant.**

**Model Answer:**

A **feasibility study** is a systematic, evidence-based evaluation of a proposed project to determine whether it is *technically viable, financially sound, legally compliant, environmentally sustainable, operationally executable*, and *socially acceptable* before significant capital is committed.

In food-plant design, the four principal types of feasibility are:

1. **Market Feasibility** — assesses demand, competition, pricing, and distribution.
2. **Technical Feasibility** — confirms process technology, capacity, utilities, and raw material availability.
3. **Economic / Financial Feasibility** — establishes profitability via NPV, IRR, BCR, and payback.
4. **Legal Feasibility** — verifies FSSAI, Factory, Pollution Control Board, and BIS compliance.

*(Other acceptable types: Environmental, Operational, Schedule)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the formula for Net Present Value (NPV). What is the decision rule?**

**Model Answer:**

**Formula:**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^{t}} - I_0$$

where $CF_t$ is the cash flow in year $t$, $r$ is the discount rate (cost of capital), $n$ is the project life in years, and $I_0$ is the initial investment at $t=0$.

**Decision Rule:**
- If $NPV > 0$: **Accept** the project.
- If $NPV < 0$: **Reject** the project.
- If $NPV = 0$: indifference — accept only for strategic reasons.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **constituents (components) of a feasibility study** for a proposed food processing plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed ready-to-eat (RTE) food plant requires an initial investment of **₹5 crore**. It is expected to generate a uniform net cash inflow of **₹1.20 crore per annum** for the next **6 years**. The cost of capital is **12% per annum**. Compute the **NPV** and state whether the project should be accepted. Use the Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF) table. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Model Answer:

A food-plant feasibility study comprises **seven components**:

1. **Market Feasibility** — demand forecasting, competitor study, price-volume analysis, distribution channel mapping, and export potential.
2. **Technical Feasibility** — process technology selection (batch/semi-continuous/continuous), plant capacity sizing (TPD), raw material seasonality, utility requirement (water, steam, power, refrigeration), effluent characterization, and skilled-manpower availability.
3. **Economic / Financial Feasibility** — total capital investment, production cost, revenue projection, profitability (NPV, IRR, BCR, payback, ROI), break-even analysis, sensitivity analysis.
4. **Legal Feasibility** — FSSAI licence, factory plan approval, Pollution Control Board consent (CTE & CTO), environmental clearance (if applicable), BIS/Agmark/FPO certifications, fire NOC.
5. **Environmental Feasibility** — EIA, ETP/STP design, solid-waste utilisation, carbon footprint, biodiversity impact.
6. **Operational / Managerial Feasibility** — organisation structure, recruitment plan, training schedule, HACCP / ISO 22000 / BRC readiness, maintenance philosophy.
7. **Schedule (Time) Feasibility** — Gantt chart, critical path, equipment lead time, regulatory approval timelines, construction seasonality.

The integrated outputs are documented in a **Techno-Economic Feasibility Report (TEFR)** which forms the basis for the Detailed Project Report (DPR) and subsequent plant layout design.

#### Solution to (b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Initial investment $I_0$ = ₹5,00,00,000 (₹5 crore)
- Annual net cash flow $R$ = ₹1,20,00,000 (₹1.20 crore)
- Project life $n$ = 6 years
- Cost of capital $r$ = 12% = 0.12

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant PVAF.**
For $r = 12\%$, $n = 6$:

$$PVAF(12\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-6}}{0.12}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(1.12)^6$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.404928 \\
(1.12)^6 &= (1.404928)^2 \approx 1.973823 \\
(1.12)^{-6} &\approx 0.506631
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PVAF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVAF(12\%, 6) &= \frac{1 - 0.506631}{0.12} \\
&= \frac{0.493369}{0.12} \\
&\approx 4.1114
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Present Value of Cash Inflows.**

$$PV_{inflow} = R \times PVAF = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 4.1114 = ₹4{,}93{,}36{,}800$$

**Step 5 — Compute NPV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{inflow} - I_0 \\
&= 4{,}93{,}36{,}800 - 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\
&= -₹6{,}63{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**
Since $NPV = -₹6{,}63{,}200 < 0$, the project is **NOT acceptable** at the 12% cost of capital. The proposal may be **revised** by either:
- reducing capital cost,
- increasing cash inflow via better pricing / capacity utilisation,
- extending project life, or
- negotiating a lower cost of capital.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **objectives and significance** of a feasibility study in food plant layout and design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed fruit juice concentrate plant has the following data: **Fixed Cost = ₹60 lakh/year, Variable Cost = ₹18 per litre, Selling Price = ₹45 per litre.** Determine the **annual break-even quantity** in litres and the **break-even sales revenue**. If the plant operates at **70% of its installed capacity of 25 lakh litres/year**, compute the annual profit. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Model Answer:

**Objectives:**
1. Confirm technical viability of producing the food product at desired capacity.
2. Quantify total capital and operating cost.
3. Forecast revenues and profitability over plant life.
4. Identify legal and regulatory requirements (FSSAI, PCB, Factory Act).
5. Identify and quantify risks (raw material, market, effluent, energy).
6. Recommend optimal location, capacity, and technology.
7. Provide a structured Go / No-Go decision.

**Significance:**
- Prevents capital wastage on unviable projects.
- Reduces investment risk via quantified metrics (NPV, IRR).
- Acts as a prerequisite for bank loan sanction and MoFPI subsidies.
- Establishes a defensible basis for plant capacity, layout zoning, and equipment selection.
- Identifies environmental and social constraints before site selection.
- Documents management intent for statutory approvals.
- Improves design quality — all downstream layout decisions reference the feasibility baseline.

#### Solution to (b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Fixed Cost $F$ = ₹60,00,000 per year
- Variable Cost $V$ = ₹18 per litre
- Selling Price $P$ = ₹45 per litre
- Installed Capacity = 25,00,000 litres/year

**Step 1 — Break-even Quantity $Q_{BE}$.**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - V} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{45 - 18} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{27} \approx 2{,}22{,}222 \text{ litres/year}$$

**Step 2 — Break-even Sales Revenue $S_{BE}$.**

$$S_{BE} = P \times Q_{BE} = 45 \times 2{,}22{,}222 = ₹1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \approx ₹1 \text{ crore/year}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Production at 70% Capacity Utilisation.**

$$Q_{actual} = 0.70 \times 25{,}00{,}000 = 17{,}50{,}000 \text{ litres/year}$$

**Step 4 — Total Revenue at Actual Production.**

$$TR = P \times Q_{actual} = 45 \times 17{,}50{,}000 = ₹7{,}87{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Total Cost at Actual Production.**

$$TC = F + V \times Q_{actual} = 60{,}00{,}000 + 18 \times 17{,}50{,}000 = 60{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}15{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}75{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Annual Profit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= TR - TC \\
&= 7{,}87{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹4{,}12{,}50{,}000 \approx ₹4.125 \text{ crore/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
The plant comfortably exceeds its break-even point (2.22 lakh L) with a comfortable margin (actual production 17.5 lakh L), and yields an annual profit of **₹4.125 crore** at 70% capacity utilisation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Formula-only answer without interpretation:** Students often write $NPV = \sum CF_t/(1+r)^t - I_0$ but forget to state the decision rule ($NPV > 0$ ⇒ Accept). Examiners award only 1 out of 2 marks for incomplete answers.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in break-even:** Mixing ₹ per day with litres per year causes catastrophic numerical errors. Always reconcile units **before** substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting Working Capital:** Initial investment $I_0$ in NPV problems *must* include working capital (typically 10–15% of fixed capital for food plants). Students frequently omit this and inflate NPV erroneously.
> 4. **Confusing Payback Period with Discounted Payback Period:** The simple payback ignores the time value of money; KTU values explicit awareness of this difference.
> 5. **Single-criterion decisions:** Accepting a project *only* because NPV > 0 (without confirming IRR > cost of capital and BCR > 1) is treated as incomplete feasibility analysis. **Always triangulate with at least two metrics.**
> 6. **Omitting sensitivity analysis:** A feasibility report that does not test ±10–20% variation in raw material price, selling price, or capacity utilisation is marked down for missing real-world risk assessment.
> 7. **Generic feasibility answer:** Lists applicable to any industry fail to score. Use **food-industry-specific** terminology — *FSSAI, ETP capacity, cold chain, shelf-life, HACCP, BIS* — to secure full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Introduction to Feasibility Study and Analysis**

- **Definition:** A feasibility study is a pre-investment, evidence-based evaluation to determine project viability *before* layout design.
- **Seven Components:** Market, Technical, Economic/Financial, Legal, Environmental, Operational, Schedule.
- **Placement in Life Cycle:** Idea → Feasibility → TEFR → DPR → Site → Layout → Engineering → Commissioning.
- **Core Financial Metrics:** NPV, IRR, BCR, Payback Period, ROI, Break-Even Quantity, Break-Even Sales.
- **NPV Formula:** $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$. **Accept if $NPV > 0$.**
- **IRR Rule:** Solve $NPV(r) = 0$ for $r$. **Accept if $IRR > r$ (cost of capital).** Food plants typically require $IRR \ge 15\%$.
- **BCR:** Discounted benefits ÷ discounted costs. **Accept if $BCR > 1$.**
- **Break-Even:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$. Output above this yields profit at contribution margin $(P - V)$ per unit.
- **Food-Specific Considerations:** FSSAI compliance, Pollution Control Board consent, effluent treatment, cold-chain logistics, HACCP/ISO 22000, raw material perishability, seasonal capacity utilisation.
- **Mandatory Subsidy / Loan Documentation:** Bank loan sanction and MoFPI subsidy claims require a TEFR based on a thorough feasibility study.
- **Sensitivity Analysis:** Always test ±10–20% variation in raw material cost, selling price, and capacity utilisation — KTU examiners expect this discussion.
- **Multi-criterion Decision Rule:** A project must satisfy NPV > 0 *AND* IRR > r *AND* BCR > 1 *AND* Payback < target horizon — never rely on a single metric.
- **Go / No-Go Outcome:** The study produces a binary managerial decision; a negative result is a *valid* and value-adding outcome.
- **PVAF Shortcut:** For uniform cash flows, use $PVAF(r, n) = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ — multiply by annual $R$ for present value.
- **Time Value of Money:** Always discount future cash flows; never compare ₹1 lakh today with ₹1 lakh ten years hence as equivalent.
- **Capital Cost Inclusion:** $I_0$ must include land, building, machinery, utilities, ETP, working capital, and contingency (typically 10% of fixed capital).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout and design aspects of pilot and semi-commercial food processing plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layout and design aspects of pilot and sub-commercial food processing plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pilot Plant** is a small-scale industrial system designed to reproduce the technical and economic performance of a proposed full-scale commercial process before final commitment of capital. In food engineering, it serves as an intermediate experimental stage between laboratory bench-scale research (typically 1–10 kg batch) and full industrial production (typically 1,000–100,000 kg batch).

A **Semi-Commercial Plant** is an intermediate-scale facility operating at roughly **5%–25%** of the proposed commercial capacity. It is used to validate product quality, market acceptance, and process economics under near-industrial conditions while minimizing capital exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the *transitional role* of pilot and semi-commercial plants in the **food product development life cycle (PDLC)** — bridging R&D with commercialization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine learning to drive a car:

- **Laboratory scale** = Practicing on a video game simulator (no real consequences, limited fidelity).
- **Pilot plant** = Driving in an empty parking lot with an instructor (real vehicle, controlled environment, focused on individual skills like steering, braking, gear-shifting).
- **Semi-commercial plant** = Driving on quiet residential streets (real traffic, real signals, but lower stakes than a highway).
- **Commercial plant** = Driving full-speed on a national highway with cargo (full optimization, maximum efficiency, zero tolerance for errors).

The pilot plant is where you discover that your "steering" (process parameters) actually works on the real machine, while the semi-commercial plant confirms the system works in real-world conditions before full-scale commitment.

### 1.3 Core Definitions in Food Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term:** **Scale-up Factor (S)**
> The ratio of production capacity of the larger system to that of the smaller reference system. For pilot-to-semi-commercial, $S$ typically ranges from **4 to 20**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term:** **Specific Capital Investment (SCI)**
> Capital invested per unit of installed production capacity. Standard industry benchmark: **$1,000–$5,000 per installed ton/year** of finished product for food processing.

### 1.4 Classification of Plant Scales in Food Industry

| Plant Type | Capacity Range | Batch Size | Primary Purpose |
|------------|----------------|------------|-----------------|
| Laboratory | < 50 kg/day | 1–10 kg | R&D, formulation |
| **Pilot Plant** | **50–500 kg/day** | **10–100 kg** | **Process validation** |
| **Semi-Commercial** | **0.5–5 tonnes/day** | **100–1000 kg** | **Market trial, scale-up** |
| Commercial | > 5 tonnes/day | > 1 tonne | Full production |

> [!TIP]
> Always state capacity in **kg/day** or **tonnes/year** for food plants — KTU examiners look for specific units in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Objectives of a Pilot Plant

The design rationale rests on **four engineering pillars**:

1. **Process Verification** — Confirm that laboratory-derived parameters (temperature, pressure, residence time, shear rates) translate predictably to larger equipment geometries.
2. **Data Generation** — Produce engineering data (mass/energy balances, kinetic constants, yield coefficients) needed for final commercial design.
3. **Risk Mitigation** — Identify bottlenecks, fouling tendencies, sensory defects, and microbiological risks before committing capital.
4. **Product Refinement** — Produce sample quantities for shelf-life studies, consumer trials, and regulatory submissions.

### 2.2 Design Philosophy: The "Miniature Factory" Principle

A pilot plant is **not** a scaled-down commercial plant. It is a **functionally complete** unit capable of producing the *same product* with *same quality* but at lower throughput. The geometry may differ (e.g., batch instead of continuous), but the *unit operations must be identical*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Similarity:** Successful scale-up demands preservation of one of these dimensionless groups — **Reynolds number (Re)**, **Froude number (Fr)**, **Weber number (We)**, **Prandtl number (Pr)**, or **Power number (Po)** — between scales.

### 2.3 Semi-Commercial Plant: The Bridge Concept

Semi-commercial plants operate at the **intersection of process engineering and business strategy**. Their design must:

- Use **commercial-grade equipment** (not lab apparatus)
- Mimic **commercial logistics** (raw material receiving, packaging, storage)
- Generate **realistic cost data** (utility consumption, labor, waste)
- Allow **continuous operation** over weeks/months (not just hours/days)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| **Scale-up Factor** | $S = \dfrac{Q_{\text{large}}}{Q_{\text{small}}}$ | dimensionless | Capacity ratio between scales |
| **Geometric Similarity Ratio** | $R_L = \dfrac{L_{\text{large}}}{L_{\text{small}}} = S^{1/3}$ | dimensionless | Linear dimension scaling (volume ∝ $L^3$) |
| **Power Consumption Scale-up** | $P_{\text{large}} = P_{\text{small}} \cdot S \cdot \rho_{\text{ratio}}$ | kW | Agitator, pump, motor sizing |
| **Reynolds Number Preservation** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow similarity |
| **Volumetric Flow Scale-up** | $\dot{V}_{\text{large}} = \dot{V}_{\text{small}} \cdot S$ | m³/h | Pump and pipe sizing |
| **Heat Transfer Area** | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}}$ | m² | Heat exchanger sizing |
| **LMTD** | $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | °C or K | Counter/parallel flow |
| **Specific Capital Investment** | $SCI = \dfrac{\text{Total Capital}}{\text{Annual Capacity}}$ | ₹/tonne·year | Economic feasibility |
| **Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Capital}}{\text{Annual Net Profit}}$ | years | Investment recovery |
| **Capacity Utilization** | $CU = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Installed Capacity}} \times 100$ | % | Operational efficiency |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless | Performance metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **never used inside tables**; instead use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in inline math.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, pilot plants are indispensable for:

- **Novel Food Development** — plant-based proteins, alternative dairy, functional beverages.
- **Process Intensification** — pulsed electric fields, high-pressure processing, ohmic heating.
- **Regulatory Approval** — FDA/EFSA require pilot-scale data before commercial approval.
- **Shelf-life Studies** — accelerated and real-time stability testing requires consistent pilot batches.

### 2.6 Decision Framework for Plant Scale Selection

The selection of pilot vs. semi-commercial vs. commercial depends on:

$$
\text{Decision Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{Risk} + w_2 \cdot \text{Capital} + w_3 \cdot \text{Time-to-Market} + w_4 \cdot \text{Product Complexity}
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ and each $w_i \geq 0$.

- **High Risk + High Capital** → Pilot → Semi-Commercial → Commercial (mandatory sequential progression).
- **Low Risk + Low Capital** → Pilot → Commercial (skip semi-commercial).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Geometric Scale-up Using Cubic Law

For a pilot plant with linear dimension $L_p$ producing volumetric output $\dot{V}_p$, the corresponding commercial plant linear dimension $L_c$ for output $\dot{V}_c$ is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** Establish proportionality between volume and linear dimension cubed.

$$
\dot{V} \propto L^3
$$

This holds because volume of any geometrically similar vessel scales with the cube of its characteristic length.

**Step 2:** Take the ratio of large to small scale.

$$
\dfrac{\dot{V}_c}{\dot{V}_p} = \left(\dfrac{L_c}{L_p}\right)^3
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $S = \dot{V}_c / \dot{V}_p$ (the scale-up factor).

$$
S = \left(\dfrac{L_c}{L_p}\right)^3
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the linear scale ratio $R_L$.

$$
R_L = \dfrac{L_c}{L_p} = S^{1/3}
$$

**Step 5:** Cross-sectional area scales as the square.

$$
R_A = \left(\dfrac{L_c}{L_p}\right)^2 = S^{2/3}
$$

**Conclusion:** When capacity increases by a factor of $S=8$, linear dimensions increase only by a factor of **2**, and areas by a factor of **4**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Consumption for Stirred Vessels (Constant Power Number)

For geometrically similar agitated tanks, the Power number $N_p$ is constant at equal Reynolds numbers.

**Step 1:** Power number definition.

$$
N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}
$$

where $P$ is power (W), $\rho$ is fluid density (kg/m³), $N$ is impeller speed (rev/s), and $D$ is impeller diameter (m).

**Step 2:** For constant $N_p$ and equal $Re$ (similar flow regime):

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_p} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_c}{N_p}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_c}{D_p}\right)^5
$$

**Step 3:** Assuming equal fluid (food product, e.g., milk, juice, sauce):

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = \left(\dfrac{N_c}{N_p}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_c}{D_p}\right)^5
$$

**Step 4:** For equal tip speed ($\pi N D$ constant):

$$
\dfrac{N_c}{N_p} = \dfrac{D_p}{D_c} = \dfrac{1}{R_L} = S^{-1/3}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into power ratio.

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = \left(S^{-1/3}\right)^3 \cdot \left(R_L\right)^5 = S^{-1} \cdot S^{5/3} = S^{2/3}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $S = 27$ (capacity scaled by 27×), then:

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = 27^{2/3} = (3^3)^{2/3} = 3^2 = 9
$$

So power consumption scales by only **9×** when capacity scales by **27×**. This non-linear advantage is why larger plants have lower specific energy costs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Parameters Comparison

| Design Parameter | Pilot Plant | Semi-Commercial Plant | Commercial Plant |
|------------------|-------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Investment Risk** | Low (₹10–50 lakh) | Medium (₹50 lakh–5 cr) | High (>₹5 cr) |
| **Equipment Type** | Lab-grade, modified | Industrial-grade, smaller | Industrial, optimized |
| **Operation Mode** | Batch (flexible) | Batch/Semi-continuous | Continuous preferred |
| **Instrumentation** | High (sensors, data loggers) | Moderate (selected sensors) | Process control (PLC/SCADA) |
| **Operator Skill** | Highly skilled (R&D team) | Skilled technicians | Trained operators |
| **Data Output** | Extensive (kinetics, mass balance) | Process economics + product | Operational metrics |
| **Run Duration** | Hours to days | Days to weeks | Months to years |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Basic (GLP) | Intermediate (HACCP ready) | Full (FSSAI, ISO, HACCP) |
| **Floor Area** | 50–200 m² | 200–1000 m² | >1000 m² |
| **Ceiling Height** | 3.5–4.5 m | 5–6 m | 6–9 m |

### 3.4 Analytical Calculation: Heat Exchanger Area Scale-up

For a pasteurizer scaling from pilot (50 L/h) to semi-commercial (500 L/h) of milk:

**Given:**
- Pilot: $\dot{V}_p = 50$ L/h, $A_p = 0.5$ m², $U_p = 1500$ W/(m²·K)
- Semi-commercial: $\dot{V}_c = 500$ L/h
- Inlet temperatures: hot fluid 90°C → 80°C, cold fluid 20°C → 75°C
- Assume same $U_c = 1500$ W/(m²·K) (clean milk, same fouling assumption)

**Step 1:** Calculate scale-up factor.

$$
S = \dfrac{500}{50} = 10
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate pilot heat duty.

$$
\Delta T_{1,p} = 90 - 20 = 70°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_{2,p} = 80 - 75 = 5°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_{LMTD,p} = \dfrac{70 - 5}{\ln(70/5)} = \dfrac{65}{\ln(14)} = \dfrac{65}{2.639} = 24.63°C
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate pilot heat duty.

$$
Q_p = U_p \cdot A_p \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD,p} = 1500 \times 0.5 \times 24.63 = 18,472 \text{ W} \approx 18.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Assume duty scales linearly with flow.

$$
Q_c = Q_p \times S = 18,472 \times 10 = 184,720 \text{ W} \approx 184.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate LMTD for counter-flow commercial unit.

$$
\Delta T_{1,c} = 90 - 75 = 15°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_{2,c} = 80 - 20 = 60°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_{LMTD,c} = \dfrac{60 - 15}{\ln(60/15)} = \dfrac{45}{\ln(4)} = \dfrac{45}{1.386} = 32.46°C
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for required area.

$$
A_c = \dfrac{Q_c}{U_c \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD,c}} = \dfrac{184,720}{1500 \times 32.46} = \dfrac{184,720}{48,690} = 3.79 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 7:** Compare with naive scaling ($A_c = A_p \cdot S$).

$$
A_{c,\text{naive}} = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** Smart LMTD optimization reduces required area by **24.2%**, saving capital. This is why layout and design must integrate thermal analysis, not just volumetric scaling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a pilot plant. List any four objectives of setting up a pilot plant in the food industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pilot plant is a small-scale processing facility that operates between laboratory research and full commercial production to validate process feasibility, generate engineering data, and produce sample quantities for testing.

**Four Objectives:**

1. **Process Verification** – Confirm laboratory parameters scale up reliably (1 mark)
2. **Engineering Data Generation** – Produce mass balance, energy balance, kinetic data for commercial design (1 mark)
3. **Product Quality Validation** – Generate samples for sensory, shelf-life, and consumer testing (0.5 marks)
4. **Risk Mitigation** – Identify technical, economic, and operational risks before major capital investment (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between a pilot plant and a semi-commercial plant (any four points).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pilot Plant | Semi-Commercial Plant |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Capacity** | 50–500 kg/day | 0.5–5 tonnes/day |
| **Purpose** | Process validation, R&D | Market trial, economic validation |
| **Equipment** | Lab-grade, modified | Industrial-grade, smaller scale |
| **Operation** | Batch, intermittent | Batch to semi-continuous |
| **Data Output** | Kinetic and process data | Economic + quality + process data |
| **Run Duration** | Hours to days | Days to weeks |

*(Any four contrasting points carry 0.75 marks each)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **layout and design considerations** for setting up a pilot food processing plant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **scale-up principles** used in transitioning from pilot to semi-commercial scale, with a suitable numerical example illustrating geometric similarity. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**1. Space and Layout Planning (2 marks)**

A pilot plant should occupy **100–300 m²** of process area with ceiling height **4–5 m**. Layout must follow a **linear or U-shaped flow** to minimize material handling. Separate zones for:
- Raw material receipt
- Pre-processing
- Core processing
- Packaging
- QC laboratory
- Utility room (steam, water, compressed air, refrigeration)
- Effluent/waste collection

**2. Equipment Selection (2 marks)**

- Equipment should be **multi-purpose** (e.g., a single reactor usable for sauce, beverage, or puree trials).
- Material of construction: **SS 304** for general food contact, **SS 316** for acidic/corrosive products.
- Capacity: typically **10–20% of proposed commercial unit**.
- Standard sanitary fittings (tri-clamp, DIN) for easy cleaning (CIP compatibility).

**3. Flexibility and Modularity (1.5 marks)**

- Use **mobile/skid-mounted equipment** that can be repositioned.
- Provide **multiple utility connections** at floor/wall points for quick equipment changeover.
- Install **movable partition walls** to reconfigure zones as per product trials.

**4. Instrumentation and Control (1 mark)**

- Fit **sensors for temperature, pressure, flow, pH, conductivity, Brix** at strategic points.
- Central **data acquisition system** for recording kinetics.
- Manual valves are acceptable at pilot scale; full automation is unnecessary.

**5. Safety, Hygiene, and Regulatory (0.5 marks)**

- **HACCP-ready** design with zoning (high-care/low-care).
- Emergency exits, fire suppression, eye-wash stations.
- **FSSAI-compliant** construction and finishes (epoxy flooring, SS wall cladding in wet zones).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Geometric Similarity Scale-up – Numerical Example (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Pilot mixer: impeller diameter $D_p = 0.2$ m, motor power $P_p = 1.5$ kW, vessel volume $V_p = 100$ L
- Required scale-up factor $S = 8$
- Fluid: fruit juice, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³ (assume constant)

**Step 1: Calculate large-scale impeller diameter (1 mark)**

$$
D_c = D_p \cdot S^{1/3} = 0.2 \times 8^{1/3} = 0.2 \times 2 = 0.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Determine impeller speed for equal tip speed (1 mark)**

For geometrically similar mixers with equal tip speed ($\pi N D$ constant):

$$
N_c = N_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_p}{D_c}\right) = N_p \cdot S^{-1/3}
$$

If pilot $N_p = 100$ rpm $= 1.667$ rev/s:

$$
N_c = 1.667 \times 8^{-1/3} = 1.667 \times 0.5 = 0.833 \text{ rev/s} = 50 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate power consumption at large scale (2 marks)**

Power scales as $P \propto \rho N^3 D^5$:

$$
\dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = \left(\dfrac{N_c}{N_p}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_c}{D_p}\right)^5
$$
$$
\dfrac{P_c}{P_p} = (0.5)^3 \cdot (2)^5 = 0.125 \times 32 = 4
$$
$$
P_c = 1.5 \times 4 = 6.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Verify power per unit volume (1 mark)**

- Pilot: $P_p/V_p = 1500/100 = 15$ W/L
- Commercial: $P_c/V_c = 6000/800 = 7.5$ W/L

Specific power decreases by 50% at larger scale — a known advantage of scale-up.

**Step 5: Discuss Reynolds number preservation (1 mark)**

For equal $Re$ at both scales:

$$
Re_p = \dfrac{\rho N_p D_p^2}{\mu} = Re_c
$$

Solving:

$$
\dfrac{N_c}{N_p} = \left(\dfrac{D_p}{D_c}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 0.25
$$

This gives $N_c = 25$ rpm (different from tip-speed criterion). **Therefore, scale-up must specify which similarity criterion is preserved** — constant tip speed or constant Reynolds number — as they yield different design outcomes.

**Step 6: Conclude with practical recommendation (1 mark)**

For food mixing (heat-sensitive products like fruit purees, dairy), **constant tip speed** is preferred because it controls shear rate and prevents product damage. For viscous mixing (dough, heavy pastes), **constant power per unit volume** is the criterion.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **typical layout of a semi-commercial dairy plant** producing paneer and yogurt. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **specific capital investment (SCI)** and **payback period** for a proposed semi-commercial fruit juice plant with the following data, and comment on its economic viability. *(7 marks)*

**Data:**
- Installed capacity: 2,000 litres/day of packed juice
- Annual working days: 300
- Total fixed capital investment: ₹80 lakh
- Annual operating cost: ₹35 lakh
- Selling price: ₹40 per litre
- Salvage value: ₹8 lakh
- Project life: 10 years

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks) + Explanation (4 marks)**

A semi-commercial dairy plant for paneer and yogurt would have the following zones arranged in a **U-shaped flow**:

1. **Raw Milk Reception Bay** — Insulated SS tanker unloading, weighing, sampling, and rapid cooling to 4°C.
2. **Storage Silo / Cold Room** — Bulk milk storage at 4°C with agitator, capacity 5,000 L.
3. **Standardization & Pasteurization Section** — Cream separator, plate heat exchanger (HTST 72°C/15s), homogenizer.
4. **Paneer Processing Line** — Coagulation tank (with steam injection for 80°C), whey drainage, hoops/press, cooling tunnel.
5. **Yogurt Processing Line** — Separate fermentation tanks (jacket-cooled), inoculation station, incubation room (42°C), cold storage (4°C) for post-fermentation.
6. **Packaging Hall** — Cup-filling machine (yogurt), vacuum/pouch packaging (paneer), coding, metal detection.
7. **Cold Storage** — Finished goods at 4°C, capacity 2 days' production.
8. **QC Laboratory** — Located adjacent to processing for rapid sampling.
9. **Utility Block** — Boiler, chilled water plant, CIP system, compressed air, electrical room.
10. **Effluent Treatment Plant** — Separate downwind zone for whey and wash-water management.

**Design Principles Followed:**

- **Forward flow** with no cross-contamination between raw and pasteurized zones.
- **Hygienic zoning** with SS-clad walls in wet zones, epoxy floors with 1:100 slope to drains.
- **CIP loop** connecting all product contact equipment.
- **Separate personnel and material entry** to maintain biosecurity.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Capacity: $C = 2000$ L/day
- Working days: $D = 300$ days/year
- Fixed capital: $FC = ₹80,00,000$
- Annual operating cost: $OC = ₹35,00,000$
- Selling price: $SP = ₹40$/L
- Salvage value: $SV = ₹8,00,000$
- Project life: $n = 10$ years

**Step 1: Calculate annual production (1 mark)**

$$
Q_{\text{annual}} = 2000 \times 300 = 6,00,000 \text{ L/year}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate annual revenue (1 mark)**

$$
R = 6,00,000 \times 40 = ₹2,40,00,000
$$

**Step 3: Calculate annual net profit (ignoring taxes and depreciation for simplicity) (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Net Profit} = R - OC = 2,40,00,000 - 35,00,000 = ₹2,05,00,000
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Specific Capital Investment (SCI) (1.5 marks)**

$$
SCI = \dfrac{FC}{Q_{\text{annual}}} = \dfrac{80,00,000}{6,00,000} = ₹13.33/\text{L·year}
$$

Converting to ₹/tonne·year (1 tonne = 1000 L):

$$
SCI = 13.33 \times 1000 = ₹13,333/\text{tonne·year}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Payback Period (1.5 marks)**

$$
PB = \dfrac{FC}{\text{Annual Net Profit}} = \dfrac{80,00,000}{2,05,00,000} = 0.39 \text{ years} \approx 4.7 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 6: Comment on Economic Viability (1 mark)**

A payback period of less than **6 months** is exceptionally attractive for any food processing investment. The SCI of ₹13,333/tonne·year is well within the typical food industry range of ₹10,000–₹50,000/tonne·year. The project is **highly viable** and recommended for execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit mismatch:** Mixing ₹/L with ₹/tonne will cost 0.5 marks. Always convert to consistent units.
> - **Neglecting depreciation:** Real KTU problems may include depreciation. Add straight-line depreciation $D = (FC - SV)/n = (80 - 8)/10 = ₹7.2$ lakh/year, then add to operating cost or subtract as expense.
> - **Working capital:** Often ignored; if mentioned, add 10–15% of FC as working capital to total investment.
> - **Forgetting annual capacity:** Some students compute SCI per day instead of per year — read carefully.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Pilot plant** capacity = **50–500 kg/day**; **Semi-commercial** = **0.5–5 tonnes/day**.
- ✅ Pilot plant objective = **process validation**; Semi-commercial objective = **market + economic validation**.
- ✅ Scale-up preserves **one dimensionless number** (Re, Fr, We, Pr, or Po) — not all simultaneously.
- ✅ Linear dimensions scale as $S^{1/3}$, areas as $S^{2/3}$, volumes as $S$.
- ✅ Power for geometrically similar mixers scales as $S^{2/3}$ (with equal tip speed), giving **economy of scale**.
- ✅ Pilot plant floor area = **50–200 m²**; Semi-commercial = **200–1000 m²**.
- ✅ Material of construction = **SS 304** (general), **SS 316** (acidic/salty foods).
- ✅ Ceiling height: Pilot **3.5–4.5 m**, Commercial **6–9 m**.
- ✅ Always mention **HACCP zoning** and **CIP compatibility** in design answers.
- ✅ Formula: $SCI = \dfrac{\text{Total Capital}}{\text{Annual Capacity}}$ in ₹/tonne·year.
- ✅ Payback Period $PB = \dfrac{FC}{\text{Annual Net Profit}}$ (years).
- ✅ Operating mode progression: **Batch (pilot) → Batch/Semi-continuous (semi-commercial) → Continuous (commercial)**.
- ✅ Utility block (steam, water, air, refrigeration) must be **centrally located** for cost-efficient distribution.
- ✅ Pilot plants need **extensive instrumentation**; commercial plants need **automation** (PLC/SCADA).
- ✅ Always justify plant scale selection with **risk + capital + time-to-market + product complexity** criteria.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** In the KTU exam, if asked to "design a pilot plant for X", always structure your answer under these headings: **Capacity Definition → Flow Diagram → Equipment List → Layout Sketch → Utility Requirements → Safety/Hygiene → Economic Justification**. This 7-point structure aligns with KTU's outcome-based marking scheme and fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reference to the design of bakery and biscuit, fruits, vegetable, and beverage processing, and dairy industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Reference to the design of bakery and biscuit, fruits, vegetable, and beverage processing, and dairy industries # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Reference Design" of Food Processing Plants? In the context of KTU's PEFTT861 course, the **Reference Design** of a food processing plant means the *standardized blueprint approach* used by food engineers to plan, lay out, and equip a factory dedicated to a specific product category (bakery, fruits, vegetables, beverages, or dairy). It is essentially a **template** that captures the recommended building footprint, equipment arrangement, material flow pattern, utility demands, hygiene zoning, and effluent handling arrangement for each industry segment. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Reference to the design of a food plant* implies the systematic application of unit operations, building services, sanitary design principles (HACCP/ISO 22000/FSSAI compliance), and process flow analysis to translate a product formulation into a functional, GMP-compliant manufacturing facility. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of designing a food plant like **designing a hospital**. Just as a hospital has strict zoning (operation theatre ≠ general ward ≠ canteen), a food plant has zoning based on contamination risk — *high-risk* (cooked, ready-to-eat products) zones must be physically separated from *low-risk* (raw material receiving, washing) zones. The "reference design" is the **master template** an architect-engineer consults before drawing the actual plant — similar to how a chef uses a recipe template before adapting it to local taste. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must appreciate that **no two plants are identical**, but the *reference design* provides the **anchor parameters**: process line capacity, equipment footprint (m²), HVAC requirements (ACH – Air Changes per Hour), CIP (Clean-In-Place) loops, drainage gradients (minimum **1:60 to 1:40**), and personnel flow corridors (≥ **1.2 m** wide). ## 1.2 Why Five Industry Segments? KTU's Module 2 deliberately picks **five** reference industries because they collectively cover the full spectrum of food engineering challenges: | Industry Segment | Dominant Unit Operation | Critical Hygiene Class | |---|---|---| | **Bakery & Biscuit** | Baking (convective/radiative heat transfer) | Medium | | **Fruit Processing** | Thermal preservation (pasteurization) | High | | **Vegetable Processing** | Blanching + freezing | High | | **Beverage Processing** | Mixing, carbonation, aseptic filling | Very High | | **Dairy Processing** | Pasteurization, homogenization, CIP | Very High (High-risk) | ## 1.3 Common Design Objectives (All Five Industries) 1. **Unidirectional Flow** — Raw material → Process → Finished goods. No backflow. 2. **Segregation of Hygienic Zones** — Black zone (raw) → Grey zone (process) → White zone (aseptic). 3. **Minimum Cross-Movement** — Personnel and forklift paths must never intersect. 4. **Easy Cleanability** — Floors with coving, wall-floor junctions curved (radius ≥ **50 mm**), drains at low points. 5. **Future Expandability** — Master plan must reserve **20–30%** undeveloped land. > [!TIP] > Memorize the **"3-S Rule"**: **Sanitation**, **Separation**, and **Space** — every reference design must satisfy these three pillars. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Bakery & Biscuit Plant — Reference Design ### 2.1.1 Product Categories - **Bread** (sliced, unsliced, brown, white, speciality). - **Biscuits** (hard-dough, short-dough, cookies, cream crackers). - **Cakes, pastries, rusk**. ### 2.1.2 Process Flow (Bread) 1. **Weighing & Sifting** of flour 2. **Mixing** (spiral / horizontal mixer) 3. **Fermentation** (proofer at **30 °C, 80% RH**, **2–4 h**) 4. **Dividing & Rounding** 5. **Intermediate Proofing** (**15–20 min**) 6. **Moulding / Sheeting** 7. **Final Proofing** (humidity chamber, **35 °C, 85% RH**) 8. **Baking** (tunnel oven, **220–250 °C**, **20–35 min**) 9. **Cooling** (spiral cooler, ambient) 10. **Slicing & Packaging** ### 2.1.3 Bakery Plant Layout — Key Numbers | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Floor area (small plant, 5 t/day) | **1500–2500 m²** | | Mixing room height | **≥ 4.5 m** | | Oven zone ventilation | **15–20 ACH** | | Cooling tower (spiral) length | **25–40 m** | | Slicer throughput | **30–60 loaves/min** | | Power load (ovens dominate) | **300–500 kW** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Load from Ovens:** Ovens are the *heart* of a bakery. HVAC for the oven zone must reject enormous sensible heat (often **> 100 kW** per oven line). Designers must account for **stack effect** — hot air rises and contaminates cooler zones if not properly exhausted. ### 2.1.4 Equipment Layout Strategy - **Linear (Product-Centered) Layout** — best for biscuits (continuous sheeting → baking → cooling). - **Functional (Process-Centered) Layout** — best for cakes & pastries (batch). - The oven is **anchor equipment**: place it centrally with raw side (proofer) on one flank and finished side (cooler → slicer → packer) on the other. --- ## 2.2 Fruit Processing Plant — Reference Design ### 2.2.1 Product Categories Juices (RTD, concentrate), jams, jellies, canned fruits, dried fruits, frozen pulp. ### 2.2.2 Process Flow (Orange Juice — Aseptic) 1. Receiving & weighing 2. Washing & sorting 3. Juice extraction (reamer / FMC extractor) 4. **Pasteurization** (**90 °C, 15–30 s** — HTST) 5. Centrifugal clarification 6. **Aseptic filling** (sterile bag-in-box or Tetra Pak) 7. Cold storage (**4 °C**) ### 2.2.3 KTU High-Yield Formulas — Fruit Plant $$ Q = \frac{M \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{3600 \cdot \eta} \quad \text{[kW]} $$ Where: - $Q$ = heat duty (kW) - $M$ = mass flow (kg/h) - $C_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), for juice ≈ **3.7 kJ/kg·K** - $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) - $\eta$ = heat exchanger efficiency (typically **0.85–0.92**) **Pasteurizer Holding Tube Length:** $$ L = v \cdot t \quad \text{[m]} $$ Where $v$ = flow velocity (**1.5–2.0 m/s**), $t$ = holding time (**15–30 s**). ### 2.2.4 Fruit Plant Layout Highlights - **Receiving bay** must be enclosed, with roller conveyors at slope **1:30**. - **Aseptic zone** must be **positive-pressure** with **HEPA-filtered** air (**≥ 12 ACH**). - Effluent from washing is high in **BOD (≈ 2000 mg/L)** — primary treatment mandatory. - Floor area for a 50 t/day juice plant: **3500–5000 m²**. --- ## 2.3 Vegetable Processing Plant — Reference Design ### 2.3.1 Product Categories - Frozen vegetables (peas, carrots, beans, cauliflower). - Dehydrated vegetables (onion, potato flakes). - Pickles, sauces, ketchup, canned vegetables. - Ready-to-cook mixes (RTC). ### 2.3.2 Process Flow (Frozen Peas) 1. Receiving & grading 2. **Pre-cooling** (hydrocooler, **4–8 °C**) 3. **Blanching** (**95–100 °C, 2–4 min**) 4. **Cooling** (cold water spray, **≤ 10 °C**) 5. **IQF Freezing** (Individually Quick Frozen, **−35 °C**, air velocity **4–6 m/s**) 6. **Glazing** (optional, water dip) 7. **Automated weighing & packing** 8. **Cold storage** (**−18 °C** or lower) ### 2.3.3 IQF Capacity Formula $$ \dot{m}_{IQF} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \eta_{load} \quad \text{[kg/h]} $$ Where $\rho$ = bulk density of peas (**600–700 kg/m³**), $A$ = belt area (m²), $v$ = belt speed (**0.5–2.0 m/min**), $\eta_{load}$ = loading factor (**0.6–0.8**). ### 2.3.4 Vegetable Plant Layout — Critical Considerations - **Cold chain integrity** — Cold store must be the *last* room before dispatch; no breaks in temperature. - **Effluent treatment** — Blanching effluent has very high **COD (≈ 5000 mg/L)** and suspended solids. - **Personal hygiene** — Hand-wash stations every **10 m** in process area. - Reference area for 20 t/day frozen veg plant: **4000–6000 m²**. --- ## 2.4 Beverage Processing Plant — Reference Design ### 2.4.1 Product Categories - **Non-carbonated**: Juices, RTD teas, energy drinks, flavored water. - **Carbonated (Soft Drinks)**: Cola, soda, sparkling water. - **Brewed**: Beer (covered separately in some syllabi). ### 2.4.2 Process Flow (Carbonated Soft Drink — CSD) 1. **Water Treatment**: Sand filter → Activated carbon → Softener → **RO** → UV 2. **Sugar Syrup Preparation**: Dissolving at **80 °C**, filtration 3. **Syrup / Water Mixing** (ratio **1:5** approx.) 4. **Carbonation**: CO₂ injection at **3.0–4.0 vol of CO₂**, **0–4 °C** 5. **Bottle/Can Rinsing, Filling, Capping** (monobloc) 6. **Pasteurization (warm fill)** OR **Cold aseptic fill** 7. **Labeling & Coding** 8. **Shrink-wrap & Crating** ### 2.4.3 Carbonation Equations (KTU High-Yield) **Henry's Law for CO₂ solubility in beverages:** $$ P = H \cdot x_{CO_2} $$ For CO₂ in water at **0 °C**: $H \approx 1.0 \times 10^6$ mmHg; at **20 °C**: $H \approx 1.45 \times 10^6$ mmHg. **Volumetric concentration (volumes of CO₂ per volume of beverage):** $$ V_{CO_2} = \frac{(P_{abs} - P_{H_2O})}{1013.25} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T}{H_{ref}}\right)^{-1} $$ A typical **3.5 vol** carbonation needs **~ 0.83 MPa** gauge pressure at **0 °C**. ### 2.4.4 Beverage Plant Layout - **Syrup room** — temperature-controlled (**20–25 °C**), humidity **< 60% RH**. - **Blowing & filling hall** — must be **positive pressure** relative to outside, **20–25 ACH**, HEPA filters for aseptic lines. - **CO₂ bulk tank farm** — outdoor, with **6 m** clearance from buildings, gas detectors. - Reference area for a 10,000 BPH (bottles per hour) plant: **8000–12,000 m²** including warehouse. --- ## 2.5 Dairy Processing Plant — Reference Design ### 2.5.1 Product Categories Liquid milk (Toned, Double Toned, Full Cream), curd/yogurt, butter, ghee, cheese, paneer, ice cream, milk powder (SMP/WMP), condensed milk. ### 2.5.2 Process Flow (Pasteurized Pouched Milk) 1. **Receiving** (chilled milk, **4 °C** at dock) 2. **Weighing & Sampling** (lab QC) 3. **Pre-heating** (**40–45 °C**) 4. **Separation** (cream standardization) 5. **Homogenization** (typically at **15–20 MPa**, **55–60 °C**) 6. **HTST Pasteurization** (**72 °C, 15 s**) 7. **Cooling** (**4 °C**) 8. **Aseptic Pouch Filling** (or chilled bulk to secondary processing) 9. **Cold Storage** (**4 °C**) 10. **Distribution (Cold Chain)** ### 2.5.3 Pasteurization Equation (HTST Validation) $$ F_{72} = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\left(\frac{T(t) - 72}{z}\right)} \, dt $$ Where: - $F_{72}$ = pasteurization effect (must be **≥ 1.0** for milk per FSSAI/IDF) - $z$ = z-value for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* / *Coxiella burnetii* = **6.5 °C** - $T(t)$ = temperature history (°C) - $t$ = time (s) ### 2.5.4 CIP (Clean-In-Place) Design — KTU Favourite CIP loop flow rate: $$ Q_{CIP} = \frac{V_{loop}}{t_{CIP}} \quad \text{[L/min]} $$ Typical velocities: **1.5–2.0 m/s** (turbulent flow required to achieve wall shear **> 1.5 Pa**). ### 2.5.5 Dairy Plant Layout — Reference Numbers | Parameter | Reference Value | |---|---| | Process hall temperature | **18–22 °C** | | Process hall humidity | **< 70% RH** | | Cold store | **4 °C ± 1 °C** | | Deep freeze (ice cream) | **−25 °C** | | Floor slope to drain | **1:60** | | Floor-wall coving radius | **≥ 50 mm** | | Drain per 40 m² | **1 drain** (min.) | | Floor area (100,000 L/day plant) | **5000–8000 m²** | | Effluent BOD (dairy) | **800–1500 mg/L** | > [!WARNING] > **Dairy = Highest Hygiene Risk:** Dairy is one of the *highest-risk* foods because pathogens grow rapidly in the pH range (**6.4–6.8**) and water activity (**0.97–0.99**) of milk. Every surface in contact with milk must be **SS 304** or **SS 316** stainless steel. ## 2.6 Master Formula Sheet — Consolidated $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Heat Duty: } Q = \frac{M \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{3600 \cdot \eta} \\[6pt] &\text{Pasteurization Effect: } F_{72} = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\left(\frac{T-72}{z}\right)} dt \\[6pt] &\text{Holding Tube Length: } L = v \cdot t \\[6pt] &\text{CO}_2 \text{ Solubility (Henry): } P = H \cdot x_{CO_2} \\[6pt] &\text{CIP Flow Rate: } Q_{CIP} = \frac{V_{loop}}{t_{CIP}} \\[6pt] &\text{IQF Throughput: } \dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \eta_{load} \\[6pt] &\text{ACH Requirement: } ACH = \frac{Q_{HVAC}}{V_{room}} \quad \text{[h}^{-1}\text{]} \end{aligned} $$ # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pasteurization Effect (F-value) for HTST This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU dairy engineering papers. Let's derive it from first principles. ### Step 1 — Define the Microbial Death Rate Microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics. The decimal reduction time **D** is the time to reduce a microbial population by 90 % at a reference temperature **T_ref**. $$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N \quad \Rightarrow \quad N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt} $$ Where the rate constant $k = \dfrac{\ln 10}{D_{T}}$. ### Step 2 — Account for Temperature Dependence (z-value) The z-value is the temperature rise required to reduce **D** by a factor of **10**. Therefore: $$ D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{T_{ref} - T}{z}\right)} $$ Substituting: $$ k(T) = \frac{\ln 10}{D_{ref}} \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}\right)} $$ ### Step 3 — Define the Pasteurization Value (F) $F_{T_{ref}}^{z}$ represents the equivalent time (in minutes or seconds) at the reference temperature that produces the same lethality as the actual time-temperature history. $$ F = D_{ref} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) $$ For **complete pasteurization** (e.g., 12-log reduction of *M. tuberculosis*), the required $F_{72}^{6.5}$ is **≥ 1.0 minute** (or 60 seconds for time in seconds convention). ### Step 4 — Integral Form For a continuous process with temperature varying in time: $$ F = \int_{0}^{t_{total}} 10^{\left(\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{z}\right)} \, dt $$ For HTST milk at constant $T = 72 °C$, $t = 15$ s: $$ F = 10^{\left(\frac{72 - 72}{6.5}\right)} \cdot 15 = 1.0 \cdot 15 = 15 \text{ s} $$ This satisfies the **FSSAI minimum of 15 seconds at 72 °C** — the legal benchmark. > [!TIP] > **Numerical Memorization:** For KTU problems, remember: at **80 °C, 1 s** is equivalent to **~ 5 s at 72 °C** because of the z-value scaling. Useful for "what-if" questions. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Heat Duty of a Juice Pasteurizer **Problem:** A fruit juice pasteurizer handles **5000 kg/h** of juice (Cp = **3.7 kJ/kg·K**). The juice enters at **30 °C** and exits at **90 °C**. The heat exchanger efficiency is **88%**. Find the steam-side heat duty in **kW**. ### Solution: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{M \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{3600 \cdot \eta} \\[6pt] Q &= \frac{5000 \cdot 3.7 \cdot (90 - 30)}{3600 \cdot 0.88} \\[6pt] Q &= \frac{5000 \cdot 3.7 \cdot 60}{3168} \\[6pt] Q &= \frac{1{,}110{,}000}{3168} \\[6pt] Q &= 350.38 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A **~ 350 kW** steam boiler is needed (plus 10% margin → **~ 385 kW**). This drives the entire utility selection of the juice plant. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Industries | Design Parameter | Bakery/Biscuit | Fruit | Vegetable | Beverage | Dairy | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Dominant Operation** | Baking | Pasteurization | Blanching+IQF | Carbonation | HTST | | **Hygiene Risk** | Low–Med | High | High | Very High | Very High | | **Critical Equipment** | Tunnel oven | Plate HX, aseptic filler | IQF tunnel, blancher | Carbonator, monobloc | HTST, homogenizer, CIP | | **Cooling Need** | Ambient | Cold | Deep freeze | Chilled + CO₂ | 4 °C cold chain | | **Effluent BOD (mg/L)** | 500–1000 | 1500–2500 | 3000–6000 | 300–800 | 800–1500 | | **Effluent Treatment** | Primary | Primary+Sec | Primary+Sec+Bio | Primary | Primary+Sec | | **Steam Demand** | Medium | High | High | Medium | High | | **Floor Area /t/day (m²)** | 300–500 | 70–100 | 200–300 | 800–1200 | 50–80 | | **HVAC (ACH)** | 8–12 | 12–18 | 12–20 | 20–25 | 15–20 | | **Zone Pressure (aseptic)** | Neutral | Positive | Neutral | Positive | Positive | | **CIP Required?** | Mostly COP | Yes (HTST) | Partial | Yes (syrup lines) | Yes (Mandatory) | > [!NOTE] > **CIP vs COP** — In CIP (Clean-In-Place), cleaning fluids circulate *without disassembly*. In COP (Clean-Out-Of-Place), equipment is dismantled and hand-washed. CIP is mandatory in dairy & beverage; COP is common in small bakeries. ## 3.4 Personnel & Material Flow — Generalized Equation For a balanced line, the **line efficiency** is: $$ \eta_{line} = \frac{\text{Number of balanced operations}}{\text{Bottleneck time}} \times 100\% $$ Target: $\eta_{line} \geq$ **85%** for all five industries. ## 3.5 Storage Capacity Calculation (Master Formula) $$ \text{Storage Volume (m}^3) = \frac{\text{Daily production (kg)} \times \text{Days of storage}}{\text{Bulk density (kg/m}^3) \times \text{Stacking factor}} $$ - **Bakery:** Flour silo for **5–7 days** (stacking factor **0.6**). - **Fruit:** Cold store for pulp/concentrate at **−18 °C** for **30 days** (stacking factor **0.5**). - **Vegetable:** Frozen store at **−20 °C**, **6–12 months** (stacking factor **0.55**). - **Beverage:** Sugar silo for **15–30 days** (stacking factor **0.65**). - **Dairy:** Cold store for finished pouches, **24 h** max (stacking factor **0.45**). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Food Plant Layout — Master Block Diagram This Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture flow** common to all five reference industries, with industry-specific equipment modules branching off the central process line. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["RECEIVING BAY
Raw Material Intake

Grading Washing"] node3 --> node4["PROCESS ZONE
Mixing Blending Cooking
HTST Pasteurization"] node4 --> node5["PACKAGING ZONE
Filling Sealing Coding

Cold Chain Logistics
Vehicle Loading"] node8["BOILER HOUSE"] -. Steam .-> node4 node9["CHILLER PLANT"] -. Cold .-> node3 node10["RO WATER PLANT"] -. Purified H2O .-> node4 node11["COMPRESSOR ROOM"] -. Air CO2 .-> node5 node12["ETP EQUALIZATION"] -. Waste Water .-> node7 subgraph ANCILLARY node8 node9 node10 node11 node12 end style node1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style node4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style node5 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style node6 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394 ``` ## 4.2 Bakery Plant — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA1["FLOUR SILO"] --> nodeA2["SIFTER WEIGHER"] nodeA2 --> nodeA3["SPIRAL MIXER"] nodeA3 --> nodeA4["FERMENTATION ROOM

Drying Cabinet"] nodeD6 --> nodeD9["HTST PASTEURIZATION"] nodeD9 --> nodeD10["ASEPTIC FILLING"] nodeD7 --> nodeD11["COLD STORE -18 C"] nodeD8 --> nodeD12["PACKING ROOM"] style nodeD5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000 style nodeD9 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 style nodeD11 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] **List any three critical differences between the reference layout of a dairy plant and a bakery plant.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Hygiene Zone Classification:** Dairy plants follow strict **Black–Grey–White** zone segregation (raw milk → process → aseptic) whereas bakery plants have only **two** zones (raw ingredients + process) since baked goods are not aseptic. 2. **HVAC:** Dairy process halls require **15–20 ACH** with positive pressure aseptic zones; bakery halls need only **8–12 ACH** since surface temperatures (> 200 °C in oven) act as a microbial kill step. 3. **CIP Mandate:** Dairy plants have *mandatory* Clean-In-Place loops for the entire HTST line (every 8–12 h of operation), whereas bakeries rely mostly on Clean-Out-Of-Place (COP) washing of dismantled equipment. *(Any three valid points – 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Why is the IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) tunnel designed with air velocity between 4–6 m/s?** **Model Answer:** The IQF tunnel operates on the principle of **forced convection freezing**. Air velocity in the range of **4–6 m/s** ensures: - **Turbulent flow** over each product piece, maximizing the convective heat transfer coefficient (h ≈ **30–50 W/m²·K**). - **Rapid heat removal** so that the product surface passes through the zone of maximum ice-crystal growth (**0 to −5 °C**) in **< 30 minutes**, preventing large intra-cellular ice crystals that damage texture. - **Uniform freezing** of each individual piece (hence "Individually Quick Frozen") rather than clumping into blocks, preserving the free-flowing characteristic for downstream weighing/packing. *(3 marks for the above)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q. (a)** Describe the **reference layout of a dairy processing plant** handling **1,00,000 litres of milk per day**, with reference to zoning, equipment arrangement, utility requirements, and effluent treatment. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Zoning (2 marks):** The plant must be divided into **Black Zone** (raw milk receiving, can washing, lab), **Grey Zone** (processing hall — pasteurization, separation, homogenization), and **White Zone** (aseptic filling, finished goods cold store). Pressure cascade: **White > Grey > Black**, with airlocks and personnel air showers between zones. **(ii) Equipment Arrangement (2 marks):** HTST line laid out as a **straight-through flow** to minimize piping and residence time in non-refrigerated sections. Sequence: Raw milk tank → Plate Heat Exchanger (regenerative section) → Separator → Homogenizer → Heating section → Holding tube (15 s) → FDV → Cooling section → Pouch filler. The CIP skid is placed in a **separate CIP room** with its own drain connected directly to ETP. **(iii) Utilities (2 marks):** - **Steam** (from boiler, **~ 500 kg/h**, working pressure **0.6 MPa**). - **Refrigeration** (chilled water at **4 °C**, capacity **~ 200 TR** for cooling + cold store). - **RO water** (for CIP final rinse, **1000 L/h**). - **Compressed air** (oil-free, **6 bar**, for pneumatic valves, FDV actuation). **(iv) Effluent Treatment (1 mark):** Dairy effluent has **BOD ≈ 1000 mg/L** with high fat content. Treatment: **oil–grease trap → equalization tank → DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation) → activated sludge → chlorination → discharge** conforming to CPCB norms. --- **(b)** A dairy plant pasteurizes **10,000 kg/h** of milk (Cp = **3.9 kJ/kg·K**) from **4 °C to 72 °C** in an HTST section with regenerator efficiency of **85%**. The regenerator recovers **80% of the heat**. Calculate: (i) Heat required in the heating section (steam side). **(4 marks)** (ii) Net steam consumption (latent heat of steam = **2200 kJ/kg**). **(3 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Total heat duty for heating 4 °C → 72 °C (ΔT = 68 K):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= \frac{M \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{3600} \\[6pt] Q_{total} &= \frac{10000 \cdot 3.9 \cdot 68}{3600} \\[6pt] Q_{total} &= \frac{2{,}652{,}000}{3600} \\[6pt] Q_{total} &= 736.67 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ Regenerator recovers **80%**, so net heat from steam: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{steam} &= Q_{total} \cdot (1 - 0.80) \\[6pt] Q_{steam} &= 736.67 \cdot 0.20 \\[6pt] Q_{steam} &= 147.33 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Steam consumption:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{steam} &= \frac{Q_{steam} \cdot 3600}{L} \\[6pt] \dot{m}_{steam} &= \frac{147.33 \cdot 3600}{2200} \\[6pt] \dot{m}_{steam} &= 240.99 \approx 241 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ Adding 10% margin (for line losses) → **~ 265 kg/h** of steam is needed. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q. (a)** Discuss the **reference design of a carbonated beverage plant** with reference to process flow, equipment layout, and material flow. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Process Flow (2 marks):** Water treatment (RO + UV) → Sugar syrup cooking & filtration → Syrup/water blending (1:5) → CO₂ injection in carbonator at **0–4 °C, 3.5 vol** → Monobloc rinser-filler-capper → In-bottle pasteurizer (**68 °C, 20 min**) OR cold aseptic tunnel → Labeler/coder → Shrink-wrap → Warehouse. **(ii) Equipment Layout (2 marks):** - **Two-hall concept**: One hall for **syrup preparation**, another for **blowing–filling–capping** (BFC) to prevent syrup mist contaminating empty bottles. - CO₂ bulk tank located **outdoors**, with **6 m clearance** and gas detectors. - Palletizing and shrink-wrap at the downstream end before dispatch bay. **(iii) Material Flow (2 marks):** Empty PET/glass bottles enter from one side (→ BFC), finished goods exit the opposite side (→ warehouse). Personnel use separate air-locked corridors (to prevent entry into the aseptic zone without sanitization). Forklifts travel in a dedicated service corridor **outside** the BFC hall. **(iv) Special Considerations (1 mark):** - **Hygiene & air quality:** BFC hall maintained at **20–25 ACH, 22 ± 2 °C**, positive pressure **+ 15 Pa**, with HEPA filters (for aseptic lines). - **Utilities:** CO₂ bulk tank, RO plant (10,000 L/h), chiller (200 TR), boiler (300 kg/h). - **Effluent:** Bottle-washer effluent (high alkalinity) and syrup spillage (high BOD) — neutralization + biological treatment required. --- **(b)** A juice plant uses a plate heat exchanger to pasteurize **3000 kg/h** of mango pulp (Cp = **3.6 kJ/kg·K**) from **25 °C to 95 °C**. The regeneration section recovers **70% of heat**. Calculate: (i) Steam-side heat duty in kW. **(3 marks)** (ii) Steam consumption (latent heat = **2200 kJ/kg**). **(2 marks)** (iii) Length of holding tube if velocity = **1.5 m/s** and holding time = **30 s**. **(2 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Total heat duty (ΔT = 95 − 25 = 70 K):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= \frac{3000 \cdot 3.6 \cdot 70}{3600} = \frac{756{,}000}{3600} = 210 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ Regenerator recovers 70%, so steam side provides 30%: $$ Q_{steam} = 210 \cdot 0.30 = 63 \text{ kW} $$ **(ii) Steam consumption:** $$ \dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{63 \cdot 3600}{2200} = 103.09 \text{ kg/h} $$ Adding 10% margin → **~ 113 kg/h**. **(iii) Holding tube length:** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= v \cdot t = 1.5 \text{ m/s} \cdot 30 \text{ s} \\[4pt] L &= 45 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:** > 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting the **3600** factor in $Q = M C_p \Delta T / 3600$ — students often confuse per-hour and per-second basis. Marks lost: **1–2**. > 2. **Failing to subtract regenerator recovery:** If the question gives regenerator efficiency, the steam duty is only the *balance*. Marks lost: **2–3**. > 3. **Forgetting the latent heat conversion:** Writing "63 kW ÷ 2200 = steam" without multiplying by 3600 — this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU 2023 papers. Marks lost: **3**. > 4. **Confusing CIP vs COP:** In dairy questions, every milk-contact surface is CIP, but bakery equipment is often COP. Marks lost: **1–2**. > 5. **Zone-pressure direction:** Students often reverse the pressure cascade (Black > Grey > White). Correct cascade is **White > Grey > Black** for aseptic protection. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Points — KTU Module 2** - **Five Reference Industries:** Bakery/Biscuit · Fruit · Vegetable · Beverage · Dairy. - **Three Design Pillars:** Sanitation, Separation, Space. - **Three Zones (High-Risk Plants):** Black (raw) → Grey (process) → White (aseptic). **Pressure cascade: White > Grey > Black**. - **HTST Pasteurization Law:** **72 °C × 15 s** (F₇₂ ≥ 15 s for milk; FSSAI mandatory). - **Key z-values to remember:** Milk pathogens **z = 6.5 °C**; vegetative bacteria generally **z = 5–10 °C**; spores **z = 10–12 °C**. - **Cₚ values (commonly asked):** Water = **4.18**, Milk = **3.9**, Juice = **3.7**, Oil = **1.9–2.2** (kJ/kg·K). - **IQF air velocity = 4–6 m/s**; IQF air temperature = **−35 °C**. - **Blanching: 95–100 °C, 2–4 min** for vegetables (inactivates enzymes like peroxidase). - **CIP loop velocity = 1.5–2.0 m/s** (turbulent, wall shear > 1.5 Pa). - **Floor slope: 1:60** for drains; coving radius ≥ **50 mm** at wall-floor junctions. - **HVAC rule of thumb:** Bakery 8–12 ACH; Fruit 12–18; Veg 12–20; Beverage 20–25; Dairy 15–20. - **Asymptotic formula $F_{72}$** is the most important integration — must be able to derive and apply. - **Henry's Law** is the basis of carbonation; remember **colder temperature = higher CO₂ solubility**. - **Ovens anchor bakery layout**; **FDV anchors dairy layout**; **Carbonator anchors beverage layout**; **IQF tunnel anchors frozen vegetable layout**; **Plate HX anchors fruit juice layout**. - **Effluent rule:** Bakery < Fruit < Vegetable (in terms of BOD); Dairy requires **oil–grease trap + DAF + activated sludge**. - **Personnel flow:** Always provide a separate **change-room cum air-shower** between Black and White zones — *non-negotiable per FSSAI Schedule 4*. - **Future expansion:** Reserve **20–30%** of total land for future utilities/buildings in master planning. - **Master formulae to memorize:** * $Q = M C_p \Delta T / (3600 \eta)$ * $F = \int 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z} \, dt$ * $L = v \cdot t$ * $Q_{CIP} = V/t$ * $P = H \cdot x_{CO_2}$ - **One-liners for viva:** *Why HTST not UHT for fluid milk?* — Because HTST retains fresh flavor; UHT gives cooked flavor. *Why is fruit juice pH-critical?* — Because low pH (< 4.6) permits milder pasteurization (90 °C/30 s) due to synergistic acid effect. *Why oven ventilation high?* — To remove moisture and combustion gases. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 2 Reference Design Notes — PEFTT861, KTU 2024 Scheme** > *Designed for Board Exam Pattern (ESE) — 3 marks short + 14 marks long with internal choice.*

topics.title: Hygienic design of food plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hygienic design of food plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HYGIENIC DESIGN OF FOOD PLANTS** is the systematic, engineering-driven approach to designing, constructing, equipping, and arranging a food processing facility in a manner that **prevents contamination**, **facilitates effective cleaning and sanitization**, and **minimizes harborage of pests and microorganisms**. It encompasses building architecture, material selection, equipment geometry, air handling, drainage, personnel flow, and process zoning, all engineered to comply with the principles of **HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)**, **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices)**, and **GHP (Good Hygiene Practices)**.

According to the **EHEDG (European Hygienic Engineering and Design Group) Document 8**, hygienic design is defined as:

> *"The application of scientific and engineering principles to the design of equipment, processes, and facilities in such a way that product contamination is prevented and cleaning and sanitization can be performed effectively and economically."*

### Key Regulatory Pillars Governing Hygienic Design in India

| Authority | Standard / Regulation | Scope |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| **FSSAI** | Food Safety and Standards (Licensing & Registration) Regulations, 2011 + Schedule 4 | Mandatory general hygienic practices |
| **Codex Alimentarius** | CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020) | International code of practice – General principles of food hygiene |
| **3-A Sanitary Standards** | 3-A SSI (USA) | Equipment sanitary design |
| **EHEDG** | Documents 1–44 | European hygienic engineering guidelines |
| **IS 2491:1998** | Bureau of Indian Standards | Hygiene in food industry – general guidelines |
| **FDA 21 CFR Part 110** | U.S. Federal Regulation | GMP in manufacturing, packaging & holding of human food |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEFTT861 – Module 2):** Students must understand *building location, construction materials, internal finishes, ventilation, lighting, drainage, equipment hygienic design, and sanitation programs* as a unified hygienic system — not as isolated topics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **hospital operating theatre**. Why does it look so different from your living room?

- The floors are seamless, sloped toward a single drain.
- The walls have no cracks or corners where dust collects.
- The ceiling is smooth and dust-free.
- Air is filtered and pressurized.
- Everything can be wiped down with disinfectant in minutes.

**A hygienic food plant is the industrial twin of a hospital OT.** If you can design a facility that treats *food* with the same rigor that hospitals treat *patients*, you eliminate the conditions in which bacteria, pests, and chemical contaminants thrive.

> [!TIP]
> **The "One-Meter Rule":** Every surface, joint, weld, and piece of equipment within the food handling area should be cleanable by a person with a brush and a hose within a reasonable time. If you cannot see it, reach it, and clean it — **it is a contamination liability**.

### The Four Pillars of Hygienic Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **SEPARATION** – Isolate raw from cooked, clean from dirty, people from product.
> 2. **SMOOTHNESS** – Every surface must be non-absorbent, non-porous, and free of crevices.
> 3. **CLEANABILITY** – Geometry must allow CIP (Clean-In-Place) or manual cleaning without disassembly.
> 4. **DRYABILITY** – Surfaces must drain and dry to deny moisture to microbial growth.

---

## 1.3 Why Hygienic Design Matters – Engineering & Business Rationale

| Driver | Engineering Implication |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Public health** | Prevention of foodborne illness outbreaks (e.g., *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *E. coli*) |
| **Regulatory compliance** | FSSAI licensing renewal, export market acceptance (EU, USFDA) |
| **Shelf life extension** | Lower initial bioburden → longer microbiological shelf life |
| **Product recall avoidance** | A single recall can cost **$10 million – $30 million** in lost brand equity |
| **Operational efficiency** | Cleanable design reduces CIP chemical + water use by 20–40% |
| **Insurance & liability** | Hygienic plants attract lower product liability premiums |

The **WHO (World Health Organization)** estimates that globally, **600 million** people fall ill and **420,000** die each year from foodborne diseases — a massive percentage of which are attributable to design-level failures in food processing environments.

---

## 1.4 The Eight Guiding Principles of Hygienic Design (KTU High-Yield List)

> [!NOTE]
> These 8 principles are **frequently asked in KTU exams** verbatim. Memorize and apply.

1. **Cleanability** – Surfaces must be readily accessible for cleaning.
2. **Smoothness** – Surface roughness **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** for food contact surfaces (stainless steel grade 304/316, polished to mirror finish).
3. **Free of crevices, dead legs, and sharp internal corners.**
4. **Self-draining** – No horizontal surfaces where water can pool.
5. **Compatible materials** – Non-toxic, corrosion-resistant, non-absorbent.
6. **No contamination transfer** – No lubricants, coatings, or paints that can migrate into product.
7. **Inspectability** – Equipment can be inspected for damage, wear, and cleanliness.
8. **Maintainability** – Parts can be replaced without compromising hygiene.
