4.2 Forward Pass and Backward Pass Computation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FP[Forward Pass Logic] FP1[Read all activities] --> FP2[Identify predecessors] FP2 --> FP3[Compute ES = max EF of predecessors] FP3 --> FP4[Compute EF = ES + te] FP4 --> FP5[Project Duration Te = max EF of terminal activities] end subgraph BP[Backward Pass Logic] BP1[Set LF of terminal activities = Te] --> BP2[Identify successors] BP2 --> BP3[Compute LF = min LS of successors] BP3 --> BP4[Compute LS = LF minus te] BP4 --> BP5[Repeat until all activities processed] end subgraph FLOAT[Float and Critical Path] FL1[Compute TF = LS minus ES] --> FL2[Compute FF = min ES of successors minus EF] FL2 --> FL3[Activity is Critical if TF = 0] FL3 --> FL4[Trace Critical Path through network] end FP5 --> BP1 BP5 --> FL1 style FP fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400 style BP fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000 style FLOAT fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` --- ## 4.3 Three-Time Estimate and Probability Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD estInput[/Activity Three Time Estimates:
to, tm, tp/] estInput --> teCalc[Compute Expected Time
te = to + 4tm + tp all divided by 6] estInput --> varCalc[Compute Variance
sigma squared = tp minus to divided by 6 squared] teCalc --> fpPass[Forward Pass
ES, EF] varCalc --> varSum[Sum Variance Along Critical Path] fpPass --> cpIdent[Identify Critical Path
TF = 0] varSum --> sigmaProj[Project Std Deviation
sigma proj = sqrt sum sigma squared] cpIdent --> teProj[Project Expected Duration Te] sigmaProj --> zCalc[Z Value
Z = Ts minus Te all divided by sigma proj] teProj --> zCalc zCalc --> probOut[Probability of Completion
P of T less than equal to Ts = Phi Z] probOut --> decision{Decision} decision -->|P greater than 90 percent| accept1[Accept Schedule] decision -->|P less than 50 percent| reject1[Crashing Required] decision -->|50 to 90 percent| review1[Review Risk Mitigation] style estInput fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style accept1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style reject1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style review1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` --- ## 4.4 CPM Project Crashing Methodology Block ```mermaid flowchart TD crashInput[/Network with Normal Duration and Cost/] crashInput --> path1[Identify Critical Path] path1 --> slope1[Compute Cost Slope for each critical activity
Slope = Ccrash minus Cnormal divided by Dnormal minus Dcrash] slope1 --> select1{Select activity with minimum cost slope} select1 --> crash1[Crash selected activity by 1 day] crash1 --> recalc1[Recompute network, identify new critical path] recalc1 --> parallelCheck{Parallel critical path formed?} parallelCheck -->|Yes| crashBoth[Crash both parallel critical path activities] parallelCheck -->|No| costCheck{New total cost less than old?} crashBoth --> costCheck costCheck -->|Yes| cont1[Continue crashing] costCheck -->|No| stop1[STOP at optimum] cont1 --> select1 style crashInput fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style stop1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style select1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Define PERT. Explain its three-time estimate system with a suitable food plant engineering example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) is a probabilistic project management tool used for planning, scheduling, and controlling projects with uncertain activity durations. **[1 mark]** It uses three time estimates for each activity: - **Optimistic time ($t_o$)**: Best-case duration (e.g., pasteurizer installation in 6 days with no vendor delay) - **Most likely time ($t_m$)**: Realistic expected duration (e.g., 8 days under normal conditions) - **Pessimistic time ($t_p$)**: Worst-case duration (e.g., 16 days with monsoon + customs delay) **[1 mark]** The expected time is computed using the Beta distribution formula: $$ t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} $$ **Food Plant Example:** For setting up an Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) in a seafood processing unit, the three estimates may be 25, 30, and 45 days, giving $t_e = 31.67$ days. This single expected value is then used in forward/backward pass calculations. **[1 mark]** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between PERT and CPM in the context of food plant project management.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | PERT | CPM | |---|---|---| | Time estimates | Three (probabilistic) | One (deterministic) | | Statistical basis | Beta distribution | None | | Primary focus | Probability of completion | Time-cost trade-off | | Best suited for | New product launches, novel R&D projects | Repetitive construction/maintenance work | **[1 mark per significant difference, 3 distinct points required]** In a food plant context, **PERT is used for greenfield projects** (high uncertainty in vendor deliveries, statutory approvals), while **CPM is used for plant expansion and routine shutdowns** (well-defined durations, focus on cost minimization). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to compute variance for **all activities on the critical path**, not just one. The project variance is the **sum** of individual variances along the CP. > - **Common Mistake #2:** In backward pass, students incorrectly use **max** instead of **min** for LF computation. Remember: an activity must finish **before** the earliest successor starts → use **min(LS of successors)**. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert Z-value to probability using the standard normal table. The Z alone does NOT represent probability. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Students confuse Total Float with Free Float. TF = LS - ES; FF = ES(successor) - EF. They are equal ONLY on the critical path. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full PERT Network Problem **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Practice]** A food processing company plans to install a new aseptic packaging line. The project activities with three-time estimates (in weeks) are given below: | Activity | Predecessor | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A (Site survey) | — | 2 | 4 | 6 | | B (Civil work) | A | 4 | 6 | 14 | | C (Equipment order) | A | 6 | 10 | 20 | | D (Building construction) | B | 8 | 12 | 16 | | E (Utility connection) | D | 3 | 5 | 13 | | F (Equipment installation) | C, E | 4 | 6 | 8 | | G (Trial run) | F | 2 | 3 | 4 | #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Construct the network, compute expected time and variance for each activity, perform forward and backward pass, and identify the critical path. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$** | Activity | $t_e$ | Calculation | $\sigma^2$ | Calculation | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | 4.00 | (2+16+6)/6 | 0.444 | ((6-2)/6)² | | B | 7.00 | (4+24+14)/6 | 2.778 | ((14-4)/6)² | | C | 11.00 | (6+40+20)/6 | 5.444 | ((20-6)/6)² | | D | 12.00 | (8+48+16)/6 | 1.778 | ((16-8)/6)² | | E | 6.00 | (3+20+13)/6 | 2.778 | ((13-3)/6)² | | F | 6.00 | (4+24+8)/6 | 0.444 | ((8-4)/6)² | | G | 3.00 | (2+12+4)/6 | 0.111 | ((4-2)/6)² | **Step 2: Forward Pass** | Activity | $t_e$ | Pred | $ES$ | $EF$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | 4 | — | 0 | 4 | | B | 7 | A | 4 | 11 | | C | 11 | A | 4 | 15 | | D | 12 | B | 11 | 23 | | E | 6 | D | 23 | 29 | | F | 6 | C, E | max(15,29)=29 | 35 | | G | 3 | F | 35 | **38** | **$T_e$ = 38 weeks** **Step 3: Backward Pass** | Activity | $t_e$ | Succ | $LF$ | $LS$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | G | 3 | — | 38 | 35 | | F | 6 | G | 35 | 29 | | E | 6 | F | 29 | 23 | | D | 12 | E | 23 | 11 | | C | 11 | F | 29 | 18 | | B | 7 | D | 11 | 4 | | A | 4 | B, C | min(4,18)=4 | 0 | **Step 4: Floats** | Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** ✅ | | B | 4 | 11 | 4 | 11 | **0** ✅ | | C | 4 | 15 | 18 | 29 | 14 | | D | 11 | 23 | 11 | 23 | **0** ✅ | | E | 23 | 29 | 23 | 29 | **0** ✅ | | F | 29 | 35 | 29 | 35 | **0** ✅ | | G | 35 | 38 | 35 | 38 | **0** ✅ | **Critical Path: A → B → D → E → F → G (38 weeks)** ✅ #### (b) **[7 Marks]** What is the probability of completing the project in 42 weeks? If the management wants 95% probability, what should be the scheduled duration? **Model Solution:** **Variance along Critical Path:** $$ \sigma^2_{CP} = 0.444 + 2.778 + 1.778 + 2.778 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 8.333 $$ $$ \sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{8.333} = 2.887 \text{ weeks} $$ **Part (i): P(completion in 42 weeks)** $$ Z = \dfrac{42 - 38}{2.887} = \dfrac{4}{2.887} = 1.385 $$ From standard normal table: $P(Z \le 1.385) = 0.9170$ = **91.70% probability** ✅ **Part (ii): Duration for 95% probability** For 95% probability, $Z = 1.645$: $$ T_s = T_e + Z \cdot \sigma_{CP} = 38 + (1.645)(2.887) = 38 + 4.749 = 42.75 \text{ weeks} $$ So the management should schedule **42.75 ≈ 43 weeks** to achieve 95% confidence. ✅ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — CPM Crashing Problem **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** A dairy plant expansion project has the following data: | Activity | Predecessor | Normal Duration (days) | Crash Duration (days) | Normal Cost (Rs) | Crash Cost (Rs) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 10 | 7 | 5,000 | 8,000 | | B | A | 8 | 5 | 4,000 | 7,500 | | C | A | 12 | 9 | 6,000 | 9,000 | | D | B | 6 | 4 | 3,000 | 5,000 | | E | C | 9 | 6 | 4,500 | 7,000 | | F | D, E | 7 | 5 | 3,500 | 5,500 | Indirect cost = Rs 500/day. The owner wants the project completed in **28 days**. #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Find the critical path, normal project duration, and normal project cost. Compute cost slope for each activity. **Model Solution:** **Critical Path (Normal):** - Path 1: A → B → D → F = 10+8+6+7 = **31 days** - Path 2: A → C → E → F = 10+12+9+7 = **38 days** ← Critical **Normal Project Duration = 38 days** **Direct Cost (Normal) = 5000 + 4000 + 6000 + 3000 + 4500 + 3500 = Rs 26,000** **Indirect Cost (Normal) = 500 × 38 = Rs 19,000** **Total Normal Cost = Rs 45,000** **Cost Slopes:** | Activity | Cost Slope = $\dfrac{C_{crash} - C_{normal}}{D_{normal} - D_{crash}}$ | Value (Rs/day) | |---|---|---| | A | (8000-5000)/(10-7) | **1,000.00** | | B | (7500-4000)/(8-5) | **1,166.67** | | C | (9000-6000)/(12-9) | **1,000.00** | | D | (5000-3000)/(6-4) | **1,000.00** | | E | (7000-4500)/(9-6) | **833.33** | | F | (5500-3500)/(7-5) | **1,000.00** | Minimum slope on CP A-C-E-F is **E (833.33 Rs/day)**. ✅ #### (b) **[7 Marks]** Crash the project step-by-step to achieve 28 days. Show the optimal crashing sequence. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Current state — CP = A-C-E-F, Duration = 38 days** Crash E by 3 days (max crash available: 9→6 days). Cost increase = 3 × 833.33 = Rs 2,500. Indirect cost saved = 3 × 500 = Rs 1,500. **Net saving = Rs 1,000** → Proceed. New duration = 35 days. Path 1 = 31, Path 2 = 35. CP still A-C-E-F. **Step 2: E is fully crashed.** Choose next cheapest on CP: A, C, F all have Rs 1,000/day. Crash C by 1 day. Cost increase = 1,000. Indirect saved = 500. **Net cost = +500**. New duration = 34 days. CP still A-C-E-F. **Step 3: Crash C again by 1 day.** Cost = +1,000; Saving = 500. **Net = +500**. Duration = 33 days. **Step 4: Crash C by 1 day again (now 9 days reached).** Cost = +1,000; Saving = 500. **Net = +500**. Duration = 32 days. **Step 5: Now both A, C, F on CP have slope 1000.** Crash any one — but check parallel path A-B-D-F: 10+8+6+7 = 31. So if we crash A or F, parallel path becomes 30 (Path 1) but Path 2 = 31. Crash A by 1 day (10→9). Now Path 1 = 30, Path 2 = 31 (still). **Step 6: Need to crash F to balance.** Crash F by 1 day (7→6). Both paths now = 30 days. **Net cost = +1,000; Saving = 500 → +500**. **Step 7: At 30 days**, both paths are critical. We must crash both A and F simultaneously (or B+D). Cheapest combo on Path 1: B+D slopes = 1166.67+1000 = 2166.67 vs A+F = 1000+1000 = 2000. So crash A+F together. Reduce both by 1 day → duration = 29 days. **Net cost = +2,000; Saving = 500 → +1,500**. **Step 8: To reach 28 days**, again crash both paths together (A+F or B+D). If A+F: A already at 9, F at 6. Can crash A by 2 more (9→7) and F by 1 more (6→5). Cost = 2(1000) + 1(1000) = 3,000. Saving = 500. Net = +2,500. **If B+D:** B by 3 days max (8→5), D by 2 days max (6→4). Cost = 3(1166.67) + 2(1000) = 5500. Worse. **Optimal Final State at 28 days:** - A: 10→8 days, C: 12→9 days, F: 7→6 days - A = 8, B = 8, C = 9, D = 6, E = 6, F = 6 - Path 1: 8+8+6+6 = **28 days** ✓ - Path 2: 8+9+6+6 = **29 days** ❌ Let us correct — need Path 2 also = 28. So crash E is already at minimum. So Path 2 components A, C, E, F: - A is at 8 (after 2 crashes), C at 9, E at 6, F at 6. Sum = 29. - To reduce by 1: cannot crash E further. Must crash A, C, or F. Crash A by 1 more day (8→7). Now A = 7. Cost = +1000. Path 1 becomes 27. Path 2 becomes 28. ✓ **Final Crashed State:** - A: 7 days (crashed 3 times), C: 9 days (crashed 3 times), F: 6 days (crashed 1 time) - Duration = 28 days ✓ **Total Crashing Cost:** - E crashed 3 days: 3 × 833.33 = Rs 2,500 - C crashed 3 days: 3 × 1000 = Rs 3,000 - A crashed 3 days: 3 × 1000 = Rs 3,000 - F crashed 1 day: 1 × 1000 = Rs 1,000 - **Total Extra Direct Cost = Rs 9,500** **Final Cost Calculation:** - Direct Cost = 26,000 + 9,500 = Rs 35,500 - Indirect Cost = 500 × 28 = Rs 14,000 - **Total Project Cost = Rs 49,500** ✅ > **Note for Students:** The "optimum" duration is where total cost (direct + indirect) is minimized. Beyond that, crashing cost exceeds indirect savings. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU University Exam** - ✅ **PERT** = probabilistic, three-time estimates, Beta distribution, used for new/uncertain projects. - ✅ **CPM** = deterministic, single-time estimate, focuses on time-cost trade-off via crashing. - ✅ **Expected Time Formula**: $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ — the "4" is the weight for $t_m$. - ✅ **Variance**: $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ — apply ONLY along the critical path, then sum. - ✅ **Z-value**: $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{proj}}$ — then look up in standard normal table for probability. - ✅ **Forward Pass** uses **MAX** of predecessor EFs for ES computation. - ✅ **Backward Pass** uses **MIN** of successor LSs for LF computation. - ✅ **Critical Path**: Path with **Total Float = 0**. Multiple critical paths possible. - ✅ **Float Hierarchy**: $IF \le FF \le TF$. If TF = 0, then FF = 0 and IF = 0. - ✅ **Cost Slope**: $\dfrac{C_{crash} - C_{normal}}{D_{normal} - D_{crash}}$ — always crash the **lowest slope critical activity first**. - ✅ **Crashing Rule**: When parallel critical paths emerge, you must crash **both** (or all) paths simultaneously. - ✅ **Optimal Duration**: Where the marginal direct cost increase equals marginal indirect cost savings (typically Rs 500/day in KTU problems). - ✅ **Food Plant Applications**: Greenfield construction → PERT; Plant expansion / shutdowns → CPM. - ✅ **Common Z-values to memorize**: $Z_{0.90} = 1.28$, $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $Z_{0.99} = 2.33$. - ✅ **Drawing Networks**: AOA (Activity on Arrow) uses dummy activities for logical dependencies; AON (Activity on Node) does not — preferred in modern CPM software like MS Project / Primavera P6. - ✅ **KTU Examiner Trivia**: Most marks are lost in **(i) arithmetic mistakes in backward pass**, **(ii) forgetting to sum variances**, and **(iii) not showing units in final answer**. Always write "days" or "weeks" explicitly.

topics.title: Application of ISO and HACCP in food plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of ISO and HACCP in food plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 ISO Standards in Food Plant Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (ISO):** The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** is an independent, non-governmental international body that develops voluntary, consensus-based international standards to ensure quality, safety, efficiency, and interoperability of products, services, and systems across industries, including food processing.

For a food plant, the most critical ISO standards are:

| ISO Standard | Focus Area | Relevance to Food Plant |
|--------------|------------|-------------------------|
| **ISO 22000** | Food Safety Management System (FSMS) | Combines HACCP with prerequisite programs (PRPs) |
| **ISO 9001** | Quality Management System (QMS) | Ensures consistent product quality and customer satisfaction |
| **ISO 14001** | Environmental Management System (EMS) | Controls waste, emissions, and sustainability |
| **ISO 45001** | Occupational Health & Safety | Worker safety in food processing environment |
| **ISO 22005** | Traceability in food chain | Farm-to-fork tracking |
| **ISO 17025** | Lab testing competence | Quality control lab accreditation |

## 1.2 HACCP – The Food Safety Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (HACCP):** **Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP)** is a systematic, science-based, preventive approach to food safety that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards (biological, chemical, and physical) that are significant for food safety, throughout the entire production process—from raw material reception to finished product dispatch.

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** developed HACCP principles, and it is now the global gold standard for food safety.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of HACCP as a "Pre-flight Checklist for Food Safety"**
> Just like a pilot goes through a strict pre-flight checklist before takeoff to prevent disasters, HACCP forces the food manufacturer to identify and control every possible "danger point" before, during, and after production. **ISO 22000** is the **"Airline Management System"** — the overall governance that ensures the checklist is followed, documented, and audited regularly.

Imagine a dairy plant producing cheese:
- **HACCP** tells the plant: "Heat milk to 72°C for 15 seconds (Critical Control Point) to kill *Listeria monocytogenes*."
- **ISO 22000** ensures: "Document the heating, train the staff, calibrate the thermometer, audit every month."

Without HACCP, you only react to food poisoning outbreaks. **With HACCP, you prevent them.**

## 1.4 Why These Systems Matter in Plant Layout & Design

The layout of a food plant is not just about workflow efficiency — it directly determines the ability to:
- **Prevent cross-contamination** (e.g., raw vs. cooked zones)
- **Establish unidirectional material flow** (low-risk → high-risk → finished)
- **Locate CCPs physically** (e.g., pasteurizer, metal detector)
- **Comply with hygienic zoning** (High-care / High-risk areas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A poorly designed food plant cannot be "salvaged" by ISO/HACCP paperwork.** True compliance begins at the **layout drawing board**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Principles of HACCP (Codex Alimentarius, 2020 Revised)

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** formally defines **seven principles** that form the foundation of any HACCP system. These principles are universally accepted and are the backbone of ISO 22000.

### Principle 1: Conduct a Hazard Analysis
- Identify **biological** (Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria), **chemical** (pesticides, allergens, cleaning agents), and **physical** (metal, glass, bone) hazards.
- Assess **severity** and **likelihood** of each hazard.

### Principle 2: Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)
- A CCP is a step where control can be applied to **prevent, eliminate, or reduce** a food safety hazard to an acceptable level.
- Use the **CCP Decision Tree** (Codex diagram).

### Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits
- Maximum and/or minimum values to which a biological, chemical, or physical parameter must be controlled at a CCP.
- Examples: temperature 72°C ± 1°C, pH ≤ 4.6, metal detector sensitivity Fe ≤ 2.0 mm.

### Principle 4: Establish Monitoring Procedures
- Define **what, how, when, who**, and **frequency** of monitoring.
- Monitoring must be **real-time** and provide rapid results.

### Principle 5: Establish Corrective Actions
- Pre-planned actions when monitoring shows deviation from critical limits.
- Example: "Reprocess the batch OR divert to non-food use AND document the incident."

### Principle 6: Establish Verification Procedures
- Activities to confirm the HACCP system is working effectively.
- Includes calibration, sampling, testing, audits.

### Principle 7: Establish Record-Keeping and Documentation
- Maintain records of CCP monitoring, deviations, corrective actions, and verification activities.
- Retention period: typically **2 years** or as per regulation.

## 2.2 The 12 Sequential Steps of HACCP Implementation

> [!NOTE]
> Before applying the 7 principles, a plant must complete **5 preliminary tasks**:

1. **Assemble the HACCP Team** — Multidisciplinary (QA, production, engineering, sanitation).
2. **Describe the Product** — Composition, packaging, shelf-life.
3. **Identify Intended Use** — Consumer group (infants, immunocompromised, general).
4. **Construct the Process Flow Diagram** — From raw material receipt to dispatch.
5. **Verify the Flow Diagram On-Site** — Walk the plant floor.
6. **(Apply Principles 1–7)**
7. **Review the HACCP Plan** — Periodic review and update.

## 2.3 ISO 22000 Structure (High-Level)

ISO 22000:2018 uses the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** shared by all ISO management system standards:

| Clause | Title | Purpose |
|--------|-------|---------|
| 4 | Context of the Organization | Define scope, interested parties |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment |
| 6 | Planning | Address risks and opportunities |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information |
| 8 | Operation | **Operational planning, PRPs, HACCP plan** |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review |
| 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although this topic is qualitative, the following quantitative parameters are **frequently tested** in KTU examinations for HACCP decisions:

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Likelihood}
$$

$$
\text{Critical Limit Range} = [\text{Min CL},\ \text{Max CL}]
$$

$$
\text{Metal Detector Sensitivity} = \begin{cases} \text{Fe} \leq 2.0\ \text{mm} \\ \text{Non-Fe} \leq 2.5\ \text{mm} \\ \text{SS} \leq 3.0\ \text{mm} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Cross-Contamination Index} = \frac{\text{Pathogen CFU in finished product}}{\text{Pathogen CFU in raw material}} \times 100\ \%
$$

$$
\text{Audit Compliance Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of conformities}}{\text{Total number of audit points}} \times 100\ \%
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **FSSAI** (India) mandates HACCP for high-risk food categories.
- **US FDA** requires HACCP for seafood, juice, meat, and dairy.
- **EU Regulation 852/2004** mandates HACCP-based procedures.
- **Export compliance** — Most importing countries demand ISO 22000 + HACCP certification.
- **Insurance and liability** — HACCP-certified plants have lower insurance premiums.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hazard Analysis Decision Logic (Worked Example)

### Problem Setup
A fruit juice plant produces pasteurized mango pulp. The plant receives raw mangoes, washes them, sorts, extracts pulp, pasteurizes (85°C, 15 sec), fills into aseptic bags, and stores in cold room. Identify potential hazards and CCPs.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: List all process steps and their potential hazards**

$$
\text{Process} = \{R, W, S, E, P, F, C\}
$$

where $R$ = raw receipt, $W$ = washing, $S$ = sorting, $E$ = extraction, $P$ = pasteurization, $F$ = filling, $C$ = cold storage.

| Step | Biological Hazard | Chemical Hazard | Physical Hazard |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| R | *Salmonella*, *E. coli* on peel | Pesticide residue | Stones, twigs |
| W | Cross-contamination in wash water | Chlorine residue | — |
| S | Microbial spread from damaged fruit | — | Glass from lamps |
| E | Microbial proliferation | Lubricant leak | Metal from cutter |
| P | **Surviving pathogens if temp inadequate** | — | — |
| F | Post-pasteurization recontamination | Sealant migration | — |
| C | Psychrotrophic growth | — | — |

**Step 2: Apply Risk Score = Severity × Likelihood (each on 1–4 scale)**

$$
\text{RS}_{R} = 4 \times 4 = 16
$$

$$
\text{RS}_{P} = 4 \times 3 = 12
$$

$$
\text{RS}_{F} = 3 \times 3 = 9
$$

**Step 3: Apply the CCP Decision Tree (Codex)**

For Step P (Pasteurization):
- Q1: Do preventive control measures exist? **Yes** (heat treatment).
- Q2: Is the step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard? **Yes**.
- → **CCP** identified at pasteurization.
- Critical limits: $T \geq 85\ ^\circ C$ for $t \geq 15$ seconds.

For Step F (Aseptic Filling):
- Q1: Preventive control? **Yes** (sterile environment).
- Q2: Specifically designed for hazard control? **Yes** (prevents recontamination).
- → **CCP** at aseptic filler (sealing integrity, environment sterility).

**Step 4: Determine Critical Limits for each CCP**

$$
\text{CCP}_{P} : \begin{cases} \text{Temperature:}\ 85\ ^\circ C \leq T \leq 95\ ^\circ C \\ \text{Holding time:}\ t \geq 15\ \text{s} \\ \text{Flow rate:}\ \leq 5000\ \text{L/h} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{CCP}_{F} : \begin{cases} \text{Seal pressure:}\ P \geq 2.5\ \text{bar} \\ \text{Environment:}\ \text{Class 100 (ISO 5)} \text{cleanroom} \\ \text{Filler steam:}\ 121\ ^\circ C \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: Monitoring Procedure**

$$
\text{Monitoring Frequency} = \frac{\text{Batch size}}{\text{Sensor reading interval}}
$$

For example, if batch = 10,000 L and sensor logs every 5 minutes:

$$
\text{Records per batch} = \frac{10{,}000\ \text{L}}{5{,}000\ \text{L/h}} \times 60 \div 5 = 24\ \text{records}
$$

**Step 6: Corrective Action Tree**

$$
\text{If}\ T < 85\ ^\circ C \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Hold product} \\ \text{Re-pasteurize} \\ \text{Document incident} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 7: Verification**

$$
\text{Verification Activity} = \text{Calibration} + \text{Plate Count Testing} + \text{Audit}
$$

$$
\text{Plate Count Acceptance} : \text{TPC} \leq 10{,}000\ \text{CFU/g},\ \text{Yeast \& Mold} \leq 100\ \text{CFU/g}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: CCP Identification for Common Food Processes

| Food Process | Critical Control Point | Critical Limit | Monitoring |
|--------------|------------------------|----------------|------------|
| Pasteurized milk | Pasteurizer | 72°C / 15 s | Continuous temp recorder |
| Canned food | Retort | F₀ ≥ 3 min | Time-temperature integrator |
| Baked bread | Metal detector | Fe ≤ 2.0 mm | Every 30 min test piece |
| Ready-to-eat meat | Chilling tunnel | T ≤ 4°C | Continuous probe |
| Pickled product | Fermentation | pH ≤ 4.6 | Daily pH meter |
| Edible oil refining | Filtration | Free fatty acids ≤ 0.1% | Lab titration |
| Carbonated beverage | Carbonator | CO₂ pressure 2.5–3.0 bar | Pressure gauge |
| Ice cream | Freezer | T ≤ –25°C | Probe every 2 h |

## 3.3 ISO + HACCP Integration Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 22000 = HACCP + PRPs + Management System**
> ISO 22000 does not replace HACCP; it **absorbs HACCP as a mandatory component** within a broader management framework that includes **Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)** and **Operational Prerequisite Programs (OPRPs)**.

$$
\text{ISO 22000} = \underbrace{\text{Management System (PDCA)}}_{\text{ISO 9001 style}} + \underbrace{\text{PRPs + OPRPs}}_{\text{Good Manufacturing Practices}} + \underbrace{\text{HACCP Plan}}_{\text{7 Principles}}
$$

where PDCA = **Plan–Do–Check–Act** cycle (Deming cycle).

$$
\text{PDCA Cycle} = \begin{cases} \text{Plan: Establish objectives, PRPs, HACCP} \\ \text{Do: Implement and operate the FSMS} \\ \text{Check: Monitor, measure, audit} \\ \text{Act: Review, improve, correct} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Cross-Contamination Index

For a meat processing plant receiving raw meat and producing cooked products:

$$
\text{CCI} = \frac{C_f - C_0}{C_i - C_0} \times 100\ \%
$$

where $C_i$ = initial CFU of pathogen in raw material, $C_0$ = detection limit, $C_f$ = final CFU in finished product.

If $C_i = 10^5$ CFU/g, $C_f = 10^2$ CFU/g, and $C_0 = 10$ CFU/g:

$$
\text{CCI} = \frac{10^2 - 10^1}{10^5 - 10^1} \times 100 = \frac{90}{99{,}990} \times 100 \approx 0.09\ \%
$$

This means the plant has achieved **99.91% reduction** in cross-contamination, indicating a well-designed layout and effective CCP control.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HACCP Flow in Food Plant — Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HACCP Team Assembly] --> B[Product Description]
    B --> C[Intended Use Identification]
    C --> D[Process Flow Diagram]
    D --> E[On-Site Verification]
    E --> F[Principle 1: Hazard Analysis]
    F --> G[Principle 2: Identify CCPs]
    G --> H[Principle 3: Set Critical Limits]
    H --> I[Principle 4: Monitoring Procedures]
    I --> J[Principle 5: Corrective Actions]
    J --> K[Principle 6: Verification]
    K --> L[Principle 7: Documentation]
    L --> M[HACCP Plan Established]
    M --> N[Periodic Review and Update]

    F1{Biological?} -->|Yes| F2[Identify Pathogen]
    F1 -->|No| F3{Chemical?}
    F3 -->|Yes| F4[Identify Toxin]
    F3 -->|No| F5[Physical Hazard]
    F2 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
```

## 4.2 ISO 22000 Framework Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Clause 4: Context]
        A1[Interested Parties]
        A2[Scope of FSMS]
    end

    subgraph B[Clause 5: Leadership]
        B1[Top Management Commitment]
        B2[Food Safety Policy]
    end

    subgraph C[Clause 6: Planning]
        C1[Risk Assessment]
        C2[Objectives]
    end

    subgraph D[Clause 7: Support]
        D1[Resources and Competence]
        D2[Documented Information]
    end

    subgraph E[Clause 8: Operation]
        E1[PRPs and OPRPs]
        E2[HACCP Plan]
        E3[Critical Control Points]
    end

    subgraph F[Clause 9: Performance Evaluation]
        F1[Monitoring]
        F2[Internal Audit]
        F3[Management Review]
    end

    subgraph G[Clause 10: Improvement]
        G1[Nonconformity]
        G2[Continual Improvement]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    E2 --> E3
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
    G1 --> G2
```

## 4.3 Food Plant Hygienic Zoning Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Zone1[Zone 1: High Risk Area]
        Z1A[Raw Material Receiving]
        Z1B[Storage of Raw Materials]
    end

    subgraph Zone2[Zone 2: Medium Risk Area]
        Z2A[Processing Hall]
        Z2B[Intermediate Storage]
    end

    subgraph Zone3[Zone 3: High Care Area]
        Z3A[CCP Operations]
        Z3B[Metal Detection]
    end

    subgraph Zone4[Zone 4: Finished Product Zone]
        Z4A[Packaging]
        Z4B[Cold Storage]
        Z4C[Dispatch]
    end

    Z1A -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z2A
    Z1B -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z2A
    Z2A -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z3A
    Z2B -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z3A
    Z3A -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z3B
    Z3B -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z4A
    Z4A -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z4B
    Z4B -->|Unidirectional Flow| Z4C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Principle:** Arrows in the diagram above represent the **unidirectional material flow** mandated by HACCP and ISO 22000. **No backflow** is permitted from a higher-hygiene zone to a lower-hygiene zone. This is a **frequently tested concept** in KTU examinations.

## 4.4 Decision Tree for CCP Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Q1: Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?}
    Q1 -->|No| NOT_CCP1[Not a CCP — Modify process]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| CCP[Critical Control Point]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Q3: Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable levels or increase to unacceptable levels?}
    Q3 -->|No| NOT_CCP2[Not a CCP]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard?}
    Q4 -->|No| CCP
    Q4 -->|Yes| NOT_CCP3[Not a CCP]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define HACCP. List its seven principles.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** is a systematic, science-based preventive system that ensures food safety by identifying, evaluating, and controlling hazards that are significant for food safety.

**Seven Principles of HACCP:**

1. Conduct a hazard analysis
2. Determine the Critical Control Points (CCPs)
3. Establish critical limits
4. Establish monitoring procedures
5. Establish corrective actions
6. Establish verification procedures
7. Establish record-keeping and documentation procedures

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for listing principles)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between ISO 22000 and ISO 9001.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ISO 22000 | ISO 9001 |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Primary Focus | **Food Safety Management** | **Quality Management** |
| Scope | Food chain organizations | All industries |
| Mandatory Element | HACCP plan included | HACCP not mandatory |
| Hazard Control | Yes (biological, chemical, physical) | Not specifically required |
| Prerequisite Programs | Required (PRPs and OPRPs) | Not specifically mandated |
| Sector | Food and feed | Generic |

*(3 marks — table + one-line distinction)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the seven principles of HACCP in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of ISO 22000 in establishing a food safety management system in a food processing plant. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **seven principles of HACCP** as defined by the Codex Alimentarius Commission are:

**1. Hazard Analysis:**
- Identify potential **biological** (*Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *E. coli*), **chemical** (pesticides, allergens, cleaning agents), and **physical** (metal, glass, stone) hazards at every process step.
- Assess **severity** (consequence) and **likelihood** (probability) to determine significance.

**2. Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs):**
- A CCP is a step at which control can be applied to prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level.
- Apply the **Codex CCP Decision Tree**.
- Example: Pasteurization in a milk plant, retort sterilization in canning.

**3. Establish Critical Limits:**
- Quantitative criteria separating **acceptable** from **unacceptable** values.
- Examples: $T \geq 72\ ^\circ C$ for $t \geq 15$ s, pH $\leq$ 4.6, salt $\geq$ 10% in cured meat, metal detector Fe $\leq$ 2.0 mm.

**4. Establish Monitoring Procedures:**
- Define **what** is measured, **how** (instrument), **when** (frequency), and **who** (responsible person).
- Examples: Continuous temperature chart recorder, periodic pH probe, hourly metal detector test.

**5. Establish Corrective Actions:**
- Pre-determined actions when a critical limit is exceeded.
- Actions include: (i) isolate affected product, (ii) re-process or destroy, (iii) restore control, (iv) document the deviation.

**6. Establish Verification Procedures:**
- Confirm that the HACCP system is functioning effectively.
- Includes: equipment calibration, sampling and lab testing, internal audits, review of records.

**7. Record-Keeping and Documentation:**
- Maintain evidence of all monitoring, deviations, corrective actions, and verification.
- Retention: typically 2 years or as per regulatory requirement.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Role of ISO 22000 in a Food Plant:**

1. **Integrates HACCP into a Management System:**
ISO 22000:2018 embeds the **HACCP plan** within a broader **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)**, following the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** used across all ISO management standards.

2. **Establishes Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) and OPRPs:**
- PRPs cover: cleaning, sanitation, pest control, personal hygiene, supplier control, equipment maintenance, training.
- OPRPs address significant hazards that are controlled by measures but may not be classified as CCPs.

3. **Applies the PDCA Cycle:**
- **Plan:** Identify risks, establish objectives, design FSMS.
- **Do:** Implement PRPs, OPRPs, and HACCP.
- **Check:** Monitor, audit, review.
- **Act:** Correct deviations and improve continuously.

4. **Ensures Top Management Commitment:**
- Clause 5 mandates leadership responsibility, food safety policy, and resource allocation.

5. **Enables Certification and Market Access:**
- ISO 22000 certification is recognized globally and facilitates export to EU, US, Japan, and Middle East markets.

6. **Supports Risk-Based Thinking:**
- Clause 6 requires the organization to identify risks and opportunities, promoting proactive prevention rather than reactive control.

7. **Documentation and Traceability:**
- Mandates **documented information** for all critical processes, ensuring transparency and accountability.
- Complements **ISO 22005** (traceability) and **ISO 17025** (lab competence).

**Real-World Example:** A seafood export plant in Kerala implementing ISO 22000 reduces customer complaints by 60% and achieves EU export approval within 12 months.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the application of HACCP in a dairy plant. Identify the CCPs and establish their critical limits. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the application of ISO 9001 and ISO 14001 in a food processing plant. How do they complement HACCP? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**HACCP Application in a Dairy Plant:**

**Process Flow:** Raw milk reception → Chilling → Filtration/Clarification → Standardization → Pasteurization → Homogenization → Cooling → Packaging → Cold storage → Dispatch.

| Step | Hazard | Risk Score | CCP? |
|------|--------|------------|------|
| Raw milk reception | Pathogens, antibiotics | 16 | No (controlled by supplier) |
| Pasteurization | Survival of pathogens | 12 | **YES (CCP-1)** |
| Aseptic packaging | Post-process contamination | 9 | **YES (CCP-2)** |
| Cold storage | Psychrotrophic growth | 8 | OPRP |

**Critical Limits for CCP-1 (Pasteurization):**

$$
T \geq 72\ ^\circ C\ \text{for}\ t \geq 15\ \text{s}\ \text{(HTST method)}
$$

**Critical Limits for CCP-2 (Packaging):**

$$
\text{Seal integrity:}\ \text{Leak test} \leq 0.1\ \%\ \text{failure};\ \text{Environment: ISO 7 cleanroom}
$$

**Monitoring:** Continuous chart recorder for temperature; hourly seal strength test.

**Corrective Action:** Deviation → divert milk, re-pasteurize, document, review cause.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**ISO 9001 in a Food Plant:**
- Establishes a **Quality Management System (QMS)** ensuring consistent product quality.
- Focus on **customer satisfaction, process approach, and continual improvement**.
- Application: Documented SOPs, calibration of weighing scales, batch traceability, complaint handling.

**ISO 14001 in a Food Plant:**
- Establishes an **Environmental Management System (EMS)** controlling environmental impacts.
- Application: Effluent treatment plant monitoring, waste segregation, energy efficiency, reduction of carbon footprint.
- Example: Setting targets for **water reuse ≥ 30%** and **effluent BOD ≤ 100 mg/L**.

**Complementarity with HACCP:**

$$
\text{HACCP} \cup \text{ISO 9001} \cup \text{ISO 14001} = \text{Integrated Food Plant Management}
$$

- **HACCP** = Food safety (biological/chemical/physical hazards).
- **ISO 9001** = Product quality and customer satisfaction.
- **ISO 14001** = Environmental sustainability and regulatory compliance.
- **ISO 45001** = Worker safety.

Together, they form a **Total Quality, Safety, and Environmental Management (TQSEM)** framework.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "HACCP plan" with "HACCP principles."** A plan includes the team, flow diagram, hazards, CCPs, limits, monitoring, corrective actions, verification, and records. The principles are **7 specific items**, not the whole plan.
> 2. **Writing "ISO 22000 replaces HACCP."** This is **wrong**. ISO 22000 *includes* HACCP as a mandatory component.
> 3. **Skipping prerequisite programs (PRPs).** Without PRPs, HACCP fails. Always mention cleaning, sanitation, personal hygiene, pest control.
> 4. **Forgetting "unidirectional flow" in layout questions.** This is a **favourite KTU question** — link ISO/HACCP to plant layout by stating that cross-flows violate the "no cross-contamination" rule.
> 5. **Omitting critical limit units.** Writing "high temperature" scores zero; writing "$72\ ^\circ C$ for 15 s" scores full marks.
> 6. **Writing vague corrective actions.** Always specify: *isolate product → identify cause → re-process or destroy → record → review*.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

### Core Concepts
- **HACCP** = preventive food safety system; **ISO 22000** = management system that includes HACCP.
- **7 Principles of HACCP** = Hazard analysis → CCPs → Critical limits → Monitoring → Corrective action → Verification → Records.
- **12 Steps of HACCP Implementation** = 5 preliminary tasks + 7 principles.
- **PRPs** are **mandatory** before HACCP can be applied.
- **OPRPs** control significant hazards but are not CCPs.

### Plant Layout Implications
- Establish **unidirectional material flow** (raw → process → finished → dispatch).
- Create **hygienic zoning** (high-care areas, separate handwashing stations, dedicated equipment for raw vs. cooked).
- **Locate CCPs physically** — pasteurizer, retort, metal detector — with adequate space for monitoring and sampling.
- Provide **separate entry/exit** for raw and finished product zones.
- Design **airflow, drainage, and traffic patterns** to prevent cross-contamination.

### ISO Family
- **ISO 22000** — Food Safety (includes HACCP)
- **ISO 9001** — Quality Management
- **ISO 14001** — Environmental Management
- **ISO 45001** — Occupational Health & Safety
- **ISO 22005** — Traceability
- **ISO 17025** — Lab competence

### Quantitative Parameters to Memorize
- Pasteurization: **72°C / 15 s** (HTST) or **63°C / 30 min** (LTLT).
- Metal detection limits: **Fe ≤ 2.0 mm**, Non-Fe ≤ 2.5 mm, SS ≤ 3.0 mm.
- Pickle safety: **pH ≤ 4.6**.
- Canning: **F₀ ≥ 3 min** (botulinum cook).
- Cold storage: **≤ 4°C** (chilled), **≤ –18°C** (frozen).

### Key Terminology
- **CCP** = Critical Control Point
- **CL** = Critical Limit
- **PRP** = Prerequisite Program
- **OPRP** = Operational Prerequisite Program
- **FSMS** = Food Safety Management System
- **PDCA** = Plan–Do–Check–Act
- **HLS** = High-Level Structure

### Favourite KTU Questions
- "Explain 7 principles of HACCP." (7 marks)
- "Apply HACCP to ___ plant." (14 marks)
- "Differentiate ISO 22000 and ISO 9001." (3 marks)
- "How does HACCP influence food plant layout?" (5–7 marks)
- "List the prerequisite programs for HACCP." (3 marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimentation in pilot plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Experimentation in pilot plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **pilot plant** is a small-scale, intermediate processing system designed to reproduce the performance and produce the products of an envisioned commercial (industrial) plant. The process of **experimentation in a pilot plant** refers to the systematic, controlled, and statistically valid investigative trials conducted within this intermediate facility to:

1. Validate laboratory-scale findings.
2. Generate engineering data for **scale-up** to industrial production.
3. Evaluate product quality, process variables, and equipment performance under near-commercial conditions.
4. Identify potential operational, safety, and economic bottlenecks prior to capital investment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Experimentation in pilot plants is the **engineering bridge** between bench-top R&D (typically 1–10 kg batches) and full commercial manufacturing (tonnes/hour). It is mandatory for KTU students to understand its link to process design, plant layout, and economic feasibility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Test"

Think of a chef who invents a new sauce in a small kitchen bowl.

- **Laboratory Stage:** The chef tastes a teaspoon — pure chemistry and sensory reaction.
- **Pilot Plant Stage:** The chef cooks the sauce in a 50-litre industrial pot on a trial stove to see how heat distributes, how stirring scales, and whether the recipe holds at larger volumes.
- **Commercial Plant Stage:** The chef scales it up to a 5000-litre production kettle.

The pilot plant is the **"50-litre industrial pot"** — too big for a lab spoon, too small for a factory. It is where the recipe is *engineered*, not just tasted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A pilot plant is **not** a prototype in the mechanical sense. It is a *data-generation facility*. Its primary output is not product — it is **information** (mass/energy balances, kinetics, economics, sensory data).

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

Pilot plants are conventionally classified by their **throughput capacity ratio** relative to the commercial plant:

| Class | Capacity Relative to Commercial Plant | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bench-scale Pilot** | 1 : 1000 to 1 : 10,000 | Initial screening, kinetic studies |
| **Mini-Pilot Plant** | 1 : 100 to 1 : 1000 | Process refinement, equipment selection |
| **Demonstration Plant** | 1 : 10 to 1 : 100 | Customer sampling, final validation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Scale-up Factor (SF):**
> $$\text{Scale-up Factor} = \frac{\text{Capacity}_{\text{commercial}}}{\text{Capacity}_{\text{pilot}}}$$
> For food plants, **SF is typically between 100 and 10,000**, depending on product and unit operation.

## 1.4 Purpose & Objectives of Pilot Plant Experimentation

The following are the canonical objectives aligned with KTU 2024 OBE (Outcome-Based Education) Course Outcomes:

1. **CO1 Knowledge:** Obtain engineering data for design of commercial plant equipment (heat transfer coefficients, residence times, pressure drops).
2. **CO2 Comprehension:** Identify critical process variables and their interactions.
3. **CO3 Application:** Test alternative raw materials and formulations.
4. **CO4 Analysis:** Develop mass and energy balances for the full-scale operation.
5. **CO5 Evaluation:** Estimate capital and operating costs.
6. **CO6 Creation (Synthesis):** Produce representative finished product samples for market testing.

---

## 1.5 Limitations & Pitfalls of Pilot Plant Trials

> [!WARNING]
> Pilot plant data, if extrapolated blindly, can mislead designers. Common limitations include:
> - **Material handling differences** (viscosity, particle behaviour change with volume).
> - **Wall effects** dominate small vessels (surface-to-volume ratio is ~10× larger than commercial).
> - **Operator skill variability** is higher in pilot settings.
> - **Non-continuous operation** — most pilot runs are batch, while commercial may be continuous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Experimental Methodology — Six Sequential Phases

The experimentation process in a pilot plant follows a structured engineering methodology:

### Phase 1 — Problem Definition
- Define the **hypothesis** (e.g., "Increasing residence time from 30 to 60 min will reduce browning index by 40%").
- Identify **Independent Variables (IVs)**: Temperature, time, concentration, pH.
- Identify **Dependent Variables (DVs)**: Yield, color, texture, microbial count.
- Identify **Controlled Variables (CVs)**: Raw material batch, ambient humidity.

### Phase 2 — Design of Experiments (DoE)
Statistical planning to minimise the number of runs while maximising information.

- **Full Factorial Design:** All combinations of variables tested.
  - Number of runs = $n^k$, where $n$ = levels, $k$ = factors.
- **Fractional Factorial Design:** Half or quarter fraction of full factorial.
- **Response Surface Methodology (RSM):** Uses second-order polynomial model.
- **Taguchi Orthogonal Arrays:** Robust design with minimum runs.

### Phase 3 — Equipment Setup & Calibration
- All instruments (thermocouples, flow meters, pressure transducers) must be **calibrated against NIST-traceable standards**.
- Documentation of piping & instrumentation diagram (**P&ID**).
- Sanitation verification (CIP — Clean-In-Place validation).

### Phase 4 — Execution & Data Logging
- Each experimental run must be **replicated** (minimum 3 replicates for statistical validity).
- Raw data must include ambient conditions and operator notes.

### Phase 5 — Data Analysis & Interpretation
- Apply **ANOVA** (Analysis of Variance) to identify significant factors.
- Determine **regression coefficients**.
- Calculate **coefficients of determination** ($R^2$) to assess model fit.

### Phase 6 — Scale-up Recommendation
- Translate pilot data into **engineering design equations** for the commercial plant.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Scale-up Theory

The mathematical foundation of pilot-plant experimentation rests on four similarity principles:

### 2.2.1 Geometric Similarity
All corresponding linear dimensions scale by a constant ratio $R$:
$$\frac{D_{\text{pilot}}}{D_{\text{commercial}}} = \frac{H_{\text{pilot}}}{H_{\text{commercial}}} = \frac{L_{\text{pilot}}}{L_{\text{commercial}}} = R$$

### 2.2.2 Kinematic Similarity
Velocities at corresponding points are in the same ratio:
$$\frac{V_{\text{pilot}}}{V_{\text{commercial}}} = \text{constant}$$

### 2.2.3 Dynamic Similarity
Ratios of all forces (inertial, viscous, gravitational, surface tension) at corresponding points are equal. This is quantified through **dimensionless numbers**.

### 2.2.4 Thermal & Chemical Similarity
Temperature profiles and concentration profiles match between scales (essential for food reactions like Maillard browning, enzyme inactivation).

## 2.3 Critical Dimensionless Numbers for Food Pilot Plants

| Dimensionless Number | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | $\text{Re}$ | Inertial vs viscous forces | Mixing, flow in pipes |
| **Prandtl Number** | $\text{Pr}$ | Momentum vs thermal diffusivity | Heat transfer |
| **Nusselt Number** | $\text{Nu}$ | Convective vs conductive heat transfer | Heat exchangers |
| **Peclet Number** | $\text{Pe}$ | Convection vs diffusion | Continuous reactors |
| **Power Number** | $\text{Po}$ | Power draw characteristic | Agitators |
| **Froude Number** | $\text{Fr}$ | Inertial vs gravitational | Vortex formation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Experimentation & Scale-up Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **all key formulas** required for solving KTU university exam problems on pilot plant experimentation. Master these and their units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | Laminar if $\text{Re} < 2100$ (pipe flow) |
| 2 | Power Number (Agitation) | $\text{Po} = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5}$ | Valid when geometric similarity holds |
| 3 | Power Law for Scale-up | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^a \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^b \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}\right)^c$ | Exponents depend on regime |
| 4 | Constant Power per Unit Volume | $\dfrac{P_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{V_2}$ | Common scale-up criterion for mixing |
| 5 | Constant Tip Speed | $\pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2$ | Used for blenders, emulsifiers |
| 6 | Constant Impeller Tip Speed (alt) | $N \propto D^{-1}$ | When shear-sensitive product is involved |
| 7 | Volumetric Scale-up Ratio | $V_2 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$ | Geometrically similar vessels |
| 8 | Heat Transfer Scale-up | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ | $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ is log-mean temperature difference |
| 9 | LMTD Formula | $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-current flow |
| 10 | Scale-up Factor | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{commercial}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{pilot}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 11 | Reynolds Similarity Criterion | $\text{Re}_{\text{pilot}} = \text{Re}_{\text{commercial}}$ | For dynamic similarity |
| 12 | Nusselt Number (Dittus-Boelter) | $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{0.4}$ | Turbulent flow in pipes, heating |
| 13 | Mass Transfer Coefficient (Sherwood) | $\text{Sh} = \dfrac{k_c \, D}{D_{AB}}$ | Analogous to Nu in mass transfer |
| 14 | Process Yield | $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{product}}}{m_{\text{raw, theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as percentage |
| 15 | Residence Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}}$ | Mean fluid residence time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state **units** in your final numerical answers. A value of "100" without units is incomplete and may lose marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Pilot Plant Experimentation

| Food Industry Sector | Pilot Plant Experiment Example |
|---|---|
| **Dairy** | Ultra-high temperature (UHT) milk processing, cheese vat design |
| **Bakery** | Continuous bread dough mixing and proofing |
| **Beverages** | Pasteurisation kinetics for fruit juices |
| **Meat** | Tumbling, massaging, and curing kinetics |
| **Confectionery** | Crystallisation control of chocolate tempering |
| **Frozen Foods** | Freezing time prediction and blast freezer design |

## 2.6 Critical Success Factors

1. **Reproducibility** — same input must yield same output within statistical tolerance.
2. **Instrumentation accuracy** — at least ±0.5% of full scale.
3. **Material balance closure** — within ±5% is acceptable for food pilot plants.
4. **Sampling protocol** — composite sampling recommended over grab sampling.
5. **Documentation** — every run must have a signed batch record.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Scale-up of Vessel Volume

**Problem Context:** A pilot plant mixer has an impeller diameter $D_1 = 0.3 \text{ m}$. The commercial mixer must have a diameter $D_2 = 1.2 \text{ m}$. Assuming geometric similarity, calculate the volume scale-up ratio and mass of product (assuming $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

**Step 1: Establish the geometric scale ratio.**
The linear scale ratio is defined as:
$$R = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{1.2}{0.3} = 4$$

**Step 2: Apply volumetric similarity.**
For geometrically similar vessels, the volume scales as the cube of the linear dimension:
$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot R^3$$
$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot (4)^3 = V_1 \cdot 64$$

**Step 3: Express the volume ratio.**
$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = 64$$

**Step 4: Assume pilot vessel capacity.**
If $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ (50 litres), then:
$$V_2 = 0.05 \times 64 = 3.2 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Compute mass of product.**
$$m_2 = \rho \cdot V_2 = 1050 \times 3.2 = 3360 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**
A 4× increase in linear dimensions results in a **64× increase in volume** and therefore a **64× increase in throughput capacity**. This is the most fundamental scale-up relation in food plant engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Requirement Scale-up Under Constant Power per Volume Criterion

**Problem Context:** A pilot mixer for fruit pulp ($D_1 = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $N_1 = 300 \text{ rpm}$, $\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $P_1 = 0.5 \text{ kW}$) is to be scaled to $D_2 = 1.6 \text{ m}$. Compute the required power if the **criterion is constant power per unit volume** ($P/V$ = const).

**Step 1: Apply the constant P/V criterion.**
$$\frac{P_1}{V_1} = \frac{P_2}{V_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the volumetric scale-up ratio.**
Since $\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3 = (1.6/0.4)^3 = 64$:
$$P_2 = 0.5 \times 64 = 32 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Verify the rotational speed.**
Under geometric similarity with constant tip speed:
$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_2} = 300 \times \frac{0.4}{1.6} = 75 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the new power using the Power Number.**
The Power Number is constant under turbulent regime with geometric similarity:
$$\text{Po}_1 = \text{Po}_2 = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5}$$

So the alternative derivation is:
$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$
$$P_2 = 0.5 \times (4)^5 \times \left(\frac{75}{300}\right)^3$$
$$P_2 = 0.5 \times 1024 \times (0.25)^3 = 0.5 \times 1024 \times 0.015625$$
$$P_2 = 8.0 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inconsistency Resolution:** The two methods give different answers ($32$ vs $8.0$ kW) because they use **different scale-up criteria**. The constant $P/V$ criterion yields $32$ kW (more conservative — safer for design). The constant tip-speed criterion yields $8.0$ kW. The KTU examiner will expect you to **explicitly state** which criterion is used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Transfer Scale-up for a Pasteuriser

**Problem Context:** A pilot plate heat exchanger pasteurises milk from $72 \text{ °C}$ to $4 \text{ °C}$ (cooling side hot-end $\Delta T_1 = 72 - 4 = 68 \text{ °C}$, cold-end $\Delta T_2 = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ °C}$ where cold fluid enters at $2 \text{ °C}$). Calculate the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD).

**Step 1: Identify end temperatures.**
$$\Delta T_1 = 72 - 4 = 68 \text{ °C} \quad \text{(hot end)}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ °C} \quad \text{(cold end)}$$

**Step 2: Apply LMTD formula.**
$$\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$
$$\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \frac{68 - 2}{\ln(68/2)} = \frac{66}{\ln(34)}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate logarithm.**
$$\ln(34) = 3.526$$
$$\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \frac{66}{3.526} = 18.72 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4: Use this LMTD in heat duty equation.**
For a commercial unit requiring $Q = 150{,}000 \text{ W}$ with $U = 2500 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$:
$$A_{\text{required}} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}} = \frac{150{,}000}{2500 \times 18.72}$$
$$A_{\text{required}} = 3.205 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Pilot plant cross-check.**
If pilot unit achieves $Q = 1500 \text{ W}$ with $A_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$ at the same $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$:
$$U_{\text{pilot}} = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}} = \frac{1500}{0.05 \times 18.72} = 1602 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

This lower $U$ value compared to commercial is **expected** due to different flow distribution and fouling — a key pilot-plant insight.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Scale-up Criterion

| Criterion | Equation | When to Use | Risk if Misapplied |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant P/V** | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot (D_2/D_1)^3$ | Liquid blending, fermentation | Over-design for shear-sensitive foods |
| **Constant Tip Speed** | $N_2 = N_1 \cdot (D_1/D_2)$ | Crystallisation, emulsification | Damage to soft solids |
| **Constant Re** | $\text{Re}_1 = \text{Re}_2$ | Flow-dominated processes | Ignores residence time |
| **Constant Shear** | $\dot{\gamma}_2 = \dot{\gamma}_1$ | Non-Newtonian foods | Difficult to measure |
| **Constant $\Delta T$** | $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ | Heat sterilisation | Overheats product |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Yield & Mass Balance Closure in a Tomato Sauce Pilot

**Problem Context:** A pilot run processes $50 \text{ kg}$ fresh tomatoes. Output: $42 \text{ kg}$ sauce and $6 \text{ kg}$ waste peel/seed. Theoretical (max) yield is $88\%$.

**Step 1: Actual yield calculation.**
$$Y_{\text{actual}} = \frac{m_{\text{sauce}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100\% = \frac{42}{50} \times 100\% = 84\%$$

**Step 2: Compare to theoretical.**
$$Y_{\text{relative}} = \frac{Y_{\text{actual}}}{Y_{\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{84}{88} = 95.45\%$$

**Step 3: Mass balance closure.**
$$\text{Total in} = 50 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{Total out} = 42 + 6 = 48 \text{ kg}$$
$$\text{Unaccounted loss} = 50 - 48 = 2 \text{ kg} \quad (4\%)$$
This 4% loss is within the **±5% acceptable closure** for food pilot plants.

**Step 4: Scale-up to 5 tonnes/day commercial.**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{commercial}} = 5000 \times 0.84 = 4200 \text{ kg sauce/day}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Pilot Plant Experimentation Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the sequential decision logic and data flow during pilot plant experiments, from problem definition through scale-up.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem Definition & Hypothesis"]:::start --> B["Identify Variables: IV DV CV"]
    B --> C["Select DoE: Full Factorial or RSM"]
    C --> D["Equipment Calibration & Sanitation"]
    D --> E["Run Pilot Trials with Replicates"]
    E --> F{"Statistical Validity Check p-value less than 0.05"}
    F -->|No| G["Modify Variables or Sample Size"]
    G --> E
    F -->|Yes| H["Mass & Energy Balance Closure"]
    H --> I["Compute Scale-up Ratios SF, P/V, Tip Speed"]
    I --> J["Economic Feasibility Analysis"]
    J --> K{"Scale-up Feasible"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Commercial Plant Design Spec"]
    K -->|No| M["Iterate: Reformulate or Re-experiment"]
    M --> E

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class A start
    class F,K decision
    class B,C,D,E,H,I,J,L,M process
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Yellow diamond at top = process initiation. Blue diamonds = decision gates. Green rectangles = transformation steps. The loop M → E represents the iterative nature of pilot experimentation.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Scale-up Decision Tree (Functional Architecture)

The following block diagram illustrates the architectural flow when selecting a scale-up criterion. Since pilot plant decisions are dependent on multiple engineering criteria, this architecture maps the logical dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Pilot Plant Data Available"]:::input
    P2["Product Type Liquid Solid Powder"]:::input
    P3["Critical Quality Attribute CQA"]:::input

    P1 --> Q1{"Process Type"}
    Q1 -->|Mixing| Q2{"Liquid or Solid"}
    Q1 -->|Heat Transfer| Q3{"Sensitivity to Temp"}
    Q1 -->|Mass Transfer| Q4{"Diffusion Controlled"}

    Q2 -->|Liquid| R1["Use Constant P per V Criterion"]
    Q2 -->|Solid| R2["Use Constant Tip Speed Criterion"]

    Q3 -->|High| R3["Use Constant delta T Criterion"]
    Q3 -->|Low| R4["Use Constant U times A Criterion"]

    Q4 -->|Yes| R5["Use Constant Pe Criterion"]
    Q4 -->|No| R6["Use Constant Re Criterion"]

    P2 --> Q2
    P2 --> Q3
    P3 --> R1
    P3 --> R2
    P3 --> R3
    P3 --> R4
    P3 --> R5
    P3 --> R6

    R1 --> S["Engineering Design Equations for Commercial Plant"]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    R6 --> S

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class P1,P2,P3 input
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 decision
    class S output
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Food Pilot Plant

This block diagram maps the unit operations, utility connections, and information flow within a typical food pilot plant facility.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAW["Raw Material Bay"]
        RM1["Weighing Scale"]:::unit
        RM2["Cold Storage 4 deg C"]:::unit
    end

    subgraph PROC["Processing Hall"]
        PR1["Mixer Reactor"]:::unit
        PR2["Heat Exchanger"]:::unit
        PR3["Separator Filter"]:::unit
        PR4["Dryer or Evaporator"]:::unit
    end

    subgraph QC["Quality Control Lab"]
        QC1["Sensory Panel"]:::unit
        QC2["Instrumental Analysis"]:::unit
        QC3["Microbiological Assay"]:::unit
    end

    subgraph UTIL["Utilities"]
        UT1["Steam Generator"]:::unit
        UT2["Chilled Water"]:::unit
        UT3["Compressed Air"]:::unit
    end

    subgraph DATA["Data Acquisition"]
        DA1["SCADA System"]:::ctrl
        DA2["Electronic Batch Records"]:::ctrl
    end

    RM1 --> PR1
    RM2 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> PR4

    PR1 -.->|Samples| QC1
    PR2 -.->|Samples| QC2
    PR4 -.->|Samples| QC3

    UT1 -.->|Steam| PR2
    UT2 -.->|Cooling| PR2
    UT3 -.->|Air| PR4

    PR1 --> DA1
    PR2 --> DA1
    PR3 --> DA1
    PR4 --> DA1
    DA1 --> DA2

    classDef unit fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class RM1,RM2,PR1,PR2,PR3,PR4,QC1,QC2,QC3,UT1,UT2,UT3 unit
    class DA1,DA2 ctrl
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The dotted lines (.-.>) represent **information/sample flows**, while solid lines (-->) represent **material flows**. Utilities are isolated in their own subgraph to highlight their cross-cutting support role. Data Acquisition is centralised — a hallmark of KTU 2024 Industry 4.0-aligned pilot plant design.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Similarity Principle Mapping for Scale-up
