5. Cooling water is needed only for vapor condensation; sensible heat of concentrate ignored

---

### **OPTION B: QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat flow sheet, describe the **material flow** in a sugar mill processing **2,500 tonnes/day of sugarcane** into refined sugar. Mark the in-process inventories and quality check points.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **steam, water, and electricity requirements** per tonne of sugar for the above plant, given:
- Steam to process ratio: 0.55 kg/kg sugar
- Cooling & process water: 8 m³/tonne cane
- Connected load: 35 kWh/tonne cane
- Plant operates 22 h/day, 80% of the time is productive processing
- Sugar yield from cane: 11%

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION B:**

#### **Part (a) — Sugar Mill Material Flow [7 marks]**

**Flow sheet description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

```
SUGARCANE UNLOADING [2,500 T/day]
        ↓
CANE WASHING & PREPARATION
        ↓
MILLING / DIFFUSER (Juice extraction, 95% efficiency)
        ↓
RAW JUICE SCREENING
        ↓
JUICE HEATING (70°C)
        ↓
CLARIFICATION (Lime + Phosphoric acid)
        ↓
MUD FILTRATION (Vacuum filter)
        ↓
QC POINT 1: Brix, Pol, Purity check
        ↓
MULTI-EFFECT EVAPORATION (5-effect)
        ↓
QC POINT 2: Brix check (60°Bx syrup)
        ↓
VACUUM PAN CRYSTALLIZATION (A, B, C strikes)
        ↓
CENTRIFUGATION
        ↓
QC POINT 3: Crystal size, Moisture
        ↓
DRYING & COOLING
        ↓
SCREENING & GRADING
        ↓
QC POINT 4: Final quality (ICUMSA color, Polarization)
        ↓
BAGGING (50 kg / 1 tonne bags)
        ↓
WAREHOUSE STORAGE
        ↓
DISPATCH
```

**By-products marked on flow sheet:**
- Bagasse → Boiler fuel
- Molasses → Distillery / Animal feed
- Filter mud → Manure / Composting

**In-process inventories:**
- Cane yard buffer: 4 hours
- Clarified juice storage: 2 hours
- Syrup storage: 4 hours
- Massecuite holding: 1 hour
- Sugar silo: 7 days

---

#### **Part (b) — Utility Calculations [7 marks]**

**Sugar production per day:**

$$\dot{m}_{sugar} \;=\; 2{,}500 \; \text{t/day} \times 0.11 \;=\; 275 \; \text{t/day}$$

**Plant productive time:** $22 \times 0.80 = 17.6 \; \text{h/day}$

**Sugar production rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{sugar} \;=\; \frac{275}{17.6} \;=\; 15.625 \; \text{t/h} \;=\; 15{,}625 \; \text{kg/h}$$

**Steam consumption (per tonne sugar):**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} \;=\; 0.55 \; \text{kg/kg} \times 15{,}625 \; \text{kg/h} \;=\; 8{,}593.75 \; \text{kg/h}$$

Per tonne sugar:

$$S_{sp} \;=\; 8{,}593.75 / 15.625 \;=\; \boxed{550 \; \text{kg steam / tonne sugar}}$$

**Water consumption (per tonne sugar):**

Total water per day = $8 \; \text{m}^3/\text{t cane} \times 2{,}500 \; \text{t} = 20{,}000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$

Per tonne sugar:

$$W_{sp} \;=\; 20{,}000 / 275 \;=\; \boxed{72.7 \; \text{m}^3 \; \text{water / tonne sugar}}$$

**Electricity consumption (per tonne sugar):**

Total electricity per day = $35 \; \text{kWh/t} \times 2{,}500 \; \text{t} = 87{,}500 \; \text{kWh/day}$

Per tonne sugar:

$$E_{sp} \;=\; 87{,}500 / 275 \;=\; \boxed{318.2 \; \text{kWh / tonne sugar}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU 2024 ESE on this topic:**
> 
> **1. Confusing Brix with sugar %:** °Brix is total soluble solids, not just sugar. For fruit juice with significant acids, sugar % < °Brix. **Loss: 1-2 marks per question.**
> 
> **2. Forgetting the 0.90 efficiency factor in steam calculations:** Industry efficiency is never 100%. Skipping η = 0.85-0.90 makes the boiler undersized. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> **3. Wrong unit for refrigeration load:** Writing "TR" without converting to kW, or vice versa, when the answer asks for chiller sizing. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> **4. Not stating assumptions explicitly:** KTU examiners award marks specifically for the *Assumptions* section. Always state: (i) steady state, (ii) no solids loss, (iii) 100% separation, (iv) standard CP values. **Loss: 2-3 marks if missing.**
> 
> **5. Confusing process flow sheet with P&ID:** PFD shows *process streams only*; P&ID shows *all pipes, instruments, valves*. Mixing up these definitions costs 1-2 marks.
> 
> **6. Skipping the flow sheet drawing in 14-mark answers:** Even if the question is a calculation, KTU mandates a *neat block diagram* with stream numbers. **Loss: 2-3 marks for missing diagram.**
> 
> **7. Sign convention error in energy balance:** $\sum Q_{in} - \sum Q_{out} = \Delta E_{system}$. For steady state, $\Delta E = 0$. Students often write the wrong sign. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [ ] **Flow sheet** = graphical representation of material + utility movement in a food plant
- [ ] **Two main types:** (1) Material flow sheet (mass balance) and (2) Utility flow sheet (energy balance)
- [ ] **Five critical utilities:** Water, Steam, Electricity, Refrigeration, Compressed Air
- [ ] **Master equation — Mass balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{loss}$
- [ ] **Master equation — Steam rate:** $\dot{m}_{steam} = Q_{req} / (\eta \cdot \lambda_s)$
- [ ] **Master equation — Refrigeration load:** $Q_{ref}[\text{TR}] = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T / (3.517 \times 3{,}600)$
- [ ] **Master equation — Specific consumption:** All "per kg product" or "per tonne product" metrics
- [ ] **Constant 1:** Latent heat of steam at 1 atm = **2,257 kJ/kg**; at 2 bar = **2,201 kJ/kg**
- [ ] **Constant 2:** 1 TR = **3.517 kW** = **12,000 BTU/h** = **3,024 kcal/h**
- [ ] **Pearson Square** for standardization problems (4.5% fat milk → 3.0% toned milk)
- [ ] **°Brix assumption:** ≈ % total soluble solids (for pure sugar solutions)
- [ ] **Industry benchmarks:** Dairy steam 0.10-0.15 kg/kg; Sugar 0.5-0.6 kg/kg; Brewery water 4-6 m³/hL
- [ ] **Efficiency factor** must be included: 0.85-0.90 for boiler, 0.80-0.85 for compressor
- [ ] **P&ID** is more detailed than PFD: includes all instrumentation, valves, control loops
- [ ] **CIP (Clean-In-Place) loop** is essential utility for hygienic food plant design
- [ ] **In-process inventories** (silos, buffer tanks) are integral parts of material flow sheet
- [ ] **By-products** must always be marked on material flow sheet (e.g., bagasse, molasses, whey)
- [ ] **Quality check points** (QC1, QC2, QC3, QC4) align with HACCP CCPs in food plant flow sheet
- [ ] **Flow sheet is the foundation** for plant layout, equipment sizing, cost estimation, and HACCP

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition + Given data** (2 marks)
> 2. **Assumptions** (1 mark)
> 3. **Mass balance / Energy balance calculation** (5-6 marks)
> 4. **Flow sheet diagram** (3-4 marks)
> 5. **Conclusion / summary table** (1-2 marks)
> 
> This template has consistently scored 12+/14 in KTU university exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost estimation for a food plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost estimation for a food plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cost estimation** in the context of a food plant is the systematic, predictive engineering procedure of forecasting the total monetary investment required to design, construct, commission, and operate a complete food processing facility, including all fixed assets (equipment, buildings, land), working capital, and recurring operating expenses over the plant's economic life. In the KTU 2024 scheme, it is treated as the **financial translation of a process flow sheet and plant layout** into capital and operating budgets.

According to standard chemical/food engineering practice adopted in KTU syllabi, cost estimation is classified by the level of project definition as:

| Estimate Class | Accuracy Range | Project Stage |
|---|---|---|
| Order-of-magnitude estimate | ± 40–50 % | Feasibility / pre-conceptual |
| Study (factored) estimate | ± 25–35 % | Conceptual / preliminary design |
| Preliminary (budget) estimate | ± 15–25 % | Engineering / schematic design |
| Definitive estimate | ± 5–15 % | Detailed engineering |
| Detailed estimate | ± 2–5 % | Construction / procurement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The student is expected to apply the **Lang Factor method** and **Hand Factor method** to estimate total capital investment from delivered equipment cost, and to compute **payback period, ROI, NPV, IRR, and break-even point** for a given food plant case.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning to open a **bakery café in Kerala**. Before you even place the oven, you must answer: *How much money do I need to start, and will I earn it back?*

- **Setting up** (rent, oven, mixer, refrigerator, interiors) is your **Capital Investment** — like buying the "skeleton" of the bakery.
- **Daily flour, sugar, electricity, baker's salary** are your **Operating (Manufacturing) Costs** — the fuel that keeps it running every day.
- **Loan interest, property tax, marketing** are your **General Expenses** — the unavoidable overheads.

**Cost estimation is the engineering accountant's crystal ball** that uses historical data from existing bakeries and mathematical factors to predict your bakery's full financial picture *before* you invest a single rupee. For a 100-tonne/day rice mill or a 50,000 L/day dairy plant, the same logic is applied at industrial scale using the **Lang Factor**:

$$C_{TM} = f_L \times C_{EQ}$$

where $C_{TM}$ is total module cost, $C_{EQ}$ is delivered equipment cost, and $f_L$ is the **Lang factor** (a single multiplier that "fattens" the equipment cost into a realistic plant cost).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In food plants, the equipment cost itself is usually only **15 % – 40 %** of the total capital investment. The remaining 60 %–85 % comes from installation, piping, instrumentation, buildings, land, engineering, contingency, and working capital. This is why we *must* use factored methods — never just sum equipment quotations.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Lang factor ($f_L$):** 3.10 for solid processing, 4.74 for fluid processing, 3.63 for mixed (food plants often fall in mixed category).
- **Working capital:** typically **10 % – 20 %** of fixed capital investment.
- **Contingency:** typically **8 % – 15 %** of direct plant cost.
- **Cost index (CEPCI):** used for time-adjustment of historical cost data.
- **Standard units:** ₹ (INR) or $; costs scaled by **plant capacity** using the Six-Tenths Rule:

$$C_B = C_A \left(\frac{S_B}{S_A}\right)^{0.6}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Plant Investment

A food plant's total capital requirement is structured as a hierarchical pyramid. Each layer is derived from the layer below using empirical or semi-empirical factors.

### 2.1.1 Fixed Capital Investment (FCI)

1. **Inside Battery Limits (ISBL) / Process Equipment** – All equipment inside the process block (mixers, heat exchangers, evaporators, reactors, separators, dryers, packaging machines).
2. **Installation** – Labour + foundations + structural supports to erect the equipment (~ 25 %–55 % of $C_{EQ}$).
3. **Piping (Insulation)** – Interconnecting process lines (~ 10 %–80 % of $C_{EQ}$ depending on fluid vs. solid handling).
4. **Instrumentation & Control** – Sensors, PLCs, SCADA (~ 6 %–30 % of $C_{EQ}$).
5. **Electrical** – Motors, cabling, transformers, MCC panels (~ 10 %–15 % of $C_{EQ}$).
6. **Buildings (process + service)** – RCC/PEB structures, flooring, drainage (~ 10 %–70 %).
7. **Yard Improvement** – Roads, fencing, landscaping, fire hydrants (~ 5 %–15 %).
8. **Service Facilities (OSBL)** – Boiler house, refrigeration plant, water treatment, ETP, compressed air, steam distribution (~ 20 %–60 % of $C_{EQ}$).
9. **Engineering & Supervision** – Design, project management, procurement (~ 8 %–15 %).
10. **Construction Expenses & Contingency** – Site office, contractor profit, unforeseen (~ 5 %–15 %).

### 2.1.2 Working Capital (WC)

- Raw material inventory (1–2 months)
- Finished product inventory (1–2 months)
- Accounts receivable (1–2 months)
- Cash reserve for salaries, utilities, freight
- *Less*: Accounts payable to suppliers

$$WC = \text{RM Inventory} + \text{Product Inventory} + \text{Cash} + \text{Receivables} - \text{Payables}$$

### 2.1.3 Manufacturing Cost (MC) / Production Cost

- **Direct Production Costs:** Raw materials, operating labour, supervisory labour, utilities (steam, power, refrigeration, water), maintenance, royalties.
- **Fixed Charges:** Depreciation, local taxes, insurance, rent, interest.
- **Plant Overhead:** Medical, safety, payroll, storage, packaging at plant, R&D, quality control labs.

### 2.1.4 General Expenses (GE)

- Administration
- Sales / marketing / distribution
- Research & development
- Financing (interest on loans)

$$\text{Total Product Cost} = MC + GE$$

$$\text{Sales Revenue} = \text{Total Product Cost} + \text{Profit (before tax)}$$

## 2.2 The Lang Factor Method (1967)

A single empirical multiplier converts the delivered cost of all major process equipment ($C_{EQ}$) into the **total module cost** of an erected plant. The factor implicitly accounts for installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, insulation, and a portion of engineering:

$$C_{TM} = f_L \cdot C_{EQ}$$

| Plant Type | $f_L$ |
|---|---|
| Solid processing (rice mill, flour mill, spice plant) | 3.10 |
| Fluid processing (dairy, beverage, oil refinery) | 4.74 |
| Mixed (bakery, ready-to-eat, confectionery) | 3.63 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The Lang factor does **not** include working capital or off-site facilities. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks in numerical problems.

## 2.3 The Hand Factor Method (1958)

A more granular approach, where each component of investment is a percentage of delivered equipment cost. The total factor is the sum of individual factors:

$$f_{Hand} = (1 + f_{inst} + f_{piping} + f_{inst\&c} + f_{elec} + f_{bldg} + f_{yard} + f_{service} + f_{eng})$$

| Item | Factor ($f$) |
|---|---|
| Equipment (delivered) | 1.00 |
| Installation | 0.40–0.45 |
| Piping | 0.40–0.70 |
| Instrumentation & Control | 0.10–0.20 |
| Electrical | 0.10–0.15 |
| Buildings (process) | 0.15–0.30 |
| Yard Improvement | 0.05–0.10 |
| Service Facilities | 0.30–0.50 |
| Engineering & Supervision | 0.20–0.30 |

## 2.4 Time-Adjustment of Costs (Cost Indexing)

Historical equipment quotations must be inflated to current-day prices using the Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI) or Marshall & Swift index:

$$C_{now} = C_{past} \times \frac{I_{now}}{I_{past}}$$

## 2.5 Capacity Scaling (Six-Tenths Rule)

When scaling a known plant to a new capacity:

$$C_{new} = C_{old} \left(\frac{Q_{new}}{Q_{old}}\right)^{n}$$

where $n \approx 0.6$ for most food process equipment (economy of scale).

## 2.6 Profitability Indicators (The KTU "Financial Four")

### 2.6.1 Simple Payback Period (SPP)

$$SPP = \frac{\text{Fixed Capital Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$$

### 2.6.2 Return on Investment (ROI)

$$ROI\,(\%) = \frac{\text{Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Fixed Capital Investment}} \times 100$$

### 2.6.3 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_0$$

where $CF_t$ is net cash flow in year $t$, $i$ is the discount rate (cost of capital), $C_0$ is initial investment.

### 2.6.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

$IRR$ is the discount rate $i^*$ at which NPV = 0:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = C_0$$

### 2.6.5 Break-Even Point (BEP)

Production volume (in units/year) at which total revenue equals total cost (no profit, no loss):

$$BEP = \frac{F}{p - v}$$

where $F$ = fixed cost per year, $p$ = selling price per unit, $v$ = variable cost per unit.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lang Method | $C_{TM} = f_L \cdot C_{EQ}$ | $f_L$ = 3.10 (solid), 4.74 (fluid), 3.63 (mixed); $C_{EQ}$ in ₹ or $ |
| 2 | Hand Method | $f_{Hand} = 1 + \sum f_i$ | $f_i$ = individual fractional factors |
| 3 | Cost Indexing | $C_{now} = C_{past} \cdot (I_{now}/I_{past})$ | $I$ = CEPCI (dimensionless) |
| 4 | Six-Tenths Rule | $C_{new} = C_{old} \cdot (Q_{new}/Q_{old})^{0.6}$ | $Q$ = capacity; exponent 0.6 (food) |
| 5 | Working Capital | $WC \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times FCI$ | 10 %–20 % of FCI |
| 6 | Contingency | $\text{Cont} \approx 0.08 \text{ to } 0.15 \times \text{DPC}$ | 8 %–15 % of direct plant cost |
| 7 | Total Capital | $TCI = FCI + WC$ | ₹ or $ |
| 8 | Manufacturing Cost | $MC = D + F + O$ | D = direct, F = fixed, O = overhead |
| 9 | Total Product Cost | $TPC = MC + GE$ | GE = general expenses |
| 10 | Payback Period | $SPP = FCI / \text{Net Cash Inflow}$ | Years |
| 11 | ROI | $ROI = (P/FCI) \times 100$ | P = annual profit, % |
| 12 | NPV | $NPV = \sum CF_t/(1+i)^t - C_0$ | $i$ = discount rate |
| 13 | IRR | $\sum CF_t/(1+i^*)^t = C_0$ | Solve iteratively for $i^*$ |
| 14 | Break-Even | $BEP = F/(p-v)$ | Units/year |
| 15 | Depreciation (SLM) | $D_{yr} = (C_{asset} - S_{salvage})/n$ | $n$ = life in years |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Lang Factor Application (Spice Processing Plant)

**Problem Statement:**
A spice processing plant requires major process equipment whose delivered cost is **₹ 5,00,000**. The plant handles *mixed* solid-fluid operations (cleaning, grinding, sieving, blending, oil extraction). Using the **Lang factor method**, calculate:
- (a) Total module cost
- (b) Total fixed capital investment (assuming contingency = 10 % of TMC and FCI = TMC + Contingency)
- (c) Working capital at 15 % of FCI
- (d) Total capital investment

**Given Data:**
- $C_{EQ} = ₹ 5,00,000$
- $f_L = 3.63$ (mixed, since spice plants are solid-dominant with some oil extraction = mixed)
- Contingency = 10 % of TMC
- $WC = 15\,\% \text{ of } FCI$

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Compute Total Module Cost
The Lang factor for a *mixed* processing food plant is $f_L = 3.63$.

$$C_{TM} = f_L \cdot C_{EQ}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$C_{TM} = 3.63 \times 5{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{TM} = ₹ 18{,}15{,}000$$

**Logical interpretation:** The total cost of erecting the equipment including installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, and a portion of engineering is **3.63 times** the equipment's delivered price.

### Step 2 — Add Contingency to Obtain Fixed Capital Investment
Contingency is added because unforeseen items (price escalation, design changes, field conditions) always appear during execution:

$$\text{Contingency} = 0.10 \times C_{TM}$$

$$\text{Contingency} = 0.10 \times 18{,}15{,}000 = ₹ 1{,}81{,}500$$

$$\text{FCI} = C_{TM} + \text{Contingency}$$

$$\text{FCI} = 18{,}15{,}000 + 1{,}81{,}500 = ₹ 19{,}96{,}500$$

### Step 3 — Compute Working Capital
Working capital is the short-term money needed to start the operation and keep it running for the first few months:

$$WC = 0.15 \times \text{FCI}$$

$$WC = 0.15 \times 19{,}96{,}500$$

$$WC = ₹ 2{,}99{,}475$$

### Step 4 — Total Capital Investment
The total capital that the entrepreneur must arrange:

$$TCI = \text{FCI} + WC$$

$$TCI = 19{,}96{,}500 + 2{,}99{,}475 = ₹ 22{,}95{,}975$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Verification:** If $C_{EQ}$ is approximately 22 % of TCI here ($5\,\text{L} / 22.96\,\text{L}$), it falls within the typical food plant band of 15 %–40 % mentioned in Section 1.2. The estimate is internally consistent.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hand Factor Application (Dairy Plant)

**Problem Statement:**
A dairy plant has a major equipment delivered cost of **₹ 10,00,000**. Apply the Hand factor method with the following individual factors:

| Component | Factor |
|---|---|
| Equipment (base) | 1.00 |
| Installation | 0.45 |
| Piping | 0.60 |
| Instrumentation & Control | 0.15 |
| Electrical | 0.10 |
| Buildings (process) | 0.25 |
| Yard Improvement | 0.05 |
| Service Facilities | 0.40 |
| Engineering & Supervision | 0.25 |

Compute (a) Hand factor, (b) Total direct plant cost, (c) Total fixed capital investment with 8 % contingency on direct plant cost, (d) Working capital at 12 % of FCI.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Sum All Hand Factors
$$f_{Hand} = 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.60 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.05 + 0.40 + 0.25$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{Hand} &= 1.00 \\
&+ 0.45 = 1.45 \\
&+ 0.60 = 2.05 \\
&+ 0.15 = 2.20 \\
&+ 0.10 = 2.30 \\
&+ 0.25 = 2.55 \\
&+ 0.05 = 2.60 \\
&+ 0.40 = 3.00 \\
&+ 0.25 = 3.25
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{f_{Hand} = 3.25}$$

### Step 2 — Direct Plant Cost (DPC)
$$DPC = f_{Hand} \times C_{EQ}$$

$$DPC = 3.25 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 32{,}50{,}000$$

### Step 3 — Add Contingency
$$\text{Cont} = 0.08 \times DPC = 0.08 \times 32{,}50{,}000 = ₹ 2{,}60{,}000$$

$$FCI = DPC + \text{Cont} = 32{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}60{,}000 = ₹ 35{,}10{,}000$$

### Step 4 — Working Capital
$$WC = 0.12 \times FCI = 0.12 \times 35{,}10{,}000 = ₹ 4{,}21{,}200$$

### Step 5 — Total Capital Investment
$$TCI = FCI + WC = 35{,}10{,}000 + 4{,}21{,}200 = ₹ 39{,}31{,}200$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The Hand factor (3.25) here is *lower* than the Lang factor for mixed (3.63), because the Hand method does not implicitly include contractor's profit and a few miscellaneous items. KTU students should always state which method they use and *why*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Break-Even & Profitability for a Fruit Juice Plant

**Problem Statement:**
A fruit juice plant produces a premium blended juice packed in 1 L tetrapaks. The following annual data are estimated:

- Fixed cost per year $F = ₹ 50{,}00{,}000$
- Variable cost per unit $v = ₹ 30$ per L
- Selling price per unit $p = ₹ 60$ per L
- Total Capital Investment $TCI = ₹ 2{,}50{,}00{,}000$
- Expected annual net cash inflow (after-tax) = ₹ 60,00,000
- Plant life = 10 years, no salvage value

Compute: (a) Break-even point in units/year, (b) Simple Payback Period, (c) Return on Investment, (d) Cumulative cash position in year 5.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Break-Even Point
At BEP, total revenue = total cost:
$$R = p \cdot Q = F + v \cdot Q$$

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F}{p - v} = \frac{5{,}00{,}00{,}000}{60 - 30} = \frac{5{,}00{,}00{,}000}{30}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = 16{,}66{,}667 \text{ L/year} \approx 1.67 \times 10^6 \text{ L/year}$$

### Step 2 — Payback Period
$$SPP = \frac{TCI}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}} = \frac{2{,}50{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000}$$

$$SPP = 4.167 \text{ years} \approx 4.17 \text{ years}$$

### Step 3 — Return on Investment
$$ROI = \frac{\text{Annual Net Profit}}{FCI} \times 100$$

Assuming FCI ≈ TCI − WC, and using the simplified formula with the given net cash inflow as a proxy for profit:

$$ROI = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 24\,\%$$

### Step 4 — Cumulative Cash in Year 5
Initial outlay at $t = 0$: $-2,50,00,000$

For years 1 to 5, cash inflow = +60,00,000 per year.

$$\text{Cumulative}_{t=5} = -2{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 60{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Cumulative}_{t=5} = -2{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}00{,}000 = +50{,}00{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** By the end of year 5, the plant has *recovered* its full investment and generated an additional ₹ 50 lakh surplus.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — NPV Computation (Bakery Plant)

**Problem Statement:**
A small-scale bakery requires an initial investment of ₹ 80 lakh. The projected net cash inflows are:

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash Flow (₹ lakh) | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 30 |

The discount rate (cost of capital) is $i = 10\,\%$. Compute NPV and decide whether the project is acceptable.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Discount Each Year's Cash Flow
Discount factor $DF_t = 1/(1+i)^t$.

$$DF_1 = 1/(1.10)^1 = 0.9091$$
$$DF_2 = 1/(1.10)^2 = 0.8264$$
$$DF_3 = 1/(1.10)^3 = 0.7513$$
$$DF_4 = 1/(1.10)^4 = 0.6830$$
$$DF_5 = 1/(1.10)^5 = 0.6209$$

### Step 2 — Present Value of Each Cash Flow
$$PV_1 = 15 \times 0.9091 = 13.64$$
$$PV_2 = 20 \times 0.8264 = 16.53$$
$$PV_3 = 25 \times 0.7513 = 18.78$$
$$PV_4 = 30 \times 0.6830 = 20.49$$
$$PV_5 = 30 \times 0.6209 = 18.63$$

### Step 3 — Sum of Present Values
$$\sum PV = 13.64 + 16.53 + 18.78 + 20.49 + 18.63 = 88.07 \text{ lakh}$$

### Step 4 — Net Present Value
$$NPV = \sum PV - C_0 = 88.07 - 80 = 8.07 \text{ lakh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** If $NPV > 0$, the project is *acceptable* (it earns more than the cost of capital). Here NPV = +₹ 8.07 lakh > 0 ⇒ the bakery project is **financially viable** at 10 % cost of capital.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Initial feasibility study, no P&ID yet | Order-of-magnitude / Lang | Quick; uses only equipment cost |
| Budget approval with completed PFD | Study estimate (Hand / Module) | Better accuracy; uses individual factors |
| Construction bidding | Detailed / vendor-quotation | Bottom-up actual quotations |
| Scaling a known plant to new capacity | Six-Tenths Rule | Accounts for economy of scale |
| Comparing two plant capacities | Total Capital + NPV | Time-value-of-money comparison |
| Single product, single plant | Break-Even + Payback | Quickest profitability screen |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Cost Build-Up of a Food Plant

The following diagram shows how the various cost components are added layer-by-layer to arrive at the **Total Capital Investment (TCI)** and **Total Product Cost (TPC)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Delivered Equipment Cost CEQ] --> B[Total Module Cost C_TM = f_L x CEQ]
    B --> C[Direct Plant Cost DPC]
    B --> D[Service Facilities Cost OSBL]
    C --> E[Engineering and Supervision]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Contingency 8 to 15 percent]
    F --> G[Fixed Capital Investment FCI]
    G --> H[Working Capital WC 10 to 20 percent of FCI]
    H --> I[Total Capital Investment TCI]

    J[Raw Materials Cost] --> K[Direct Production Cost D]
    L[Operating Labour] --> K
    M[Utilities Steam Power Water] --> K
    N[Maintenance] --> K
    K --> O[Manufacturing Cost MC]
    P[Depreciation Taxes Insurance Interest] --> O
    Q[Plant Overhead R D QC Admin] --> O
    O --> R[General Expenses GE]
    R --> S[Total Product Cost TPC = MC + GE]
    T[Selling Price Revenue] --> U[Profit Before Tax = Revenue minus TPC]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cash Flow Over Plant Life

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Year 0: Outlay C_0] -->|negative| A1[Year 1: CF_1 positive]
    A1 --> A2[Year 2: CF_2 positive]
    A2 --> A3[Year 3: CF_3 positive]
    A3 --> A4[Year 4: CF_4 positive]
    A4 --> A5[Year 5: CF_5 positive]
    A5 --> AD[Year n: CF_n plus Salvage]
    AD --> END[End of Project Life]

    P0 -.->|Discounting| NPV[NPV Calculation]
    A1 -.-> NPV
    A2 -.-> NPV
    A3 -.-> NPV
    A4 -.-> NPV
    A5 -.-> NPV
    AD -.-> NPV
    NPV --> DEC{Accept if NPV > 0}
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Profitability Indicator Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Plant Capital and Cash Flow Data] --> A[Compute Annual Net Cash Inflow]
    A --> B[Compute Payback Period SPP]
    B --> C{SPP less than acceptable limit}
    C -- Yes --> D[Pass Payback Screen]
    C -- No --> E[Reject Project]
    D --> F[Compute ROI]
    F --> G{ROI greater than MARR}
    G -- Yes --> H[Pass ROI Screen]
    G -- No --> I[Reject Project]
    H --> J[Compute NPV at Cost of Capital i]
    J --> K{NPV greater than 0}
    K -- Yes --> L[Pass NPV Screen ACCEPT]
    K -- No --> M[Reject Project]
    L --> N[Compute IRR]
    N --> O{IRR greater than i}
    O -- Yes --> P[Project Financially Robust]
    O -- No --> Q[Project Marginal]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lang vs. Hand Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Have delivered equipment cost] --> Q{Do you have itemized factors?}
    Q -- No --> L[Use Lang Method: C_TM = f_L x C_EQ]
    Q -- Yes --> H[Use Hand Method: sum of f_i x C_EQ]
    L --> R1[Add Contingency 8 to 15 percent]
    H --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Obtain FCI]
    R2 --> R3[Add Working Capital 10 to 20 percent of FCI]
    R3 --> R4[Total Capital Investment TCI Ready]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "working capital" as used in food plant cost estimation. List any four components of working capital.**

**Model Answer:**

*Working capital* is the amount of money (above and beyond the fixed capital) that is required to start the plant and keep it operating smoothly during the initial months before revenue stabilizes. It represents the *short-term* operating funds tied up in the production–distribution cycle.

**Four components of working capital:**

1. **Raw material inventory** — value of unprocessed food materials held in store (e.g., paddy in a rice mill, milk in a dairy).
2. **Finished product inventory** — value of packed finished goods awaiting dispatch.
3. **Accounts receivable** — credit sales yet to be collected from distributors/retailers.
4. **Cash reserve** — for day-to-day expenses such as wages, freight, utility bills, and petty purchases.

*(Accounts payable, when subtracted, *reduces* working capital since it is supplier credit.)*

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the Lang factor? State its values for solid, fluid, and mixed food processing plants.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lang factor** ($f_L$) is an empirical dimensionless multiplier, proposed by H. J. Lang (1947), used to convert the *delivered cost of all major process equipment* into an approximate *total module cost* of an erected plant. It implicitly includes installation, piping, instrumentation, electrical, insulation, buildings, and a share of engineering:

$$C_{TM} = f_L \cdot C_{EQ}$$

| Plant Type | Lang factor $f_L$ |
|---|---|
| Solid processing (rice mill, spice plant, grain handling) | 3.10 |
| Fluid processing (dairy, beverage, oil refinery) | 4.74 |
| Mixed (bakery, confectionery, ready-to-eat meals) | 3.63 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker insight:** Students often quote $f_L = 3.10$ for *every* food plant, losing 1 mark. Identify the *type* first — solid / fluid / mixed — then quote the correct factor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Lang factor method** and the **Hand factor method** of cost estimation for a food plant. Compare the two methods, indicating the situations in which each is most useful.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A food extrusion plant has a delivered equipment cost of ₹ 8,00,000. Using the *Hand method* with the following factors, calculate the total fixed capital investment and total capital investment:

| Component | Factor |
|---|---|
| Equipment (base) | 1.00 |
| Installation | 0.40 |
| Piping | 0.55 |
| Instrumentation & Control | 0.15 |
| Electrical | 0.10 |
| Buildings | 0.20 |
| Yard Improvement | 0.05 |
| Service Facilities | 0.35 |
| Engineering | 0.20 |

Assume contingency = 10 % of DPC and working capital = 12 % of FCI.

### Model Answer — Question B1

**Part (a) — Theory [7 Marks]**

**Lang Factor Method:**
This is a *single-multiplier* approach where the total plant cost is obtained by multiplying the delivered equipment cost by a factor $f_L$ that depends on the *physical state* of the material handled:

$$C_{TM} = f_L \cdot C_{EQ}$$

The factor implicitly includes installation, piping, insulation, instrumentation, electrical, buildings, and engineering. It is *fast* and ideal for *early-stage* feasibility studies, when detailed itemized factors are not yet available.

**Hand Factor Method:**
This is a *granular* approach that adds individual percentage factors to the base equipment cost (1.00) for each cost component:

$$f_{Hand} = 1.00 + f_{inst} + f_{piping} + f_{i\&c} + f_{elec} + f_{bldg} + f_{yard} + f_{service} + f_{eng}$$

Each $f_i$ is a fractional value chosen by the estimator from standard tables based on plant complexity. The total plant cost is then:

$$C_{plant} = f_{Hand} \cdot C_{EQ}$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Lang | Hand |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Lower (± 25–35 %) | Higher (± 15–25 %) |
| Effort | Minimal — one factor | Requires itemized judgment |
| Data needed | Equipment cost only | Equipment cost + design judgment |
| Stage of project | Feasibility / conceptual | Schematic / preliminary design |
| Customisation | None (fixed $f_L$) | High (each $f_i$ chosen per plant) |

**Best use:** Lang is best for *quick screening* of many alternatives; Hand is best when the *PFD is complete* and a budget is being prepared for management approval.

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Sum the Hand factors:**
$$f_{Hand} = 1.00 + 0.40 + 0.55 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.05 + 0.35 + 0.20$$

$$f_{Hand} = 3.00$$

**Step 2 — Direct Plant Cost:**
$$DPC = f_{Hand} \times C_{EQ} = 3.00 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 24{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Contingency (10 % of DPC):**
$$\text{Cont} = 0.10 \times 24{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 2{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Fixed Capital Investment:**
$$FCI = DPC + \text{Cont} = 24{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹ 26{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Working Capital (12 % of FCI):**
$$WC = 0.12 \times 26{,}40{,}000 = ₹ 3{,}16{,}800$$

**Step 6 — Total Capital Investment:**
$$TCI = FCI + WC = 26{,}40{,}000 + 3{,}16{,}800 = ₹ 29{,}56{,}800$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Box:** TCI = ₹ 29,56,800; FCI = ₹ 26,40,000; WC = ₹ 3,16,800

---

### Question B1 — Question B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with one example each from a food plant context: (i) Fixed Capital Investment, (ii) Manufacturing Cost, (iii) Break-Even Point.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy plant has the following projected data:
- Annual fixed cost $F = ₹ 40,00,000$
- Variable cost per litre $v = ₹ 25$
- Selling price per litre $p = ₹ 50$
- Total Capital Investment = ₹ 3,00,00,000
- Expected annual net cash inflow (after tax) = ₹ 75,00,000

Calculate: (i) Break-Even Point in L/year, (ii) Payback Period, (iii) Return on Investment, (iv) Comment on the project's attractiveness.

### Model Answer — Question B1-B

**Part (a) — Definitions [7 Marks]**

*(i) Fixed Capital Investment (FCI):* The total one-time capital required to design, construct, install, and commission the *fixed* (non-moving) assets of the plant — equipment, buildings, piping, instrumentation, electrical, service facilities, land, and engineering. *Example:* For a ₹ 5 crore dairy plant, the FCI is approximately ₹ 4.2 crore (i.e., excluding the ~ ₹ 80 lakh working capital).

*(ii) Manufacturing Cost (MC):* The total cost of *producing* the food product, comprising direct production costs (raw materials, operating labour, utilities, maintenance, royalties), fixed charges (depreciation, taxes, insurance), and plant overhead (R&D, quality control, packaging). *Example:* For a spice grinding plant producing 5 tonnes/day, MC = ₹ 18,000/tonne comprising ₹ 12,000 raw material, ₹ 3,000 utilities/labour, ₹ 3,000 overhead.

*(iii) Break-Even Point (BEP):* The annual production volume (in units or ₹ revenue) at which *total revenue exactly equals total cost* — the plant neither makes a profit nor a loss. *Example:* A juice plant with fixed cost ₹ 50 lakh/year, selling price ₹ 60/L, variable cost ₹ 30/L has BEP = 50,00,000 / (60 − 30) = 1,66,667 L/year.

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Step (i) Break-Even Point:**
$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{F}{p - v} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{50 - 25} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{25}$$

$$Q_{BEP} = 1{,}60{,}000 \text{ L/year}$$

**Step (ii) Payback Period:**
$$SPP = \frac{TCI}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000} = 4.0 \text{ years}$$

**Step (iii) Return on Investment:**
$$ROI = \frac{75{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 25\,\%$$

**Step (iv) Project Attractiveness:**

- **Payback 4 years** — *excellent* for a food plant (typical food-industry acceptance ≤ 5 years).
- **ROI 25 %** — *very good* (typical food-industry benchmark is 15 %–20 %).
- The plant is **financially attractive** and recommended for investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Box:** BEP = 1,60,000 L/year; SPP = 4 years; ROI = 25 %; Project is financially attractive.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks in Cost Estimation problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Working Capital:** Students compute FCI correctly but stop there. The question almost always asks for *TCI* — adding WC is mandatory (+ 1 mark).
> 
> 2. **Wrong Lang factor:** Quoting $f_L = 3.10$ for a *dairy* (fluid) plant instead of 4.74. Always classify the plant first.
> 
> 3. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing ₹ and ₹ lakh, or using L/year and litres/day without conversion. State units on every line.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the FCI–DPC distinction:** In the Hand method, $f_{Hand} \cdot C_{EQ}$ gives DPC, not FCI. Contingency must be added *separately*.
> 
> 5. **NPV at year 0:** $C_0$ is *not* discounted — it is already at present value. Students often divide it by $(1+i)^0 = 1$ (correct) but some mistakenly discount it again. Marks lost: 1.
> 
> 6. **Payback vs. ROI confusion:** Payback uses *cash inflow*; ROI uses *profit*. These are not the same number.
> 
> 7. **No "year" labels in payback:** Always state "4.0 years", not "4". Marks lost: 0.5 for unit omission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cost estimation pyramid:** Equipment → DPC → FCI → TCI (each layer adds a defined component).
- **Lang factor** = 3.10 (solid), 4.74 (fluid), 3.63 (mixed); single multiplier, fast but coarse.
- **Hand factor** = sum of individual fractional factors; more accurate; needs design judgment.
- **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_{new} = C_{old} \cdot (Q_{new}/Q_{old})^{0.6}$ — economy of scale, $n \approx 0.6$ for food.
- **Cost index:** $C_{now} = C_{past} \cdot (I_{now}/I_{past})$ to adjust historical quotations.
- **Working capital** = 10 %–20 % of FCI; covers RM inventory, FG inventory, cash, receivables minus payables.
- **Contingency** = 8 %–15 % of DPC (or TMC) to cover unforeseen items.
- **Payback Period (SPP)** = FCI / Annual Net Cash Inflow (years). Acceptable limit for food plants: typically 4–6 years.
- **ROI** = (Annual Profit / FCI) × 100; benchmark 15 %–25 % for food processing.
- **NPV** = $\sum CF_t/(1+i)^t - C_0$; accept if NPV > 0.
- **IRR** = discount rate at which NPV = 0; accept if IRR > cost of capital.
- **Break-Even Point** = $F/(p - v)$ units/year; covers fixed cost with the *contribution margin* (p − v) per unit.
- **Depreciation (SLM)** = (Asset cost − Salvage value) / Useful life in years.
- **KTU 2024 numerical recipe** (memorize for ESE): (1) sum the cost components to get DPC; (2) add contingency → FCI; (3) add working capital → TCI; (4) compute payback, ROI, NPV, IRR as required.
- **Always classify the plant** (solid / fluid / mixed) before choosing $f_L$.
- **Always state units** on every intermediate step — examiners reward clarity.
- **Time value of money** must be applied for multi-year cash flow questions (NPV/IRR) — simple payback alone is *insufficient* for KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scale-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Scale-up # Scale-up – Module 4 Notes ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scale-up** is the engineering procedure of transferring a process from the **laboratory** (or bench) scale → **pilot plant** scale → **industrial/commercial production** scale, while preserving the desired performance characteristics such as product quality, yield, heat/mass transfer rates, and economic viability. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT861)**, scale-up is studied under **Food Process Engineering** as a tool that bridges the gap between **R&D findings** and **full-scale plant operation**. The discipline relies heavily on: - **Dimensional Analysis** (Buckingham π Theorem) - **Regime of Operation Theory** (Identical dimensionless groups) - **Pilot Plant Engineering** > [!NOTE] > **Core Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > *Scale-up criteria, regime of operation analysis, similarity concepts, scale-up of equipment used in food industry (mixers, heat exchangers, dryers, evaporators, fermenters), pilot plant design, dimensional analysis.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview – Plain English Analogy Think of **scale-up** like **cooking a family recipe for a wedding banquet of 500 guests**. - **Lab Scale** = cooking for **4 people** on a domestic stove using a small spoon. - **Pilot Scale** = cooking for **50 people** in a school kitchen. - **Commercial Scale** = cooking for **500 people** in an industrial canteen. If you simply **multiply every ingredient by 125×**, the dish *might* taste similar — but in practice, the big vessel heats **unevenly**, the mixing spoon is too small for the pot, the gravy burns at the bottom, and the spices don't distribute equally. This is because **not everything scales linearly** — heat transfer area, mixing intensity, evaporation rate, and residence time all change with size. Scale-up is the science of figuring out *what to keep constant* (temperature, mixing intensity, heat flux) and *what to allow to change* (volume, dimensions) so the **large-scale product matches the lab-scale product** in quality. | Scale | Volume (L) | Heat Transfer Area (m²) | Area / Volume Ratio (m⁻¹) | |---|---|---|---| | Beaker (lab) | 0.5 | 0.025 | 0.050 | | Pilot kettle | 50 | 0.85 | 0.017 | | Plant kettle | 5000 | 28 | 0.0056 | > [!TIP] > Notice the **Area/Volume ratio decreases by ~10×** when scaling from lab to plant. This single fact is the root cause of most scale-up nightmares in food processing — **heat cannot be delivered fast enough** to the bulk. ## 1.3 Why Scale-up is Indispensable in Food Plant Layout 1. **Capital Investment Protection** – A poorly scaled-up plant wastes crores of rupees. 2. **Food Safety** – Pathogen destruction kinetics (D-value, z-value) depend on residence time and temperature distribution. 3. **Product Consistency** – Brand identity demands every batch tastes identical. 4. **Regulatory Compliance** – FSSAI / Codex requires process validation at production scale. 5. **Energy Efficiency** – Specific energy consumption (kWh/kg) must remain competitive. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Scale-up Theory ### Pillar 1 – **Geometric Similarity** All linear dimensions are scaled by the same factor, $R$, and all angles are preserved. $$\frac{D_{\text{plant}}}{D_{\text{pilot}}} = \frac{H_{\text{plant}}}{H_{\text{pilot}}} = \frac{d_{\text{plant,impeller}}}{d_{\text{pilot,impeller}}} = R$$ > *Why?* Geometric similarity is the **foundation** — without it, dynamic and thermal similarities become ill-defined. ### Pillar 2 – **Dynamic (Mechanical) Similarity** The **ratio of forces** acting on corresponding fluid elements is identical at both scales. $$\left[\frac{F_{\text{inertia}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}}\right]_{\text{plant}} = \left[\frac{F_{\text{inertia}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}}\right]_{\text{pilot}}$$ ### Pillar 3 – **Thermal / Chemical (Process) Similarity** Heat and mass transfer rates, as well as reaction kinetics, must remain proportional. $$\left[\frac{\text{Heat transferred}}{\text{Heat generated}}}\right]_{\text{plant}} = \left[\frac{\text{Heat transferred}}{\text{Heat generated}}}\right]_{\text{pilot}}$$ ## 2.2 Dimensionless Groups – The Heart of Scale-up | # | Group | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Food Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Reynolds (Re)** | $\displaystyle \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Inertia ÷ Viscous | Mixing, pumping, flow in pipes | | 2 | **Froude (Fr)** | $\displaystyle \frac{v^{2}}{g D}$ | Inertia ÷ Gravity | Unbaffled mixers, surface waves | | 3 | **Weber (We)** | $\displaystyle \frac{\rho v^{2} L}{\sigma}$ | Inertia ÷ Surface tension | Spray dryers, atomization | | 4 | **Prandtl (Pr)** | $\displaystyle \frac{C_{p}\,\mu}{k}$ | Momentum ÷ Thermal diffusivity | Heat transfer correlations | | 5 | **Schmidt (Sc)** | $\displaystyle \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$ | Momentum ÷ Mass diffusivity | Drying, extraction | | 6 | **Nusselt (Nu)** | $\displaystyle \frac{h D}{k}$ | Convective ÷ Conductive heat transfer | Heat exchangers | | 7 | **Sherwood (Sh)** | $\displaystyle \frac{k_{c} D}{D_{AB}}$ | Convective ÷ Diffusive mass transfer | Drying, leaching | | 8 | **Power (Po)** | $\displaystyle \frac{P}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}}$ | Dimensionless power consumption | Agitator selection | | 9 | **Péclet (Pe)** | $\displaystyle \frac{v D}{D_{AB}}$ | Convection ÷ Diffusion | Continuous reactors | | 10 | **Damköhler (Da)** | $\displaystyle \frac{k\,C^{n-1}\, \tau}{1}$ | Reaction rate ÷ Mass transfer rate | Fermentation, pasteurization | > [!NOTE] > In LaTeX, the absolute value or "divided by" within tables is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax (e.g., write $\rho v^{2} L \mid \sigma$ if needed). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet – Master Cheat Table for Scale-up | Sl. | Scale-up Criterion | Governing Equation | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Constant Power per Volume ($P/V$)** | $N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\dfrac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{2/3}$ | Blending of miscible liquids (most common in food) | | 2 | **Constant Tip Speed ($\pi D N$)** | $N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\dfrac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)$ | Solid suspension, crystallization | | 3 | **Constant Reynolds Number (Re)** | $N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\dfrac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{2}$ | Laminar mixing, viscous food pastes | | 4 | **Constant Froude Number (Fr)** | $N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\dfrac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{1/2}$ | Unbaffled tanks, surface aeration | | 5 | **Constant Shear (γ̇)** | $N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\dfrac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)$ | Shear-sensitive products (yoghurt, cream) | | 6 | **Constant Mixing Time ($t_m$)** | $N_{2}\,D_{2} = N_{1}\,D_{1}$ | Batch uniformity requirement | | 7 | **Constant Heat Transfer Coeff. ($h$)** | $h \propto N^{a}D^{b}$ (from Nu–Re correlation) | Jacketed vessels, scraped surface | | 8 | **Constant Specific Energy** | $E/V = \text{const}$ | Emulsification, homogenization | > **Universal power law form (with geometric similarity):** > $$P = P_{o}\,\rho\,N^{3}\,D^{5} \quad ; \quad P_{o} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Fr})$$ ## 2.4 Regime of Operation – The Decision Algorithm A "regime" is the set of dominant forces. Choosing the right regime is the **single most important step** in scale-up. ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Identify dominant force │ │ (viscous? gravity? │ │ surface tension? inertia)│ └────────────┬─────────────┘ │ ┌─────────────┼─────────────┬──────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ VISCOUS INERTIAL GRAVITY SURFACE regime regime regime regime │ │ │ │ Re<10 Re>10⁴ Unbaffled Droplet tanks formation │ │ │ │ Hold Re Hold Fr Hold We Hold constant constant constant Re & We ``` ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering - **Dairy Industry** – Scaling UHT sterilization from 100 L/h pilot to 10,000 L/h plant while maintaining $F_{0} \geq 3$ min lethality. - **Baking** – Scaling dough mixers; constant **mixing time per kg** is critical to avoid gluten over-development. - **Spray Drying** – Atomizer scaling using **Weber number** to keep droplet size spectrum identical. - **Fermentation** – Scaling bioreactors using **$k_L a$** (volumetric mass transfer coefficient) – the lifeline of aerobic cultures. - **PHTST Pasteurization** – Maintaining **$P^{*}$-value** across plate heat exchanger plates when adding plates to increase capacity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Power Per Volume Criterion (Constant $P/V$) **Starting point:** Power correlation for a baffled agitated tank with geometric similarity. $$P = P_{o}\,\rho\,N^{3}\,D^{5}$$ For a given geometry and turbulent regime, $P_{o}$ = constant. We require: $$\frac{P_{2}}{V_{2}} = \frac{P_{1}}{V_{1}}$$ Since the vessel volume is proportional to the cube of impeller diameter under geometric similarity: $$V \propto D^{3}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} = \left(\frac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{3}$$ Substituting $P \propto \rho N^{3} D^{5}$: $$\frac{\rho_{2} N_{2}^{3} D_{2}^{5}}{\rho_{1} N_{1}^{3} D_{1}^{5}} = \left(\frac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{3}$$ For the same fluid ($\rho_1 = \rho_2$): $$N_{2}^{3} = N_{1}^{3} \left(\frac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{3} \cdot \left(\frac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{-2}$$ $$N_{2}^{3} = N_{1}^{3} \left(\frac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{1}$$ Wait — let us redo this carefully: $$N_{2}^{3} D_{2}^{5} = N_{1}^{3} D_{1}^{5} \cdot \left(\frac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{3}$$ $$N_{2}^{3} = N_{1}^{3} \cdot \frac{D_{1}^{5}}{D_{2}^{5}} \cdot \frac{D_{2}^{3}}{D_{1}^{3}} = N_{1}^{3} \cdot \left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{2}$$ Taking cube root: $$\boxed{\,N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{2/3}\,}$$ > **Interpretation:** As tank diameter increases, impeller speed must decrease to maintain the same $P/V$ — exactly what a chef experiences: a giant ladle in a huge pot must move slowly to avoid splashing. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Reynolds Number Criterion (Constant Re) We require: $$\text{Re}_{2} = \text{Re}_{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_{2} D_{2}^{2}}{\nu_{2}} = \frac{N_{1} D_{1}^{2}}{\nu_{1}}$$ For the same fluid ($\nu_1 = \nu_2$): $$N_{2} D_{2}^{2} = N_{1} D_{1}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\,N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{2}\,}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Froude Number Criterion (Constant Fr) We require: $$\text{Fr}_{2} = \text{Fr}_{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_{2}^{2} D_{2}}{g} = \frac{N_{1}^{2} D_{1}}{g}$$ $$N_{2}^{2} D_{2} = N_{1}^{2} D_{1}$$ $$\boxed{\,N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{1/2}\,}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 – Heat Exchanger Plate Addition (Constant $P^*$) The pasteurization effect $P^{*}$ is given by: $$P^{*} = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t)-T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,dt$$ When adding plates (geometrically similar), the **flow length per plate $L_p$** remains constant but total length scales with the number of plates $n$. The total residence time is: $$t = \frac{n \cdot L_{p}}{v}$$ For geometric similarity and the same fluid, the heat transfer coefficient follows: $$\text{Nu} = a\,\text{Re}^{b}\,\text{Pr}^{c} \quad\Rightarrow\quad h \propto v^{b}$$ Since $v$ is constant per channel, $h$ is constant. But the **log-mean temperature difference** (LMTD) changes because $\Delta T$ at outlet drops as more heat is transferred. The criterion for constant $P^{*}$ is therefore **constant $\beta$ (holding time)** rather than constant $v$: $$\beta = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{\dot{V}} = \text{const}$$ This is why commercial PHTST units simply **add identical plates in parallel** to the original plate pack — minimal scale-up risk. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example – UHT Sterilizer **Given:** Pilot PHTST unit has $D_1 = 0.05$ m (flow channel), $N_1 = 1.5$ m/s, $h_1 = 2200$ W/m²·K, holding tube length $L_1 = 12$ m, total capacity 2000 L/h, milk $C_p = 3.9$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho = 1030$ kg/m³. **Plant unit** must handle **20,000 L/h** — find the new impeller / pump speed and confirm holding tube length. **Solution:** Step 1 — Capacity ratio: $$\frac{\dot{V}_{2}}{\dot{V}_{1}} = \frac{20{,}000}{2{,}000} = 10$$ Step 2 — With $P/V$ criterion, since $V \propto D^{3}$, the linear scale factor is: $$R = \left(\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{1/3} = 10^{1/3} = 2.154$$ Step 3 — New impeller (or pump) speed using $N_2 = N_1 (D_1/D_2)^{2/3}$: $$N_{2} = 1.5 \times \left(\frac{1}{2.154}\right)^{2/3} = 1.5 \times 0.598 = 0.897 \text{ m/s}$$ Step 4 — New holding tube length (residence time constant): $$\beta = \frac{V_{\text{holding}}}{\dot{V}} = \text{const} \quad\Rightarrow\quad L_{2} = L_{1} \cdot R = 12 \times 2.154 = 25.85 \text{ m}$$ Step 5 — Verify heat transfer coefficient (using Dittus–Boelter, $Nu = 0.023\,Re^{0.8}Pr^{0.4}$): $$h_{2}/h_{1} = (N_{2}/N_{1})^{0.8} = (0.897/1.5)^{0.8} = 0.65$$ So $h_2 = 2200 \times 0.65 = 1430$ W/m²·K. The plant operates at a **lower $h$**, but with more area ($A_2 = A_1 \cdot R^2 = 4.64\,A_1$), the total heat duty still scales correctly. > **Conclusion:** Plant operates at lower velocity but larger area; the $F_0$ value must be re-validated by time–temperature integration. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Scale-up Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Lab Data Available"] --> B["Define Process Objective
Quality Yield Safety"] B --> C["Identify Dominant
Physical Phenomenon"] C --> D1{"Mixing
Dominant"} C --> D2{"Heat Transfer
Dominant"} C --> D3{"Mass Transfer
Dominant"} C --> D4{"Reaction
Dominant"} D1 --> E1["Apply Mixing
Scale-up Rule"] D2 --> E2["Apply Heat Transfer
Scale-up Rule"] D3 --> E3["Apply Mass Transfer
Scale-up Rule"] D4 --> E4["Apply Reaction
Scale-up Rule"] E1 --> F["Pilot Plant
Validation"] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G["Measure
Performance"] G --> H{"Meets
Spec"} H -- No --> I["Adjust Criterion
or Geometry"] I --> F H -- Yes --> J["Commercial Plant
Design"] ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchy of Similarity ```mermaid graph TD L0["Process Goal"] --> L1["Geometric Similarity
Shape ratios constant"] L1 --> L2["Kinematic Similarity
Velocity ratios constant"] L2 --> L3["Dynamic Similarity
Force ratios constant
Re Fr We constant"] L3 --> L4["Thermal Similarity
Heat transfer ratios constant
Nu Pr constant"] L4 --> L5["Chemical Similarity
Reaction rates constant
Sc Da constant"] L5 --> L6["Full Process Similarity
All Pi groups identical"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Scale-up Methodology ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"] I1["Lab Data
10 to 100 L"] I2["Bench Recipes
and Procedures"] end subgraph ANALYSIS["Analysis Stage"] A1["Dimensional Analysis
Buckingham Pi"] A2["Regime Identification
Dominant Force"] A3["Pilot Plant Sizing
50 to 5000 L"] end subgraph CRITERION["Criterion Stage"] C1["Constant P per V"] C2["Constant Tip Speed"] C3["Constant Re or Fr"] C4["Constant h or kLa"] end subgraph VALIDATION["Validation Stage"] V1["Pilot Trials
DOE"] V2["Quality Testing
Sensory Microbiology"] V3["Energy Audit
SEC kWh per kg"] end subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"] O1["Commercial Plant
5000 to 100000 L"] O2["Validated SOPs"] O3["Operator Training"] end INPUT --> ANALYSIS --> CRITERION --> VALIDATION --> OUTPUT A3 --> V1 C1 --> V1 C2 --> V1 C3 --> V1 C4 --> V1 ``` ## 4.4 Mixer Scale-up Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph LAB["Lab Scale"] L1["D = 0.15 m"] L2["N = 600 rpm"] L3["V = 5 L"] L4["P per V = 1000 W per m3"] end subgraph PILOT["Pilot Scale"] P1["D = 0.50 m"] P2["N = 257 rpm"] P3["V = 150 L"] P4["P per V = 1000 W per m3"] end subgraph PLANT["Plant Scale"] M1["D = 1.50 m"] M2["N = 147 rpm"] M3["V = 4000 L"] M4["P per V = 1000 W per m3"] end LAB -->|"R = 3.33"| PILOT PILOT -->|"R = 3.0"| PLANT note["Constant P per V criterion
applied at each step"] PLANT -.- note ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** · [3 Marks] **Q1.** Define **scale-up**. List any **two** scale-up criteria used in food processing. **Model Answer:** Scale-up is the engineering procedure of transferring a process from laboratory or pilot scale to commercial production scale while preserving product quality and process performance. Two scale-up criteria commonly used in food processing are: (i) **constant power per unit volume ($P/V$)** for blending operations, and (ii) **constant tip speed** ($\pi D N$) for solid suspension. *Approx. word count: 50–60 words.* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** · [3 Marks] **Q2.** State the **Buckingham π Theorem** and mention its significance in scale-up. **Model Answer:** Buckingham π Theorem states that any physically meaningful equation involving $n$ variables expressed in $k$ fundamental dimensions can be reduced to a relationship among $(n - k)$ **dimensionless groups (π terms)**. Its significance in scale-up is that it identifies the **minimum number of independent dimensionless groups** that must be held constant between scales to guarantee dynamic, thermal, and chemical similarity. *Approx. word count: 50–60 words.* --- ## 5.2 Part B – Full 14-Mark Question (with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Set, Module 4]** ### **Question A (14 Marks) – Constant $P/V$ Scale-up** **(a)** A pilot-scale agitated vessel used for preparing a fruit pulp has the following data: impeller diameter $D_1 = 0.30$ m, rotational speed $N_1 = 180$ rpm, working volume $V_1 = 100$ L, density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, viscosity $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. **[7 Marks]** **Calculate the rotational speed $N_2$ for a commercial vessel of $D_2 = 0.90$ m using the constant $P/V$ criterion. Verify the flow regime.** **Step 1 – Identify scale-up rule.** Constant $P/V$ criterion (geometric similarity is assumed): $$N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{2/3}$$ **Step 2 – Substitute numerical values.** $$N_{2} = 180 \times \left(\frac{0.30}{0.90}\right)^{2/3} = 180 \times (0.333)^{0.667}$$ $$(0.333)^{0.667} = e^{0.667 \times \ln(0.333)} = e^{0.667 \times (-1.099)} = e^{-0.733} = 0.480$$ $$N_{2} = 180 \times 0.480 = 86.4 \text{ rpm} \quad \approx 1.44 \text{ rps}$$ **Step 3 – Compute new Reynolds number.** $$\text{Re}_{2} = \frac{\rho N_{2} D_{2}^{2}}{\mu} = \frac{1100 \times 1.44 \times (0.90)^{2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$= \frac{1100 \times 1.44 \times 0.81}{0.005} = \frac{1283}{0.005} = 2.566 \times 10^{5}$$ **Step 4 – Verify regime.** Since $\text{Re}_{2} = 2.566 \times 10^{5} \gg 10^{4}$, the flow is **fully turbulent**, justifying the use of the constant $P/V$ criterion (which is most reliable in the turbulent regime). ✓ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{N_{2} \approx 86.4 \text{ rpm, fully turbulent flow}}$ --- **(b)** With the same fruit pulp conditions, compute the **power required** by the commercial vessel if the power number $P_o = 5.5$ (turbulent regime, for a PBT impeller at $D/T = 0.33$). **[7 Marks]** **Step 1 – Power formula.** $$P = P_{o}\,\rho\,N^{3}\,D^{5}$$ **Step 2 – Convert units.** $N_2 = 86.4$ rpm $= 86.4/60 = 1.44$ rps, $D_2 = 0.90$ m. **Step 3 – Compute step by step.** $$N_{2}^{3} = (1.44)^{3} = 2.986 \text{ s}^{-3}$$ $$D_{2}^{5} = (0.90)^{5} = 0.5905 \text{ m}^{5}$$ $$\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$ $$P = 5.5 \times 1100 \times 2.986 \times 0.5905$$ $$P = 5.5 \times 1100 \times 1.763 = 10{,}664 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 10.66 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 4 – Sanity check using $P/V$.** Working volume $V_2 = 10 \times V_1 = 1000$ L $= 1.0$ m³ (since linear scale-up by 3× means $V \propto 3^3 = 27$, so $V_2 = 100 \times 27 = 2700$ L $= 2.7$ m³). $$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{10{,}664}{2.7} \approx 3950 \text{ W/m}^{3}$$ Pilot value: $P_1 = 5.5 \times 1100 \times (3)^{3} \times (0.30)^{5} = 5.5 \times 1100 \times 27 \times 0.00243 = 397$ W. $$P_1/V_1 = 397 \text{ W} / 0.1 \text{ m}^{3} = 3970 \text{ W/m}^{3}$$ The two values match ($\approx 3950$ vs $3970$ W/m³) — small difference due to rounding. **Constant $P/V$ criterion verified.** ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – Heat Exchanger Scale-up** **(a)** A PHTST pasteurizer handles **5000 L/h** of milk with 7 plates, total heat transfer area $A_1 = 1.4$ m², $U_1 = 2500$ W/m²·K, $\Delta T_{\text{lm,1}} = 12$ °C. The plant wants to increase capacity to **15,000 L/h** by adding plates. Calculate the **new heat transfer area** $A_2$ and **new $U$** assuming identical plate geometry. **[7 Marks]** **Step 1 – Original heat duty.** $$Q_{1} = U_{1} A_{1} \Delta T_{\text{lm,1}} = 2500 \times 1.4 \times 12 = 42{,}000 \text{ W} = 42 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 – Required heat duty (capacity ratio = 3).** $$Q_{2} = 3 \times Q_{1} = 126 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 – Assumption of constant LMTD.** Since milk enters at the same temperature and is heated to the same outlet temperature, $\Delta T_{\text{lm,2}} \approx \Delta T_{\text{lm,1}} = 12$ °C (small change due to extra plates is neglected in first approximation). **Step 4 – Required area.** $$A_{2} = \frac{Q_{2}}{U_{1} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm,2}}} = \frac{126{,}000}{2500 \times 12} = 4.2 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 5 – Verify $U$.** Since flow per channel remains the same, $v$ = constant, Re = constant, $h$ = constant → $U_2 = U_1 = 2500$ W/m²·K. **Final Answer:** $\boxed{A_{2} = 4.2 \text{ m}^{2}, \quad U_{2} = U_{1} = 2500 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}}$ --- **(b)** A spray dryer is being scaled up from a pilot unit producing **50 kg/h** of milk powder to a **commercial unit** producing **2000 kg/h**. The atomizer wheel diameter increases from $D_1 = 0.05$ m to $D_2 = 0.20$ m. Using **constant Weber number**, find the **new wheel speed** $N_2$, given pilot speed $N_1 = 25{,}000$ rpm. Also comment on **droplet size** expectation. **[7 Marks]** **Step 1 – Constant Weber criterion.** $$\text{We} = \frac{\rho v^{2} D}{\sigma} = \text{const}$$ For the atomizer wheel, the linear velocity at the periphery is $v = \pi D N$. Substituting: $$\text{We} = \frac{\rho (\pi D N)^{2} D}{\sigma} = \frac{\rho \pi^{2} D^{3} N^{2}}{\sigma}$$ **Step 2 – Set We equal at both scales.** $$\rho_{1} D_{1}^{3} N_{1}^{2} = \rho_{2} D_{2}^{3} N_{2}^{2}$$ For the same fluid ($\rho_1 = \rho_2$): $$N_{2}^{2} = N_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{3}$$ $$N_{2} = N_{1}\left(\frac{D_{1}}{D_{2}}\right)^{3/2}$$ **Step 3 – Compute.** $$N_{2} = 25{,}000 \times \left(\frac{0.05}{0.20}\right)^{1.5} = 25{,}000 \times (0.25)^{1.5}$$ $$(0.25)^{1.5} = 0.25 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125$$ $$N_{2} = 25{,}000 \times 0.125 = 3125 \text{ rpm}$$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{N_{2} \approx 3125 \text{ rpm}}$ **Comment on droplet size:** Since Weber number is constant, the **droplet formation mechanism** (sheet → ligament → droplet) is preserved, so the **mean droplet diameter** remains similar (assuming all other physical properties unchanged). This is the **key advantage** of Weber-based scale-up for atomizers — it preserves the spray quality. Without this criterion, a 4× larger wheel spinning at 25,000 rpm would deliver 16× higher peripheral velocity, producing much finer droplets and a completely different powder granulometry. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Unit conversion** – rpm to rps is the most common arithmetic mistake; examiners actively hunt for this. > 2. **Mixing up criteria** – Students often apply *constant tip speed* when the problem demands *constant $P/V$*. Read carefully! > 3. **Forgetting the exponent** – $2/3$ for $P/V$, $1$ for tip speed, $2$ for Re, $1/2$ for Fr. A wrong exponent means full marks lost on the sub-part. > 4. **No regime verification** – Always state whether Re > 10⁴ (turbulent), otherwise justify the criterion choice. > 5. **Skipping dimensional consistency** – SI units throughout; no mixing cm with m. > 6. **Wrong area-to-volume reasoning** – Surface area scales as $D^{2}$, volume as $D^{3}$; ratio scales as $1/D$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap – Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - ⭐ **Scale-up** = Lab → Pilot → Plant, preserving quality and performance. - ⭐ The **three pillars**: Geometric → Kinematic → Dynamic → Thermal → Chemical similarity. - ⭐ **Buckingham π Theorem** is the theoretical backbone — minimize dimensionless groups. - ⭐ Choose the **dominant force regime first**, then pick the appropriate scale-up rule. - ⭐ **Mixer scale-up rules (with exponents on $D_1/D_2$):** - $P/V$ constant → $2/3$ - Tip speed constant → $1$ - Re constant → $2$ - Fr constant → $1/2$ - Mixing time constant → $1$ - ⭐ **Power Law:** $P = P_o \rho N^{3} D^{5}$ — master this. - ⭐ **Heat exchanger scale-up** → usually add plates, $U$ stays nearly constant. - ⭐ **Spray dryer scale-up** → constant We preserves droplet size. - ⭐ **Fermenter scale-up** → constant $k_L a$ is the king parameter. - ⭐ **A/V ratio drops with size** — the fundamental reason why lab-to-plant surprises happen. - ⭐ **Pilot plant** is non-negotiable — it validates all math, especially for **shear-sensitive**, **heat-sensitive**, and **fermentation** products. - ⭐ **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption, kWh/kg)** is the KTU-asked economic yardstick. - ⭐ Always **re-validate $F_0$, $P^{*}$, texture, color, and microbial counts** at plant scale.

topics.title: Food processing enterprise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT861 - FOOD PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food processing enterprise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Food Processing Enterprise** is a legally registered business entity engaged in the transformation of raw agricultural, horticultural, animal, or marine produce into finished, semi-finished, or intermediate food products through one or more unit operations (cleaning, grading, sorting, thermal processing, packaging, etc.) with the objective of commercial sale, value addition, and consumer delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Terminology):**
> A food processing enterprise encompasses the entire organizational, managerial, financial, and technical framework required to plan, establish, operate, and sustain a food manufacturing business — from concept generation to plant commissioning, production, marketing, and distribution.

The enterprise may operate at any of the following scales:
- **Micro Enterprise** — Investment ≤ ₹1 Crore; Turnover ≤ ₹5 Crore.
- **Small Enterprise** — Investment ≤ ₹10 Crore; Turnover ≤ ₹50 Crore.
- **Medium Enterprise** — Investment ≤ ₹50 Crore; Turnover ≤ ₹250 Crore.

These thresholds are aligned with the **MSME Classification (Udyam Registration)** under the Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises, Government of India.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Think of a food processing enterprise like a professional restaurant kitchen. The **chef (entrepreneur)** has a vision for a dish (product). He/she must first scout a location (site selection), arrange equipment (plant & machinery), hire skilled cooks (manpower), source raw materials (procurement), follow hygiene rules (FSSAI compliance), cook the dish (production), plate it attractively (packaging), and finally serve it to customers (marketing & distribution). A *food processing enterprise* is essentially this same chain — but on an industrial scale.

A food processing enterprise is **not just a factory** — it is a **holistic business system** that integrates:
1. **The technical plant** (buildings, machinery, utilities)
2. **The business strategy** (market, finance, regulatory)
3. **The human capital** (managers, technicians, workers)

### 1.3 Core Components of a Food Processing Enterprise

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Land & Building | Physical location and infrastructure |
| Plant & Machinery | Production equipment |
| Manpower | Skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled |
| Raw Materials | Input supply chain |
| Utilities | Water, steam, electricity, refrigeration |
| Quality Control | FSSAI, BIS, ISO, HACCP compliance |
| Marketing & Sales | Distribution and revenue generation |
| Finance | Capital, working capital, ROI |
| R&D | New product development | 

### 1.4 Why Study This in Plant Layout & Design?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> The *enterprise* perspective dictates the **layout** itself. Whether you design a **process-product layout**, a **fixed-position layout**, or a **cellular layout** depends entirely on the *type of enterprise* (batching, continuous, job-shop). Therefore, understanding the enterprise is foundational to layout decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Food Processing Enterprises

Food processing enterprises can be classified along multiple axes:

#### A. Based on Scale of Operation
- **Micro / Cottage / Tiny Units** — Traditional, family-run, low investment.
- **Small-Scale Industries (SSI)** — Mechanized but limited capacity.
- **Medium-Scale Industries** — Semi-automated, regional distribution.
- **Large-Scale Industries** — Fully automated, national/international markets.

#### B. Based on Product Range
- **Single-Product Enterprise** — E.g., a dairy plant processing only milk.
- **Multi-Product Enterprise** — E.g., a fruit processing unit making juices, jams, and pulp.

#### C. Based on Production Process
- **Continuous Process Enterprise** — E.g., dairy pasteurization, beverage bottling.
- **Batch Process Enterprise** — E.g., bakery, confectionery.
- **Job-Shop Enterprise** — E.g., custom spice blending.

#### D. Based on Ownership Structure
- **Sole Proprietorship**
- **Partnership Firm**
- **Private Limited Company**
- **Public Limited Company**
- **Cooperative Society** (e.g., AMUL model)
- **Joint Venture / Foreign Collaboration**

#### E. Based on Technology Level
- **Traditional / Conventional**
- **Semi-Mechanized**
- **Fully Automated / Industry 4.0 Compliant**

### 2.2 Steps in Establishing a Food Processing Enterprise

A systematic, stepwise methodology is followed:

1. **Idea Generation & Concept Development**
   - Identify a market gap or processing opportunity.
2. **Feasibility Study**
   - **Technical Feasibility** — Availability of technology, raw materials.
   - **Financial Feasibility** — Capital costs, ROI estimation.
   - **Market Feasibility** — Demand-supply analysis.
   - **Managerial Feasibility** — Skilled manpower availability.
3. **Business Plan Preparation**
   - Executive summary, operations plan, financial projections.
4. **Project Report & DPR Preparation**
   - Detailed Project Report for bank loans/subsidies.
5. **Registration & Licensing**
   - Udyam Registration, FSSAI License, GST, Trade License, Pollution NOC.
6. **Site Selection & Plant Layout**
   - Proximity to raw material, market, transport.
7. **Plant Design & Equipment Selection**
   - Process flow design, capacity calculation.
8. **Construction & Installation**
   - Civil works, utility setup, machinery erection.
9. **Trial Runs & Commissioning**
   - Pilot batches, calibration, validation.
10. **Commercial Production & Marketing**
    - Branding, packaging, distribution.

### 2.3 Financial Metrics in Food Processing Enterprises

The financial viability of a food enterprise is evaluated using standardized engineering economics formulas. Let $\text{I}$ denote total investment, $\text{R}$ the annual revenue, and $\text{C}$ the annual operating cost.

#### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| Total Investment | $I = F_c + W_c + M_c$ | Sum of fixed, working, and miscellaneous capital |
| Payback Period | $PBP = \dfrac{I}{R - C}$ | Years to recover investment from net annual profit |
| Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{(R - C)}{I} \times 100\%$ | Percentage annual return |
| Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I$ | Present worth of all future cash flows |
| Internal Rate of Return | $NPV = 0$ when $r = IRR$ | Discount rate making NPV zero |
| Break-Even Point (units) | $BEP = \dfrac{F_c}{p - v}$ | Units where revenue equals total cost |
| Break-Even Point (₹) | $BEP_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{F_c}{1 - \dfrac{v}{p}}$ | Sales value at no-profit-no-loss |
| Capacity Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Designed Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Plant efficiency measure |
| Working Capital | $W_c = \text{Raw Materials} + \text{Finished Goods} + \text{Debtors} - \text{Creditors}$ | Short-term operational liquidity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Assumptions:**
> - Linear depreciation is assumed unless stated otherwise.
> - All cash flows occur at year-end.
> - Tax and inflation effects are incorporated using $r_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{1+r_{\text{nom}}}{1+i} - 1$.
> - Break-even analysis assumes single-product constant unit price.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises — they directly determine whether a food processing project receives:
- **Bank Loan Approval** (Banks require a PBP ≤ 7 years and IRR ≥ 15% for most FPI projects).
- **Subsidy Disbursement** (MoFPI's PMKSY scheme offers 35% subsidy for general and 50% for SC/ST/NER applicants).
- **Investor Funding** (VCs evaluate NPV and IRR rigorously).
- **Insurance & Risk Assessment** (PBP and BEP are key inputs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Point (BEP)

The **Break-Even Point** is the production volume at which total revenue equals total cost — i.e., the enterprise makes neither profit nor loss. This is a cornerstone concept in food enterprise economics.

**Step 1: Define Total Cost**
The total cost of producing $Q$ units per year has two components:
- **Fixed Cost ($F_c$):** Cost that does not depend on production volume (e.g., rent, salaries, depreciation).
- **Variable Cost ($V$):** Cost that scales with production (e.g., raw materials, packaging).

$$
\text{Total Cost} = F_c + v \cdot Q
$$

where $v$ is the variable cost per unit.

**Step 2: Define Total Revenue**
If the selling price per unit is $p$:

$$
\text{Total Revenue} = p \cdot Q
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Break-Even Condition**
At break-even, $\text{Revenue} = \text{Total Cost}$:

$$
p \cdot Q_{BE} = F_c + v \cdot Q_{BE}
$$

**Step 4: Solve Algebraically for $Q_{BE}$**
Subtract $v \cdot Q_{BE}$ from both sides:

$$
p \cdot Q_{BE} - v \cdot Q_{BE} = F_c
$$

Factor $Q_{BE}$:

$$
Q_{BE} \cdot (p - v) = F_c
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c}{p - v}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to Revenue (₹) Form**
Multiply both numerator and denominator by $p$:

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot p}{p(p - v)}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $p^2$:

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot p}{p^2 - v \cdot p}
$$

The revenue at break-even is:

$$
R_{BE} = p \cdot Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot p^2}{p^2 - v \cdot p} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot p^2}{p(p - v)}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
R_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot p}{p - v}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $p$:

$$
R_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c}{\dfrac{p - v}{p}} = \dfrac{F_c}{1 - \dfrac{v}{p}}
$$

Hence:

$$
BEP_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{F_c}{1 - \dfrac{v}{p}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The denominator $1 - \dfrac{v}{p}$ is called the **Contribution Margin Ratio (CMR)**. The smaller the variable cost fraction, the higher the CMR, and the lower the BEP — meaning the enterprise becomes profitable faster.

### 3.2 Derivation: Payback Period (PBP)

**Step 1: Define Annual Net Cash Flow**
Let $R$ be the annual revenue and $C$ the annual operating cost (excluding depreciation, which is non-cash):

$$
\text{Annual Net Cash Flow} = R - C
$$

**Step 2: Equate Cumulative Cash Flow to Investment**
The payback period is the year when cumulative cash flow equals the initial investment $I$:

$$
(R - C) \cdot PBP = I
$$

**Step 3: Solve for PBP**

$$
PBP = \dfrac{I}{R - C}
$$

**Step 4: Incorporate Depreciation (More Realistic Model)**
If annual depreciation is $D$ (a non-cash but tax-deductible expense), the *accounting* annual profit is:

$$
\text{Annual Profit} = R - C - D
$$

But cash flow adds back depreciation:

$$
\text{Annual Cash Flow} = R - C
$$

(Since $D$ is already excluded from $C$ as non-cash, the cash flow remains $R - C$).

Thus, the **simple PBP formula** holds:

$$
PBP = \dfrac{I}{R - C}
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Example: A Fruit Juice Enterprise

**Problem Statement:**
A small fruit juice processing enterprise has the following data:
- Fixed Capital Investment ($F_c$) = ₹20,00,000
- Working Capital ($W_c$) = ₹5,00,000
- Selling Price per unit ($p$) = ₹50
- Variable Cost per unit ($v$) = ₹30
- Annual Sales ($Q$) = 1,00,000 units
- Annual Revenue ($R$) = ₹50,00,000

**Find:** (a) Break-Even Point in units and ₹, (b) Payback Period.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Break-Even Point**

Substituting into the formula:

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c}{p - v}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{50 - 30}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{20} = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

Revenue at break-even:

$$
BEP_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{F_c}{1 - \dfrac{v}{p}}
$$

$$
BEP_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{1 - \dfrac{30}{50}}
$$

$$
BEP_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{1 - 0.6}
$$

$$
BEP_{\text{Rs}} = \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.4} = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Part (b): Payback Period**

Total Investment:

$$
I = F_c + W_c = 20{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Annual Operating Cost:

$$
C = v \cdot Q = 30 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Annual Net Cash Flow:

$$
R - C = 50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Payback Period:

$$
PBP = \dfrac{I}{R - C} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
PBP = 1.25 \text{ years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> A PBP of just **1.25 years** is exceptionally attractive for a food processing enterprise — most banks consider projects viable if PBP ≤ 5 years. The break-even of ₹50,00,000 indicates that the enterprise must achieve at least 100% of its current sales to be profitable, suggesting **capacity expansion risk**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Enterprise Type vs. Layout Decision

| Enterprise Type | Product Volume | Process Type | Recommended Layout | Example |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|---------------------|---------|
| Micro / Cottage | Low | Manual / Batch | Fixed-Position | Local pickle maker |
| Small SSI | Moderate | Batch | Product Layout | Bakery unit |
| Medium | High | Mixed | Hybrid (Process-Product) | Fruit juice plant |
| Large | Very High | Continuous | Product Layout | Dairy pasteurization line |
| Job-Shop | Variable | Custom | Process Layout | Custom spice blending |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Establishing a Food Processing Enterprise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Idea Generation] --> B[Step 2: Feasibility Study]
    B --> B1[Technical]
    B --> B2[Financial]
    B --> B3[Market]
    B --> B4[Managerial]
    B1 --> C[Step 3: Business Plan]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Step 4: DPR Preparation]
    D --> E[Step 5: Registration and Licensing]
    E --> E1[Udyam]
    E --> E2[FSSAI]
    E --> E3[GST]
    E --> E4[Pollution NOC]
    E1 --> F[Step 6: Site Selection]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Step 7: Plant Layout Design]
    G --> H[Step 8: Construction and Installation]
    H --> I[Step 9: Trial Runs]
    I --> J[Step 10: Commercial Production]
    J --> K[Marketing and Distribution]
    K --> L{Sustain and Grow?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Scale Up and Diversify]
    L -->|No| N[Reassess Strategy]
    M --> K
    N --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Food Processing Enterprise

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DOMAIN]
        R[Raw Materials]
        U[Utilities]
        L[Labour]
        CAP[Capital]
    end

    subgraph CORE[CORE ENTERPRISE]
        PROC[Processing Unit]
```
